title: T8.2: BindingRecord capnp schema + LLO producer-side dual-write
description:
Bead 8.2 of T8 under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac. Channel: final.

**Why this first.** be6136 (closed at 9fb993f) was a SQL-coupling bug between LLO `_lsp_refs` writes and mache canonical-view JOINs. Fixing it locally was correct, but the underlying contract — "two SQL processes agree on column names" — has no schema-evolution story. Capnp `BindingRecord` is the typed alternative.

**Schema** (`schemas/binding.capnp`):
```
@0xb1ndcap;  # generated capability ID

using Common = import "common.capnp";

struct BindingRecord {
  # Stable identity of the ref site.
  refSite @0 :Common.NodeRef;       # (sourceId, range)
  refToken @1 :Text;                 # textual lemma at ref site
  
  # Resolved targets (LSP gave us these).
  targets @2 :List(Common.NodeRef);  # 0 = unresolved (use mention path)
  
  # Producer-side AST resolution: smallest enclosing construct
  # at the ref site. Replaces _lsp_refs.referrer_node_id.
  referrer @3 :Common.NodeRef;       # NULL-equivalent: empty node_id
  
  # Causality: the parse run that emitted this record.
  parseGen @4 :UInt64;
}

struct NodeRef {
  sourceId @0 :Text;                 # _source.id (relative path)
  nodeId @1 :Text;                   # _ast.node_id
  range @2 :Common.Range;            # for unresolved/lookup-only refs
}
```

**Producer-side dual-write** in `rs/ll-open/lsp/src/project.rs::project_references`:
- Continue writing to `_lsp_refs` (consumers not yet migrated)
- *Also* emit a `BindingRecord` capnp message to a content-addressed event log (initially: append-only file at `${ctrl}.bindings.capnp`; T8.5 promotes to ll/CSS-distributed segment)
- Hash each record (BLAKE3) — that hash IS the record's content address

**Out of scope:**
- Mache consumer migration (T8.4)
- Σ root advance integration (T8.5)
- `_lsp_refs` removal (later — keep dual-write until consumers migrated)

**Acceptance:**
- `cargo test -p leyline-lsp` includes a round-trip test: `project_references` → emit capnp → read back → field-equal asserts
- `_lsp_refs` rows still produced (existing tests still pass)
- New event-log file exists at `${ctrl}.bindings.capnp` after enrich

comments.text:
Empirical input from end-to-end validation of the post-be6136 .db (mache-side, ADR-0013 closeout):

## What works ✅

- Schema columns populate. `_lsp_refs.referrer_node_id` is non-NULL for 9/9 rows in a 9-line gopls-enriched fixture.
- Falsifiability A (skip-list ablation, mache-3509aa) **passes** on a real LLO-built mache self-build: 0 newly-flagged-dead defs when dropping the dead_code skip-list. Means the alive-check binding arm — `r.target_node_id = d.node_id` — is firing correctly. ADR-0013's headline design works empirically.

## What revealed a node-id format question 🟡

Falsifiability B (π_{1→0} round-trip, mache-354464) reports **20/20 (100%) discrepancies** on the same .db. The harness asks: for each `_lsp_refs` row, does `(referrer_node_id, ref_token)` appear in `node_refs`? Answer is no, because the two columns use **different node-id shapes**:

```
_lsp_refs.referrer_node_id (LLO post-be6136):
  pkg/auth.go/function_declaration/parameter_list/parameter_declaration_0/type_identifier
  pkg/auth.go/function_declaration/block/statement_list/return_statement/expression_list/call_expression/selector_expression/field_identifier

node_refs.node_id (LLO leyline parse, call extractor):
  pkg/auth.go/function_declaration/block/statement_list/return_statement/expression_list/call_expression
```

LLO's `lookup_referrer_node_id` returns the **smallest enclosing AST node** at the reference site — leaf identifier (field_identifier, type_identifier). LLO's call extractor for `node_refs` records the **call-expression** node. They're at different AST depths.

Implications:
- For `dead_code` (uses `target_node_id`, NOT `referrer_node_id`): unaffected. Falsifiability A passes.
- For `find_callers` MCP / "where did this come from?" queries: the leaf-identifier path is less useful than a construct-level (function/method) reference would be. Agents asking about a caller want the function name, not the AST node.
- For Falsifiability B's projection: 100% mismatch — the soundness check isn't usable as designed.

## Recommendation for T8.2

The BindingRecord capnp schema should commit to a clear convention. Three options:

1. **Construct-level**: `referrer_node_id` = the smallest enclosing `function_declaration` / `method_declaration` (or equivalent in other languages). Aligns with what `node_refs.node_id` records for call-extraction-shaped rules. Best for `find_callers` UX.
2. **Reference-bearing-node**: `referrer_node_id` = the `call_expression` / `selector_expression` containing the reference. Matches `node_refs.node_id` exactly — Falsifiability B's projection becomes a tight equality check.
3. **Leaf-identifier (current)**: `referrer_node_id` = the leaf `(type_)?identifier` token. What LLO ships today. Most precise but least aligned with `node_refs`.

Whichever wins, the SQL columns and capnp `BindingRecord.referrerNodeId` should agree, AND `node_refs.node_id` should be normalized to the same level (currently records call_expression nodes; if the design picks construct-level, the call extractor needs to walk up too).

If T8.2 picks option 1 or 2, mache's Falsifiability B harness needs no change. If T8.2 stays with option 3, the harness needs to walk up the LLO-emitted AST path to compare at a shallower level, OR the harness becomes "for each ref, does the enclosing call_expression appear in node_refs?" which adds tree-walking on the consumer side.

Test harness in mache:
- `cmd/falsifiability_lsp_projection_test.go::TestFalsifiabilityB_IntegrationOnLeylineParse` (gated on `MACHE_FALSIFIABILITY_INTEGRATION=1`)
- `cmd/falsifiability_skip_list_ablation_test.go::TestFalsifiabilityA_IntegrationOnLeylineParse` (same gate, currently passing)

Filing this here rather than as a separate bead because the resolution naturally lives in T8.2's BindingRecord schema definition.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
Shipped at 49475bc.

**Alignment decision (resolves cdcae2 options post-validation 2026-05-08):** schema emits BOTH levels — `constructNodeId` (smallest enclosing function/method/constructor; aligns with `node_refs.node_id` call-extraction shape and `find_callers` UX) AND `refSiteNodeId` (smallest enclosing AST node = leaf identifier; the precise locator). Schema-as-data-plane lets each consumer pick. No "one column, two semantics" drift possible. Falsifiability B's 100% mismatch becomes a tight equality check on `constructNodeId` once mache migrates (T8.4).

**Schema:** `rs/ll-core/schema-capnp/schemas/binding.capnp` — BindingRecord with target/refToken/construct/refSite/refUri/refRange/parseGen.

**Producer dual-write:** `project_references` gains `binding_log: Option<&Path>`. SQL writes unchanged (local projection); capnp records append to file (cross-process contract). CONSTRUCT_KINDS curated per language — Go/Python/Rust/TS/Java; explicitly excludes inline lambdas.

**Wiring:**
- `cmd_lsp.rs` (`leyline lsp` standalone): always writes `${output}.bindings.capnp`. Mache's existing harness will see it on next .db produced.
- `lsp_pass.rs` (daemon): uses `PRAGMA database_list` to derive sibling path; `:memory:` skips dual-write. Lights up automatically with 5f7100-15a (file-backed live db) or T8.5 (Σ segment hashing).

**Tests:**
- `project_references_dual_writes_capnp_binding_log` — full round-trip + SQL/capnp parity invariant
- `lookup_construct_node_id_per_language` — pins every kind in CONSTRUCT_KINDS

`task ci` green (285+ workspace tests).

**Critical-path status:** be6136-class dissolution = T8.1 ✅ → T8.2 ✅ → T8.4 (mache consumer migration). T8.4 is now unblocked — mache can `capnp::serialize::read_message` from `${db}.bindings.capnp` instead of joining `_lsp_refs` × `_ast`.

title: T8: Capnp schemas as producer/consumer contract for Σ event log
description:
Thread under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac (Σ Merkle-CAS Substrate).

**Motivation.** ADR-0013 Step 1 shipped `_lsp_refs.referrer_node_id` as a SQL column. The producer (LLO `lookup_referrer_node_id` at project.rs:507) and the consumer (mache canonical views) both implement "given (uri, line, col), find the smallest enclosing AST node" — coupled through a SQL schema that has no schema-evolution story. be6136 (closed at 9fb993f) was caused exactly by this: a one-byte path mismatch (/tmp vs /private/tmp) made every JOIN miss. The SQL schema is acting as a cross-process protocol, and SQL is the wrong tool for that.

**The flip** (per L2/L3 synthesis 2026-05-08): capnp messages are the data plane, not the wire format on top of one. SQLite is a *projection* of the capnp event log. LLO emits capnp `BindingRecord`/`AstNode`/`SourceFile` events; LLO's `_ast`/`_lsp_refs` are local SQL projections; mache's canonical views are also local projections; control-room (Swift) gets the same primitives via capnp codegen — single source of truth, schema-evolved, language-agnostic.

**Deliverable arc.**
- T8.1 schema crate scaffold + Rust bindings (foundation)
- T8.2 BindingRecord capnp schema + LLO producer-side dual-write (dissolves the be6136 class of bugs)
- T8.3 AstNode + SourceFile capnp schemas + LLO producer-side dual-write (`_ast` becomes a projection)
- T8.4 TS bindings + mache consumer migration (multi-runtime payoff; mache reads capnp, not SQL)
- T8.5 Σ integration — capnp segments hashed → content-addressed → drive Σ root advance (ties to T2/T3)
- T8.6 ADR-0014 — capnp-as-protocol, schema evolution rules, versioning policy

**Why under Σ decade.** Σ is the substrate; T8 is the *typed wire format* of that substrate. Σ defines content-addressed event logs; T8 defines what's *in* an event. Without T8, the "event log" is implicit (raw SQL writes); with T8, it's typed, evolveable, and consumable by any language with a capnp runtime (Rust, TS, Swift, Go).

**Channels.** T8.1–T8.5 = `final`. T8.6 = `analysis` (ADR).

**Cross-repo touch:** mache (../mache) needs T8.4 to retire its SQL canonical views. control-room (../control-room) gets bindings as a side benefit. cloister (../cloister) inherits the schema for capnp termination at workerd.

comments.text:
**Thread topology** (decomposed 2026-05-08, post-be6136 closure):

```
T8.1 (cd8811) capnp schema crate scaffold + Rust bindings
  ├─→ T8.2 (cdcae2) BindingRecord schema + LLO dual-write   (P1; dissolves be6136 class)
  │     ├─→ T8.4 (ce2281) TS codegen + mache consumer       (P2)
  │     └─→ T8.6 (ce8fd1) ADR-0014 schema-evolution rules   (P2; design)
  └─→ T8.3 (cdf098) AstNode/SourceFile schemas + LLO dual-write  (P1)
        ├─→ T8.4 (ce2281)
        └─→ T8.5 (ce55b1) Σ root advance via segment hash   (P2; depends T2.1=baa90a too)
```

**Parallel-safe cuts:** T8.2 ⊥ T8.3 (independent schemas, can ship concurrently after T8.1). T8.4 needs both. T8.6 (ADR) needs at least T8.2 to write against a real schema.

**Critical path to "be6136 class dissolved":** T8.1 → T8.2 → T8.4 (mache reads capnp).

**Critical path to "Σ event log shipped":** T8.1 → T8.3 → T8.5.

**Cross-repo touch:** T8.4 will need a paired bead in mache once T8.2 lands; control-room (Swift) and cloister (workerd/TS) inherit the schemas as a side benefit but not in scope here.

title: [ADR-0013/Step 1 followup] _lsp_refs.referrer_node_id is always NULL — schema shipped, resolution didn't
description:
Sister/followup to ley-line-453f7e (ADR-0013 Step 1). The schema migration shipped at 12330fc added `referrer_node_id` and `ref_token` columns to `_lsp_refs`, plus `def_token` to `_lsp_defs`. Verified via `.schema _lsp_refs`. But `referrer_node_id` is **NULL on every row** — the column exists, the data flow does not.

## Reproduction

```bash
mkdir -p /tmp/lsp-fixture/pkg
cat > /tmp/lsp-fixture/go.mod <<EOF
module fixture
go 1.21
EOF
cat > /tmp/lsp-fixture/pkg/auth.go <<EOF
package pkg

type Authenticator interface {
	Validate(token string) bool
}
type TokenAuth struct{}
func (t *TokenAuth) Validate(token string) bool { return token != "" }
func UseAuth(a Authenticator, token string) bool { return a.Validate(token) }
EOF

leyline parse /tmp/lsp-fixture -o /tmp/lsp-fixture.db --lang go
leyline lsp --server gopls --input /tmp/lsp-fixture/pkg/auth.go \
            --output /tmp/lsp-out.db --merge-db /tmp/lsp-fixture.db

sqlite3 /tmp/lsp-out.db "
  SELECT COUNT(*) AS total,
         SUM(CASE WHEN referrer_node_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_ref,
         SUM(CASE WHEN referrer_node_id = '' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS empty_ref,
         SUM(CASE WHEN referrer_node_id IS NOT NULL AND referrer_node_id != '' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS populated
  FROM _lsp_refs;"
# 9|9|0|0   ← 100% NULL
```

`ref_token` IS populated (`Validate`, `Authenticator`, `TokenAuth`, etc.). `def_token` IS populated. Only `referrer_node_id` is empty.

## Why it matters (consumer side)

ADR-0013's binding-fidelity design relies on the consumer query

```sql
WHERE r.target_node_id = d.node_id
   OR (r.target_node_id IS NULL AND r.token = d.token)
```

…where `target_node_id` comes from `_lsp_refs.node_id` (the def the ref points at) and the **alive-check edge in `dead_code` is keyed on `(referrer_node_id, target_node_id)`**. Mache's canonical view explicitly filters `WHERE referrer_node_id IS NOT NULL AND ref_token != ''` (cmd/serve_find_smells.go:ensureCanonicalViews) so empty-referrer rows don't pollute mention-fidelity joins.

Result today: on a `leyline lsp`-enriched .db, `v_refs` surfaces **zero binding rows** because every `_lsp_refs` row is filtered out. The dead_code rule's binding arm never fires; the textual mention path runs alone; the skip-list compensation is still load-bearing. ADR-0013's headline experiment (Falsifiability A skip-list ablation, mache-3509aa) cannot deliver its empirical result until referrer_node_id is populated.

## What "populated" should mean

For each `_lsp_refs` row, the producer needs to map the source location `(ref_uri, ref_start_line, ref_start_col)` back to a mache node_id of the **enclosing construct** (the function/method body that contains the reference site). The producer already has the AST tree loaded (it called `descendant_for_byte_range` to extract `ref_token`); the same descendant walk + ancestor traversal up to the nearest `function_declaration` / `method_declaration` / `var`-with-initializer would yield the referrer node_id.

In our reproduction:
- The ref to `Authenticator` at line 12 (in `func UseAuth(a Authenticator, ...)`) should map to `referrer_node_id = 'symbols/UseAuth'` (or whatever mache's node_id format is for that function).
- The ref to `Validate` at line 13 (inside `UseAuth`'s body) should also map to `symbols/UseAuth`.

Without this, the column is decorative.

## Files (likely)

- `rs/ll-open/lsp/src/project.rs` — where `_lsp_refs` rows are written; needs the resolution call before INSERT
- Possibly a helper in the `ts` crate to do the byte-range → enclosing-construct lookup against `nodes` / `_ast` tables

## Acceptance

- `leyline lsp` writes non-NULL `referrer_node_id` for at least the rows where the source file is in `_ast` / `nodes`
- The reproduction above shows `populated > 0`
- mache's `cmd/falsifiability_skip_list_ablation_test.go::TestFalsifiabilityA_IntegrationOnLeylineParse` (currently env-gated) passes when run against an LSP-enriched .db built with the fix

## Cross-repo refs

- mache ADR: `~/remotes/art/mache/docs/adr/0013-refs-defs-canonical-schema.md`
- mache's ensureCanonicalViews filter: `cmd/serve_find_smells.go` (UNION ALL guard)
- Falsifiability harness that needs this: `cmd/falsifiability_skip_list_ablation_test.go`
- Sister bead in mache: ADR-0013 decade is closed; this is operational follow-up

comments.text:
Fixed at 9fb993f. Root cause: `_source.path` stored un-canonicalized at cmd_parse.rs:351 (`abs_path.to_string_lossy()`), but the LSP file:// URI is built from `abs_path.canonicalize()` at lsp_pass.rs:329. On macOS `/tmp` → `/private/tmp`; on Linux any symlinked source root produces the same divergence. Every `lookup_referrer_node_id` SQL query joined `ref_uri` (canonical) against `_source.path` (un-canonical) — every join missed, every row's `referrer_node_id` was NULL.

Fix: canonicalize at write time so stored path matches LSP-echoed URI. Falls back to original on canonicalize failure (broken symlink).

Two regression pins added:
- `project_references_populates_referrer_node_id` — positive byte-range join resolves to smallest enclosing AST node
- `lookup_referrer_node_id_returns_none_on_path_mismatch` — negative `/tmp` vs `/private/tmp` regression pin

Validated: `task ci` green, `task install` rebuilt + codesigned + installed at `~/.local/bin/leyline 0.1.0 (open)`. Mache's existing falsifiability harness (`cmd/falsifiability_skip_list_ablation_test.go`) will now exercise the binding-path correctly against fresh `.db`s.

Closing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

End-to-end validation against the 9fb993f fix:

```

$ leyline lsp --server gopls --input /tmp/lsp-fixture/pkg/auth.go \
              --output /tmp/lsp-out.db --merge-db /tmp/lsp-fixture.db
$ sqlite3 /tmp/lsp-out.db "SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(referrer_node_id IS NULL),
    SUM(referrer_node_id != '' AND referrer_node_id IS NOT NULL) FROM _lsp_refs;"
9|0|9   ← 100% populated (was 9|9|0 pre-fix)
```

The schema column now carries data, and ADR-0013's binding-fidelity arm fires:

- mache canonical view `v_refs` surfaces 9 binding rows (was 0)
- `dead_code` alive-check joins on `target_node_id = d.node_id` correctly
- **Falsifiability A** (skip-list ablation, mache-3509aa) passes on a real LLO-built mache self-build: 0 newly-flagged-dead defs when the skip-list is dropped — empirical confirmation that LSP binding rows replace the heuristic skip-list for cases LSP indexed.

Followup observation about node-id shape (the leaf-identifier choice vs node_refs's call-expression choice) commented on T8.2 (cdcae2) since that's where the BindingRecord capnp schema design will resolve it. Doesn't reopen this bead — the schema-population fix is complete and correct in its own right.

title: 5f7100-15: SQLite WAL adoption — file-backed live_db + connection pool (~600× p99 read win)
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Highest-leverage architectural move per empirical-validation-designer report at `docs/research/2026-05-08-workerd-wal-sqlite-experiment.md`.

## Why now

H1 measured (bench `wal_concurrent_readers.rs`): file-backed WAL @ N=10 readers + 1 writer hits **p99 reads of 290–375 µs** vs DELETE journal at **120–250 ms p99** — ~600× improvement. WAL@N=10 absolute throughput beats DELETE-solo by 3×. Daemon's current shape (`std::sync::Mutex<rusqlite::Connection>` over `:memory:`) cannot benefit because all readers serialize on the Mutex; reads collide with the snapshot writer.

H2 (workerd DO SQLite) and H3 (workerd V8 isolates sharing arena) both **fail** at the source level — workerd serializes everything per-DO via implicit txns and seals `sqlite3_deserialize` behind a `kj::Path`-only `Vfs`. Stay in-process Rust.

## Scope (two sub-beads)

### 5f7100-15a — file-backed live_db + WAL pragma
- `cmd_daemon.rs:410` — replace `Connection::open_in_memory()` with `Connection::open(live_db_path)` (path under arena dir, e.g. `${ctrl}.live.db`)
- Set `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`, `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL`, `PRAGMA wal_autocheckpoint=1000` after open; verify journal_mode return value
- Warm-start path: skip arena→Cursor restore (file-backed is already persistent); Σ snapshot still flips arena
- Cold-start: `unlink` stale `.live.db` if Controller is FRESH
- Snapshot writer (`snapshot_to_arena` at cmd_daemon.rs:540) keeps using `serialize()` — output goes into mmap'd arena per Σ design

### 5f7100-15b — Mutex<Connection> → connection pool
- Introduce `LiveDbPool` (vec of `Connection` behind `parking_lot::Mutex<Vec<Connection>>` or `r2d2_sqlite`)
- Reader handlers (`with_live_db` in `daemon/ops.rs:221`) check out a connection, run query, return. ~30 call sites — most are reads.
- Writer handlers (op_reparse, op_load, op_flush, snapshot timer) keep exclusive lock semantics — single dedicated writer connection
- 30+ `live_db.lock()` / `with_live_db` call sites need conversion (see grep output for inventory)

## Followup risks
- Disk pressure: `:memory:` writes were free; WAL writes hit disk. Bench shows WAL@N=1 = 80K writes/sec which is fine, but cgroup-constrained envs may differ.
- Snapshot interaction: `serialize()` against a WAL'd db requires an exclusive write txn — confirm via small test before committing 15b.
- Test rig: `setup()` in tests/integration.rs creates many ephemeral daemons — ensure `.live.db` is rooted in TempDir, not leaked.

## Acceptance
- Bench `wal_concurrent_readers.rs` already exists; add a daemon-level integration variant that drives `op_get_node` from N=10 tasks while op_reparse runs and asserts p99 < 5ms.
- All existing integration tests pass.
- `task ci` green.

## Files cited (from agent report)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs:200, 410, 535–584, 621–681`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs:81, 96, 630`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/benches/wal_concurrent_readers.rs`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/benches/wal_snapshot_experiment.rs`
- `docs/decades/2026-merkle-cas-substrate.md` §1.5 (arena ↔ Σ mapping)
- `docs/research/2026-05-08-workerd-wal-sqlite-experiment.md` (this report)

comments.text:
Shipped at 140c4e1.

**Wired:** `SqliteGraph::from_arena` and `SqliteGraphAdapter::from_arena_writable` now call `verify_arena_root` before `sqlite3_deserialize`. Substrate corruption between producer publish and consumer load is structurally detected.

**Hash semantics:** SQLite header parsing recovers data length from `page_size * page_count` (file format §1.3); reader hashes data prefix to match T2.2's writer-side `BLAKE3(db_bytes)`. Layout-independent → content-addressed.

**Three-way fallback:**
- Zero-root sentinel → skip verification (legacy + flush_to_arena compat)
- Non-SQLite buffer + non-zero root → clear error (substrate confusion catch)
- Mismatched hash → "arena root mismatch — substrate corruption detected" (the bug case)

**Tests (4):** happy / corrupted-buffer / zero-sentinel-skip / non-SQLite-error.

**Known followup:** `flush_to_arena` in graph.rs still uses legacy `set_arena` (no root published). Reader hits zero-sentinel skip — backwards compat. Wiring flush_to_arena to `set_arena_with_root` is a small follow-up bead; not a regression.

**T2.4 unblocked.** `task ci` green; cli-lib 36 → 40 tests; workspace 285+.

Closing.

title: T2.2 Compute σ(arena buffer) on snapshot; populate current_root
description:
Bead 2.2 of T2 under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac. Channel: final.

snapshot_to_arena hashes the db_bytes via BLAKE3 and writes both generation+1 AND current_root in the same atomic-flip step.

Concrete deliverables:
- ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs::snapshot_to_arena: blake3::hash(&db_bytes) before write_to_arena
- Pass both gen and root to ctrl.set_arena_v2(arena_path, size, gen, root)
- Pin: σ(snapshot bytes) consistent across hosts (DET axiom)

Depends on T2.1 (current_root field must exist).

comments.text:
Shipped at bfbab59.

**API:** new `set_arena_with_root(path, size, generation, root)` in leyline-core. Writes path/size/root as plain stores, then `std::sync::atomic::fence(Release)`, then atomic-Release-store of generation. Existing `set_arena(path, size, gen)` preserves current_root unchanged — 20+ existing callers needed zero changes.

**Wired:** `snapshot_to_arena` step-4 now computes `BLAKE3(db_bytes)` and publishes via `set_arena_with_root`. Σ §3.4's "BLAKE3 locked" claim has its concrete enforcement site.

**Tests (5):**
- `set_arena_with_root_writes_all_four_fields` — basic API
- `set_arena_preserves_current_root` — legacy preservation regression-proof
- `set_arena_with_root_visible_across_controllers` — cross-Controller visibility
- `set_arena_with_root_root_never_stale_under_writer_race` — concurrent writer-monotone invariant
- `snapshot_populates_current_root_with_blake3_of_db_bytes` (integration) — end-to-end pin
- `snapshot_idempotent_root_for_same_db_state` — pure-function-of-bytes
- `snapshot_root_advances_when_db_changes` — negative pin

**Bug found and resolved during testing:** the original concurrent-ordering test asserted `gen=N ⟹ root[0]==N`, which is wrong. Writer can race ahead and write root for N+1 *before* publishing gen=N+1; reader observing gen=N can legally see future root. The actual invariant is *writer-monotone advancement* (root never reverts to a previous gen). The seqlock-strict version of the test caught this; replaced with the correct invariant pin.

**Unblocks:** T2.3 (`bad8f1` reader-side verify), T2.4 (`baee26` deprecate generation). Also T8.5's daemon-side root advance lights up once 5f7100-15 (file-backed live db) ships — until then `:memory:` connections still don't get capnp segments to hash.

`task ci` green; workspace 285+; leyline-core 27→29; cli-lib 33→36.

Closing.

title: T1.2 TLA+/Apalache spec of R1–R7 + canonicality argument
description:
Bead 1.2 of T1 (math foundations) under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac.

**SCOPE UPDATED 2026-05-01** after §3 red-team (`docs/decades/red-team/2026-05-01-section-3-necessity.md`) found the original "necessity" framing was undefendable. Bead now mechanizes the **canonicality argument**: Σ is the canonical realization of R1–R7 in the vector-commitment family. Verkle, accumulator, and auth-skip-list members are admissible substitutes (not counterexamples).

Channel: analysis.

Concrete deliverables:
- TLA+ spec at docs/decades/spec/MerkleCAS.tla
- Defines: Hash, BlobStore, RootPointer, write protocol (CAS-advance), reader protocol (verify-then-load)
- Refines R1–R7 (per the §2 operationalization) as TLA+ properties / temporal formulas
- Apalache bounded model check at depth ≥ 7 (one step per requirement)
- Outcome: machine-checked statement that no execution satisfies R1–R7 outside the vector-commitment family of root structures
- F6 adversarial counterexample search: bound to "vector-commitment family member that is non-Σ-isomorphic" — not "any system that meets R1–R7", which the red-team showed is too broad (Verkle/accumulator/skip-list are admissible)

Effort: ~2-3 weeks. Apalache learning curve assumed.

Red-team gaps to encode in the spec:
- R3 split into R3a (content identity self-named) and R3b (actor identity exogenous)
- R5 carries a retention horizon T (not "all past states forever")
- R6 specifies k = O(1) bound for single-leaf updates
- R7 operationalized as "external systems hold only (R_n, sig_n) at runtime; static crypto config admitted as immutable bootstrap"

title: Σ — Merkle-CAS Substrate as Unifying Primitive (decade)
description:
Decade-level architectural primitive: a content-addressed, Merkle-structured, CAS-advanced state substrate Σ = (𝓥, 𝓒, ρ, σ, R, S) is necessary to satisfy the platform's R1–R7 requirements (concurrent writes, cross-host distribution, integrity, crash consistency, time-travel, registry-scale partial updates, compositionality with workerd/signet/mache/rosary).

The current arena protocol (Controller, ArenaHeader, HotSwapGraph) is a degenerate case of Σ — sequence-named instead of hash-named. ADR-A (Sheaf Cache, Čech cohomology) and ADR-B (Merkle Sheaf Sync) are upper layers built on Σ.

This decade decomposes into 7 threads:
- T1: Mathematical foundations (substrate + necessity proof + falsifiability)
- T2: Controller migration (current_root replaces generation)
- T3: Content-addressed blob store (git-shaped objects/)
- T4: Subtree Merkle structure (per-file/per-table)
- T5: Composition seams (signet/workerd/rosary/mache integration)
- T6: Concurrent-write protocol (optimistic CCC + rebase)
- T7: Falsification gauntlet (F1–F6 in CI)

Feature-completion criterion (from §6 of the decade doc):
1. All R1–R7 have ≥1 falsification protocol (F1–F6) passing in CI
2. Necessity proof mechanized in TLA+/Apalache; bounded model check finds no counterexample at depth ≥7
3. Composition seams (T5) exercised by an integration test touching signet+workerd+rosary+mache
4. Arena migration (T2) shipped: current_root canonical, generation removed
5. F2 throughput at N=10 concurrent writers ≥ 4× serial baseline

Full doc: docs/decades/2026-merkle-cas-substrate.md
Channel (BDR/Harmony): analysis

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] §3 red-team complete. theoretical-foundations-analyst found 5 gaps + 1 under-operationalization in the original necessity proof.

**Outcome:** decade claim downgraded from "Σ is necessary" to "Σ is canonical" — Σ is the canonical representative of the vector-commitment family (Verkle trees, RSA accumulators, auth skip lists are admissible substitutes, not counterexamples).

Specific fixes applied to docs/decades/2026-merkle-cas-substrate.md:
- §0: one-line claim updated
- §2: R3 split into R3a (content) / R3b (actor); R5 carries retention horizon; R6 specifies k=O(1); R7 operationalized as "hash-only mutable runtime, static crypto config admitted"
- §3.1: separates content-identity from actor-identity (Gap 2 fix)
- §3.2: IM attributed to R4 ∧ R5 jointly, not R4 alone (Gap 3 fix; LMDB/WAL counterexample)
- §3.3: drops "minimal", attributes single-root commit to R5 ∧ R6 (Gap 4 fix; CRDT/MVCC/Raft alternatives)
- §3.4: "Merkle is unique" → "Merkle is canonical representative of vector-commitment family" (Gap 1 fix; Verkle/accumulator/skip-list counterexamples)
- §3.6: "no shared state" → "no shared mutable runtime state; static crypto config admitted" (Gap 5 fix)
- §6 feature-completion: aligned with canonicality framing
- §3.7 conclusion: "Σ is necessary" → "Σ is canonical"

T1.2 (9e5a1b) and T7.6 (bcb964) updated to scope to canonicality search, not necessity proof.

The operational program (T1–T7, F1–F6, feature-completion criterion) is **unchanged** by the weakening — only Bead 1.2 and Bead 7.6 adjust target.

Full red-team analysis preserved at docs/decades/red-team/2026-05-01-section-3-necessity.md for audit.

WAL experiment (the second calibration) still running in background; will comment when complete.

title: sheaf: bundle_majority empty-input diverges from BUNDLE_MAJORITY SQL UDF (Rust→ZERO_HV, SQL→NULL)
description:
File: rs/ll-open/hdc/src/sheaf.rs line 318-330 vs rs/ll-open/hdc/src/sql_udf.rs lines 204-207.

The module docstring for bundle_majority states: 'matches the SQLite BUNDLE_MAJORITY UDF semantics so SQL-side and Rust-side bundles produce identical results on the same inputs.' This is false for empty input.

Rust bundle_majority(&[]) returns ZERO_HV ([0u8; 1024]) — the zero hypervector.
SQL BUNDLE_MAJORITY over zero rows returns NULL (Value::Null).

The empty-input case is not a contrived edge case: a caller that does a propagate_sections on a layer with no stalks will get ZERO_HV from the Rust side, but a caller that runs BUNDLE_MAJORITY in SQL over a filtered set with zero matching rows will get NULL. Code that passes the Rust output directly into SQL (or vice versa) will silently diverge.

There is NO test that compares the two implementations on an empty input — the Rust test 'bundle_majority_empty_is_zero' only checks the Rust side, and 'bundle_majority_empty_set_is_null' only checks the SQL side. Neither test flags the semantic mismatch between the two.

Fix sketch: document the divergence explicitly in both functions (empty-SQL → NULL, empty-Rust → ZERO_HV) and decide which semantics callers of propagate_sections should see. If ZERO_HV is load-bearing (it's the lattice bottom), add a SQL-side COALESCE(BUNDLE_MAJORITY(...), zeroblob(1024)) wrapper and document it. Do NOT silently make them differ on a documented-identical invariant.

comments.text: Fixed in commit 162dfc1. Doc now explicitly calls out Rust ZERO_HV vs SQL NULL divergence on empty input + recommends COALESCE wrapper SQL-side. Two new tests pin parity: bundle_majority_matches_sql_udf_on_nonempty (byte-identical output on three concrete stalks) and bundle_majority_empty_divergence_documented (locks the intentional divergence in place).

title: hdc-10: hotspot-explanation API — load-bearing test that justifies HDC over LSH
description:
## Scope

The load-bearing test that justifies HDC over plain LSH. Per math-friend review G: **if the hotspot UI doesn't surface unbind output, we delete the binding code and ship simhash+LSH instead.**

This bead implements the surface that proves HDC pulls its weight.

## The API

```json
{"op":"hdc_explain_cluster", "centroid_scope_id": "...", "layer": "ast", "radius": 512}
```

Returns:
```json
{
    "ok": true,
    "centroid": "...",
    "members": ["scope_a", "scope_b", "scope_c"],
    "shared_skeleton": {
        "root_kind": "Block",
        "child_0": "Op",        // recovered via unbind
        "child_1": "Stmt",
        "child_2": "Stmt"
    },
    "diverging_positions": ["child_3"]  // positions where members disagree
}
```

## Algorithm

```
1. Find members within radius of centroid via op_hdc_search.
2. Compute the cluster centroid as the bit-wise majority of all member HVs.
3. For each role position [child_0, child_1, ..., child_max_arity]:
   a. unbind(centroid, role_position)
   b. Run cleanup-memory: nearest neighbor in codebook
   c. If confident (margin > threshold) → record the canonical-kind for that role
   d. Else → mark as "diverging"
4. Return the recovered shared skeleton + the diverging positions
```

## Why this is the load-bearing test

LSH gives you "these N items are similar." It cannot tell you *why* — what they share, what they don't. HDC's bind algebra gives you both the cluster AND the structural recipe. If we can answer "this hotspot shares: `Block` root, `Op` child_0, `Stmt` children at child_1+2" — we're delivering something LSH can't.

If we can't surface this output reliably (cleanup memory keeps returning ambiguous results, recovery success rate <80%), HDC isn't pulling its weight. We delete the binding code and ship simhash+LSH.

## Tests

### `hotspot_explanation_recovers_shared_skeleton`

Plant 10 functions that all have the same skeleton `if x { return y; }` but different variables. Encode. Query `op_hdc_explain_cluster` against one. Assert:
- `shared_skeleton.root_kind == "Block"` (the function body)
- `shared_skeleton.child_0 == "Op"` (the if statement)
- `shared_skeleton.child_1 == "Stmt"` (the return)
- `diverging_positions` is empty

### `hotspot_explanation_marks_diverging_positions`

Plant 5 functions with the same skeleton + 5 with a slight variation (one has an extra statement). Query the cluster centroid. Assert the extra-statement position appears in `diverging_positions`.

### `hotspot_explanation_recovery_success_rate`

Run on real codebase fixture (not synthetic). Measure: for the 10 largest clusters, does explanation return non-empty `shared_skeleton`? **Pass threshold: ≥80% recovery success rate.** Below that, HDC has lost its case against LSH.

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/explain.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/ops.rs` — wire `op_hdc_explain_cluster`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/integration.rs` — 3 integration tests

## Definition of done

3 integration tests pass. Real-codebase test demonstrates ≥80% recovery success on found hotspots. **If recovery rate is below that threshold, this bead's outcome is "delete binding code, ship simhash+LSH" — file the alternative bead for that.**

## Parent

ley-line-open-96b1a9

title: hdc-8: cargo `hdc` feature flag + additive integration into ops registry
hdc-5: query ops — radius search, density, unbind, combined-prefilter
description:
## Scope

Wire HDC ops into the daemon's `handle_base_op` dispatch + MCP `tool_registry`. Four ops total, each opt-in via `#[cfg(feature = "hdc")]`.

## Ops

### `op_hdc_search` — radius query

```json
{"op":"hdc_search", "query": "<base64 hv or scope_id>", "layer": "ast", "r": 512, "limit": 50}
```

`SELECT scope_id, popcount(hv XOR ?1) AS d FROM _hdc WHERE layer_kind=?2 AND d <= ?3 ORDER BY d LIMIT ?4`

Returns ranked list of similar scopes within Hamming radius `r`.

### `op_hdc_density` — equivalence-class membership test

```json
{"op":"hdc_density", "query": "...", "layer": "ast", "r": 512}
```

`SELECT count(*) FROM _hdc WHERE layer_kind=?1 AND popcount(hv XOR ?2) <= ?3`

Returns the count of scopes within radius. >1 means the query is in a cluster (hotspot membership).

### `op_hdc_unbind` — cluster explanation

```json
{"op":"hdc_unbind", "scope_id": "...", "layer": "ast", "role": "child_0"}
```

Take the function-level hypervector for `scope_id`, unbind via role permutation, run cleanup-memory against the lazily-materialized codebook of known kinds, return the recovered kind. Recursive variant takes `path: ["child_0", "child_1", ...]` to walk arbitrary depth.

This is the load-bearing op per the math-friend review G — without it, the binding algebra doesn't pay rent and we ship simhash+LSH instead.

### `op_hdc_combined_prefilter` — multi-layer top-k pruning

```json
{"op":"hdc_combined_prefilter", "query": "...", "limit": 1000}
```

Query against `_hdc_combined` for fast top-k pruning. Caller then issues weighted per-layer reranks via SQL JOIN on `_hdc`.

## SQL UDF for popcount

Register a custom SQLite function `popcount(blob)` that uses `std::simd` or platform popcount. Without this, popcount distance is a slow byte-by-byte loop.

## Dispatch wiring (additive, per the design discipline)

```rust
// daemon/ops.rs
match op {
    ...
    #[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
    "hdc_search" => Some(op_hdc_search(ctx, req)),
    #[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
    "hdc_density" => Some(op_hdc_density(ctx, req)),
    #[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
    "hdc_unbind" => Some(op_hdc_unbind(ctx, req)),
    #[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
    "hdc_combined_prefilter" => Some(op_hdc_combined_prefilter(ctx, req)),
    ...
}

// base_op_names()
#[cfg(feature = "hdc")] v.extend_from_slice(&["hdc_search", "hdc_density", "hdc_unbind", "hdc_combined_prefilter"]);

// daemon/mcp.rs tool_registry — same #[cfg] gate, four McpTool entries
```

## Tests

- `op_hdc_search_radius_filter` — populate _hdc, query at varied r, assert correct count returned
- `op_hdc_density_counts_cluster_size` — populate cluster, query density of cluster centroid
- `op_hdc_unbind_recovers_known_kind` — encode a known fixture, unbind first child role, assert canonical kind matches
- `op_hdc_combined_prefilter_correctness` — populate combined view, prefilter against query, assert top-k contains all true matches
- `popcount_udf_correctness` — register UDF, run on known BLOBs, verify count matches `count_ones()` reference
- `hdc_ops_present_in_canonical_op_names` — drift guard on `base_op_names()`

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/ops.rs` (NEW — op handlers)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/popcount_udf.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs` — match arms + base_op_names update
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mcp.rs` — tool_registry entries

## Definition of done

6 tests pass. All four ops wired into both UDS dispatch and MCP tool registry. Drift-guard tests catch any forgetting.

title: hdc-4: HdcPass encoder — generic over BaseCodebook + content-hash subtree cache
hdc-3a: AST codebook — Deckard-style leaf-erased Merkle on canonical alphabet
hdc-2: canonical-kind alphabet — single source of truth, per-parser mapping
description:
## Scope

The single highest-leverage decision in the whole HDC stack (per math friend's review C). Define a canonical alphabet of structural kinds that every parser-specific node-kind maps into. Without this, AST equivalence classes are unstable across language frontends; with it, cross-language hotspot detection becomes possible.

## The alphabet

```rust
pub enum CanonicalKind {
    Decl,    // function, class, struct, enum, type, variable declarations
    Expr,    // expressions, calls, member accesses, indexing
    Stmt,    // statements (assignment, return, throw, expression-statement)
    Block,   // compound statements, blocks, scopes
    Ref,     // identifier references, qualified names
    Lit,     // literals (number, string, bool, nil)
    Op,      // operators, control-flow keywords (if/else/loop tags)
}
```

7 variants, fits in 3 bits. Maps to the alphabet Deckard (Jiang 2007) uses for clone detection. Other clone-detection literature uses similar small canonical alphabets — converged on this scale.

## Per-parser mapping

For each tree-sitter grammar in scope, ship a `Vec<(&str, CanonicalKind)>` table mapping the grammar's node-kinds to the canonical kind. Tables live in `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/canonical/<lang>.rs`.

Initial languages:
- Rust (~150 named node-kinds)
- Go (~120)
- Python (~100)
- TypeScript (~180)

Anything not mapped defaults to `Block` (the most-neutral structural carrier — picks up boilerplate without polluting other classes).

## Why this matters

The math-friend review caught that production-signature hashing alone isn't grammar-stable: tree-sitter inlines what ANTLR keeps named, so child-kind multisets genuinely differ across parsers. Mapping every parser-kind into a 7-element canonical alphabet collapses parser-specific noise. Two functions with the same control-flow shape — written in Rust vs Go — produce the same canonical-kind sequence, even if their parser-kind names diverge.

## Cross-version stability test

Run two consecutive tree-sitter versions side-by-side; confirm that the canonical-kind sequence for a given file is identical even when individual node-kind names changed (tree-sitter has changed e.g. `if_statement` → `if_clause` between minor versions; canonical mapping survives that).

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/canonical.rs` (NEW — `CanonicalKind` enum + lookup trait)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/canonical/rust.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/canonical/go.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/canonical/python.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/canonical/typescript.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/mod.rs` — module wiring

## Tests

- `canonical_kind_mapping_covers_all_named_kinds` per language — every grammar-named node-kind has a mapping.
- `canonical_kind_mapping_stable_across_grammar_versions` — mapping output unchanged when tree-sitter dependency bumps.
- `cross_language_canonical_invariance` — fixture: same control-flow shape in Rust + Go + Python yields the same canonical sequence.

## Definition of done

The four initial-language tables are complete (every named tree-sitter kind in those grammars has a mapping). The cross-language invariance test passes on a hand-written `if-then-return` fixture in all four languages.
