ley-line-open-96b1a9

title: hdc-1: storage schema — _hdc, _hdc_combined, _hdc_baseline, _hdc_subtree_cache
description:
## Scope

Land the SQLite schema for the HDC sidecar tables. Pure schema work — no encoder, no codebooks, no ops yet. Establishes the contract that downstream beads write to.

## Tables

```sql
CREATE TABLE _hdc (
  scope_id   TEXT NOT NULL,
  layer_kind TEXT NOT NULL,         -- {ast, semantic, temporal, module, lex(reserved), fs(reserved)}
  hv         BLOB NOT NULL,         -- D/8 bytes (default D=8192 → 1024 bytes)
  basis      INTEGER NOT NULL,      -- per-layer freshness key (parse_version, embed_version, head_sha hash, etc.)
  PRIMARY KEY (scope_id, layer_kind)
);

CREATE INDEX _hdc_layer_basis_idx ON _hdc(layer_kind, basis);

CREATE TABLE _hdc_combined (
  scope_id  TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  hv        BLOB NOT NULL,           -- XOR-bind of per-layer HVs
  basis     INTEGER NOT NULL         -- max(per-layer basis)
);

CREATE TABLE _hdc_baseline (
  layer_kind        TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  median_distance   INTEGER NOT NULL,  -- popcount over corpus sample
  mad               INTEGER NOT NULL,  -- median absolute deviation
  sample_size       INTEGER NOT NULL,
  computed_at_ms    INTEGER NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE _hdc_subtree_cache (
  content_hash BLOB(32) PRIMARY KEY,  -- BLAKE3 of subtree canonical form
  hv           BLOB NOT NULL,
  ref_count    INTEGER DEFAULT 1
);
```

## Why this shape

- `_hdc` keyed on `(scope_id, layer_kind)` so any one row is independently queryable per layer.
- `layer_kind` is TEXT not enum so it's extensible at the data layer without migrations.
- Schema reserves `lex` and `fs` variants for future addition without schema change.
- `_hdc_combined` is the unweighted XOR-bind prefilter (math friend's review F).
- `_hdc_baseline` stores the empirical radius calibration (math friend's review B). Computed at daemon startup.
- `_hdc_subtree_cache` is the content-hash-keyed cache that makes bidi unbind tractable (avoids re-encoding subtrees on every query).

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/schema.rs` (NEW)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/mod.rs` (NEW — module skeleton, re-export `create_hdc_schema`)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mod.rs` — `#[cfg(feature = "hdc")] pub mod hdc;`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/Cargo.toml` — `hdc = []` feature flag

## Test

- Unit test `create_hdc_schema_idempotent` — running on existing schema is a no-op.
- Unit test `_hdc_pkey_enforces_layer_uniqueness` — same scope_id with same layer_kind rejects duplicate insert.
- Unit test `_hdc_layer_basis_idx_used` — EXPLAIN shows the index is hit on `WHERE layer_kind=? AND basis>?` queries.

## Definition of done

`cargo test -p leyline-cli-lib --features hdc --lib` passes 3 new tests. No changes to any existing schema. `--no-default-features` build is byte-identical to today.

## Parent
ley-line-open-96b1a9 (epic: HDC hypervectors as structural stalks)

title: epic: HDC hypervectors as structural stalks (LLO HdcPass + sheaf-cache integration)
description:
## Why this exists

Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) gives us a third coordinate system alongside the structural AST tables (`_ast`, `nodes`) and the dense semantic VectorIndex (sqlite-vec sidecar). It uniquely fills a gap none of those handle well: **structural fuzzy match under α-renames** ("find every function that has *this shape* even if variable names changed").

But the strategic insight isn't just AST search. HDC hypervectors are **richer stalks** for the existing sheaf machinery:

- ADR-011 (sheaf cache) defines stalks as "content hash (Merkle root) summarizing a topological region's current state" — but explicitly says "any data structure that can be hashed can produce a stalk." Merkle stalks only give *equality* (changed / unchanged). HDC stalks give *continuous structural similarity* via Hamming distance.
- ADR-012 (merkle sheaf sync) per-community stalks today drive sync decisions binarily. HDC stalks let us measure *how much* a community drifted, not just whether — informing partial sync, weighted restriction maps, and the defect vector `||d⁰(stalks)||²` continuously rather than 0-or-large.

So the actual scope is: ship the HdcPass in LLO; the private LL crate then wires HDC stalks into the sheaf-cache and merkle-sync layers as a richer alternative to (or complement of) plain Merkle roots.

## Math foundation (vetted)

Theoretical-foundations review confirmed:
- For binary MAP-B with D=8192 (byte-aligned popcount), capacity bound `D ≥ 2·N·log(M/ε)` handles N≈250 items per flat bundle cleanly.
- Most function ASTs exceed N=300 → **flat bundling fails** (your initial proposal). Need **hierarchical/recursive** encoding (Plate 1994, Schlegel 2022): each subtree → its own hypervector → XOR-bound with ρ-permuted child slot into parent → bundled. XOR is bijective, depth doesn't lose info; Schlegel showed D=10k handles depth 5–7 cleanly, matches typical AST.
- Edit fidelity: one leaf rename → ΔH/D ≈ 1/(2√N). Sqrt-scaled, not linear. Threshold tuning has to use the curve.
- Genuinely orthogonal to dense embeddings (lexical) and AST tables (rigid). Not dominated.

## Subordinate beads (to file separately)

1. `hdc-1`: `_hdc` sidecar table + storage layout (BLOB column, parse_version basis).
2. `hdc-2`: codebook — random base vectors per AST node-kind + ρ permutation for child position. Seeded RNG, persisted alongside parse_version so different daemon instances produce comparable vectors.
3. `hdc-3`: `HdcPass` enrichment pass — recursive hierarchical encoding, function granularity. Bottom-up bundling capped at local fanout. Memoize by subtree content-hash.
4. `hdc-4`: `op_hdc_search` — bottom-k by `popcount(query_hv XOR row.hv)`. SQL UDF for hamming distance (or reuse sqlite-vec's bit-mode if available).
5. `hdc-5`: feature flag `hdc` (cfg-gated like `vec`), default-off. Documented.
6. `hdc-6`: integration tests — clone detection (rename one var, expect Hamming d/D ≈ 1/(2√N)), structural pattern search (find all `if-then-return` shapes), and a drift-guard that random-pair Hamming distance stays near 0.5.

## Scope, this epic

LLO-side only. The LL-side wiring (HDC stalks in sheaf cache, weighted restrictions, continuous defect) is a separate epic in the LL repo. This epic's deliverable: `_hdc` table populated correctly, `op_hdc_search` returns the documented Hamming-rank, all behind `hdc` feature flag.

## Files (anticipated)

- NEW: `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc.rs` — codebook + encoder + HdcPass + op_hdc_search.
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mod.rs` — register `pub mod hdc;` under cfg.
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs` — wire `op_hdc_search` into handle_base_op + tool_registry + base_op_names.
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/Cargo.toml` — `hdc` feature flag.
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/integration.rs` — integration tests.

## Math friend's recipe (for sub-beads to follow)

- D = 8192 bits (byte-aligned, fast popcount, ~18% smaller than 10k with negligible capacity loss)
- Binary MAP-B: XOR-bind `kind ⊕ position`, majority-bundle locally, ρ-permute for child order
- Hierarchical: bottom-up recursive; cache per-subtree by content-hash
- Threshold (starting point, tune empirically): `d/D < 0.42` for "match". Expect <10⁻⁶ FP, ~90–95% recall for k=1–3 leaf edits at function sizes 30–150 nodes.

## Connection to existing work

- ADR-011 (`docs/design/011-sheaf-cache.md` in ley-line repo) — HDC stalks slot in as richer Merkle alternatives.
- ADR-012 (`docs/design/012-merkle-sheaf-sync.md` in ley-line repo) — HDC enables continuous sync decisions.
- Existing `EnrichmentPass` framework (`daemon/enrichment.rs`) — HdcPass slots in downstream of TreeSitterPass, parallel to EmbeddingPass.
- Existing SSOT pattern (`base_op_names`, `tool_registry`, `STATE_CHANGING_OPS`) — `op_hdc_search` registers the same way as `op_vec_search`.

comments.text:
## Gearbox / logogram lead — codebook design

User redirected: see `../x-ray/internal/cartographer/cairn_*.go` (Cairn cartographer "Semantic Gearbox") and the "logogram" framing.

x-ray's gearbox is a multi-resolution coding-theory pipeline for visual zone tokenization:
- Gear 1: Tetracode [4,2,3] — 4D ternary, 9 codewords (coarsest)
- Gear 3: Ternary Golay [12,6,6] — 12D, 729 codewords
- Gear 5: Leech lattice Λ₂₄ — 24D continuous (default)
- Gear 6: Barnes-Wall BW₃₂ — 32D, finest

Each visual zone gets quantized to *one discrete codeword* in a richly-structured lattice. That's the "logogram" framing: **a unit of meaning encoded as a single symbol in a richly-structured space**, where distance is meaningful.

### Three concrete links to HDC

1. **Codebook design** — the math friend's recipe used random Bernoulli base vectors per AST node-kind (textbook HDC). Replace with **Leech/Golay/Tetracode codewords** as base vectors. Same XOR-bind / majority-bundle algebra; better properties:
   - Provably better minimum Hamming distance between distinct kinds (no accidental collisions among ~200 tree-sitter node-kinds)
   - Lattice quantization for nearest-codeword cleanup (more robust than raw popcount-threshold)
   - Closed-form analysis of capacity bounds (lattice codes have known sphere-packing density)

2. **Multi-resolution recursive encoding** — the gearbox's progressive Gear1→Gear5 stages mirror HDC's hierarchical/recursive bundling depth. We can use *coarse* lattice codes for shallow subtrees and *fine* lattice codes for deep ones, controlling capacity per depth. Maps directly onto Schlegel 2022's hierarchical VSA approach but with lattice base vectors instead of random ones.

3. **Cross-modal logograms — the strategic insight.** If both code-LLO and visual-x-ray use the *same* lattice base vectors, then their hypervectors live in the same space. You can literally bind a code-AST hypervector with a visual-screenshot hypervector and ask "which functions correspond to which UI zones." This is the unified "logogram" representation across art's ecosystem — code, visual, document zones, all in one coordinate system.

### Updated sub-bead recipe

`hdc-2` (codebook) should pick **Leech-derived base vectors** by default, not Bernoulli random:
- 196,560 minimum-norm Leech vectors → plenty of distinct codewords for ~200 AST node-kinds with huge headroom
- Maps to D=8192 by lifting/embedding (Leech is naturally D=24 but tensor products into higher-D codes preserve minimum-distance properties)
- Alternative: Barnes-Wall BW₃₂ for byte-aligned ops with provable minimum distance

`hdc-3` (HdcPass) stays the same algorithmically — still bind+bundle hierarchically — but base vectors come from the lattice codebook instead of an RNG.

### Files to read for codebook implementation

- `internal/cartographer/cairn_leech.go` — `ProjectToLeech` and Λ₂₄ generator matrix
- `internal/cartographer/cairn_tetracode.go` — `QuantizeTetracode` and the [4,2,3] generator matrix at lines 16-27
- `internal/cartographer/cairn_m12.go` — Mathieu group M₁₂ for Golay code automorphisms
- `internal/cartographer/cairn_pipeline.go` — how the gearbox stages compose

### Updated bead title (mental note)

The epic isn't quite "HDC hypervectors as structural stalks" anymore — it's **"Lattice-coded logograms as structural stalks"**. HDC algebra (bind + bundle) is the operator; lattice codes (Leech, Golay, BW32) are the representation.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## Correction: bidi is the goal, quantization is wrong frame

The previous comment talked about "lattice quantization for cleanup" — that was sloppy. AST node-kinds are already discrete (`IfStatement` is `IfStatement`); there's nothing analog to quantize. x-ray's gearbox quantizes *continuous* visual features (luminance, edge density) to nearest lattice points — that's the right move for analog signals. For our use case, the lattice's role is **codebook with provable minimum-distance**, not quantizer.

The actual goal is **bidirectional (invertible) encoding**, and HDC's bind/bundle/cleanup algebra is built for it:

```
encode:    H(node) = base[kind(node)] ⊕ ρ⁰·H(child₀) ⊕ ρ¹·H(child₁) ⊕ ...
unbind:    H ⊕ ρ⁰  → noisy estimate of H(child₀)
cleanup:   nearest-neighbor in codebook → recover kind
recurse:   query subtree-hypervector cache keyed on content-hash
```

XOR-bind is bijective. With a cleanup memory (the codebook) + cached subtree hypervectors (content-hash keyed), the encoding becomes **walkable both directions**: encode bottom-up, recover top-down by repeatedly unbinding ρⁱ and cleaning up.

### Why this beats plain Merkle stalks for sheaf cache

| Property | Merkle hash | HDC bidi vector |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Equality detection | ✓ | ✓ (popcount = 0) |
| Continuous similarity | ✗ | ✓ (small popcount) |
| Partial reconstruction | ✗ | ✓ (unbind + cleanup) |
| Restriction-map weights | trivial 0/1 | continuous via Hamming distance |
| Defect vector `||d⁰||²` | 0 or large | continuous structural defect |

### Bidi cost (be honest)

Recovery at depth D requires D successful cleanups, each with small failure probability `p`. Per-cleanup error at D=8192 with ~200-entry codebook is ~10⁻⁶ — fine for typical AST function depth 5-7. Past depth ~12 it compounds (~(1-10⁻⁶)¹² ≈ 99.999%). For function granularity (the target), bidi works. For whole-file granularity, it doesn't.

### Updated sub-bead recipe (supersedes previous)

- `hdc-2` (codebook): Leech/Golay/BW32 **base vectors** (not quantizers). Chosen for minimum-distance properties — gives reliable cleanup memory.
- `hdc-3` (HdcPass): hierarchical bind+bundle, **cache every subtree hypervector** keyed on content-hash. The cache is what makes bidi work — without it, bundle is one-way.
- `hdc-4` was `op_hdc_search`, now becomes **two ops**:
  - `op_hdc_search` — popcount-rank for similarity queries (fast).
  - `op_hdc_unbind` — recover structural info from a function-level vector (slow but bidi).
- `hdc-7` (new): subtree-hypervector cache layer. parse_version-versioned, content-hash-keyed BLOB store. This is the structure that makes bidi tractable.

### Lattice codes vs Bernoulli — independent of bidi choice

Bernoulli random vectors *also* support bidi via cleanup memory; the algebra doesn't care. Lattice codes give better SNR margin for cleanup at deeper trees (quantitative win), but they're not strictly required for bidi to function. So `hdc-2` can ship with Bernoulli first and upgrade to lattice codebook later — bidi works on day one.

The strategic value of the lattice route remains the **cross-modal logogram** angle: if x-ray and LLO share lattice base vectors, code hypervectors and visual hypervectors live in the same space. That's a separate decision, downstream of getting bidi working.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## Unifying frame: HDC as continuity-equipped logogram, alongside tokenizers/ASTs/CAS/CDC

User's lens: tokenizing, ASTs/CSTs, CAS, CDC, HDC, sheaf stalks are all instances of one pattern. Capturing it because it informs the integration design.

| System | Symbols | Composition algebra | Cleanup store | Bidi via |
|--------|---------|--------------------|--------------:|----------|
| Tokenizers (BPE) | subwords | concatenation | vocabulary | `decode ∘ encode ≈ id` |
| ASTs/CSTs | node-kinds | tree containment | grammar | re-print from CST |
| CAS | blobs | hash-of-bytes | content store | hash → blob lookup |
| CDC | chunks | hash-of-chunks | chunk store | rolling-hash boundaries |
| HDC | base vectors | XOR-bind + bundle | codebook + subtree cache | unbind + cleanup |
| Merkle stalks | regions | restriction maps | Merkle tree | equality only (today) |

The triple in every row: **discrete signature + composable algebra + cleanup memory ⇒ bidi**.

### What this changes about the LLO design

1. **`_hdc` is a CAS for hypervectors, not a separate sidecar.** Content-hash key → hypervector → constituent kind+children (via subtree cache). Plug into ley-line's existing CAS layer — same primitive, different payload.

2. **Hierarchical HDC encoding *is* CDC chunking, structurally.** Top-level hypervector = bind of child-permuted child hypervectors; CDC top-hash = hash of chunk hashes. Same identity, different algebra (XOR-bind+bundle vs. concatenate-and-hash). Implication: ley-line's existing CDC chunk store can host the hypervector cache without redesign.

3. **The unique thing HDC adds**: continuous structural similarity *in the symbol space itself* (popcount), without a downstream embedder. Tokenizers need a learned embedding to get continuity. CAS/CDC/Merkle have no continuity. HDC bakes it in.

4. **Therefore: HDC stalks ≠ replacement for Merkle stalks.** They're a *richer* stalk class. Merkle stalks answer "is this region the same?" (binary). HDC stalks answer that *plus* "how similar?" (continuous). The sheaf cache's defect operator `||d⁰||²` becomes continuous; restriction-map weights become principled instead of trivially 0/1.

### Implementation reuse

- Subtree-hypervector cache (`hdc-7`): use ley-line's CAS infrastructure as the storage backend. Don't reinvent.
- Content-hash keying: same Merkle-style hash as the existing arena layer.
- Versioning: parse_version basis, identical to other enrichment passes.

### Position relative to dense embeddings

Dense embeddings (existing `VectorIndex`) get continuity through *learned semantic distribution* — they capture what tokens *mean* in context. They have **no structural awareness**. HDC vectors get continuity through *structural composition* — they capture what tokens *are* under tree-shape preserving operations. They have **no learned semantics**.

These are orthogonal axes:
- AST-table query: exact structural match (rigid)
- HDC popcount: fuzzy structural match (rename-invariant, edit-tolerant)
- Dense vector cosine: semantic match (paraphrase-tolerant, structure-blind)

LLO should expose all three at the op level. Clients (mache, ley-line sheaf cache, rosary) pick whichever axis their query needs.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## V0 (ship now) vs V1 (research) — codebook is human-based today, machine-based later

User flagged: today's encoding designs are human-based (parser-author kind-names, human-readable role labels). For machine reasoning, we want a label-invariant representation. This is genuinely a research problem with no settled answer in the HDC literature.

### What's human-based about the V0 recipe

- Hash `"if_statement"` → base vector. The string is a parser-author choice. Tree-sitter calls it `if_statement`; ANTLR calls it `ifStmt`; ESLint AST calls it `IfStatement`. Same code, different hypervectors across grammars.
- Role labels `"LeftChild"`, `"NextSibling"` are conventions, not topology.
- The codebook implicitly encodes *which language the code came from* via the parser's kind namespace.

### What machine-based looks like (research axes)

1. **Production-signature hashing**: hash the grammar production (multiset of child-kinds), not the label. Cross-parser robust.
2. **Equivalence-class induction**: group kinds by role (branching/looping/binding/applying); hash by class. All `for`/`while`/`do-while` → same base. Lossy but cross-language.
3. **Anonymous structural fingerprint**: hash `(in_degree, out_degree, depth_to_leaf, n_descendants)` quantized. No labels at all — pure topology.
4. **Learned base vectors**: contrastive training on semantic-equivalent code pairs. Most expressive; trades the "no codebook to ship" property.
5. **Frequency-induced (SkipGram-for-AST)**: hash by co-occurrence stats on a corpus. Deterministic per-corpus, machine-derived.

### Design implication: codebook is a trait, not a constant

```rust
trait BaseCodebook: Send + Sync {
    fn base_vector(&self, node: &AstNode) -> [u8; D/8];
    fn role_vector(&self, role: &Role) -> [u8; D/8];
}

struct LabelCodebook;          // V0: hash(kind_name) — human-based, ships today
struct ProductionCodebook;     // V1a: hash(production_signature) — research
struct EquivalenceCodebook;    // V1b: hash(class_id) — research
struct LearnedCodebook { ... } // V1c: trained hypervectors — research, ships an artifact
```

`HdcPass` takes a `Box<dyn BaseCodebook>`. Swapping codebooks is a one-argument change. Storage, queries, sheaf integration — unchanged. Pure additive.

### Subordinate bead structure refined

- `hdc-2` (codebook): ship `LabelCodebook` first; V1 codebooks land in `hdc-9..` as research-grade follow-ups, each gated `#[cfg(feature = "hdc_v1_*")]` so users opt-in per variant.
- All V1 codebooks reuse the same encoder (`hdc-3`), the same storage (`hdc-1`), and the same query ops (`hdc-4`). Codebook swap is the only delta.

### Why not skip V0 and go straight to research

V0 is the only variant where every other piece of the puzzle (storage, encoder, query, sheaf integration) can be validated end-to-end without inventing the codebook first. Ship V0 → confirm the rest works → then research the codebook in isolation, swap it in, regression-test against V0's test suite. Standard "make it work, then make it right" sequencing.

### Implementation language: Rust, std-only

- 128 × `u64` per D=8192 vector
- XOR-bind: element-wise XOR (auto-vectorizes to AVX2 / NEON)
- Bundle: bit-counting + majority threshold
- Popcount distance: `u64::count_ones()` reduce
- ~50-100 LOC for core encoder, single file
- No SIMD library needed for V0 — std handles it
- Future: `wide` or `packed_simd` if benchmarks show headroom; not for the initial ship
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## Multi-scale topology — six layers, one hypervector space

User's framing: we have topology at multiple levels (lex, AST/CST, code structure, file system, semantics, temporal), and the value comes from *combining the view*. HDC's algebraic uniformity is what makes the combination work — every layer produces a hypervector in the same D-dimensional space.

### The six layers

| Layer | Topology unit | Example query |
|-------|---------------|---------------|
| Lex | token sequence | "same token-bag in this function" |
| AST/CST | tree shape | "same control-flow structure" |
| Code | module/class hierarchy | "same class-method organization" |
| FS | directory layout | "test dirs that mirror src" |
| Semantic | meaning | "functions that do the same thing" |
| Temporal | edit history | "functions that change together" |

Each layer has its own topology, its own equivalence relation, its own hotspot patterns.

### Two combination strategies (ship both)

1. **Independent layers, weighted Hamming.**
   Per-layer sidecar tables; query carries a weight vector across layers. Consumer picks the mix.
   ```sql
   SELECT scope_id, 
          0.4 * popcount(?1 XOR _hdc_ast.hv) +
          0.3 * popcount(?2 XOR _hdc_lex.hv) +
          0.3 * popcount(?3 XOR _hdc_temporal.hv) AS weighted_distance
   FROM _hdc_ast JOIN _hdc_lex USING (scope_id) ...
   ```

2. **Cross-level bind into combined view.**
   ```
   H_combined = ρ_lex·H_lex ⊕ ρ_ast·H_ast ⊕ ρ_code·H_code ⊕ ρ_fs·H_fs ⊕ ρ_sem·H_sem ⊕ ρ_temp·H_temp
   ```
   XOR-bind across 6 layers — no saturation (binding ≠ bundling). One BLOB encodes all layers; one Hamming query asks "structurally similar at all layers simultaneously."

### Hotspots are multi-scale

This reshapes the hotspot story:
- **True hotspot**: dense neighbors at multiple layers (same shape AND similar tokens AND mirror directories AND change together)
- **Spurious cluster**: only matches at one layer (e.g., AST match but completely different semantics)
- **Cross-layer divergence**: "looks like its neighbors at AST level but semantically very different" → refactor candidate or bug suspect
- **Drift across vendored copies**: combined-view defect operator catches when one layer drifts away from the others

### Per-layer codebook

Each layer needs its own `BaseCodebook`:
- Lex: token-class signatures (topology-fingerprint, token-names are volatile across grammars)
- AST: production-signature (cross-version stable)
- Code: structural module-hash (already CAS primitive, deterministic)
- FS: path-relative fingerprint
- Semantic: dense vector → binary projection (existing VectorIndex projected via random-binary-projection)
- Temporal: git-history fingerprint hash

Same encoder + trait surface; only the codebook changes per layer.

### Storage shape

```
_hdc_lex(scope_id, hv BLOB, parse_version)
_hdc_ast(scope_id, hv BLOB, parse_version)
_hdc_code(scope_id, hv BLOB, parse_version)
_hdc_fs(scope_id, hv BLOB, fs_basis)
_hdc_sem(scope_id, hv BLOB, embed_version)
_hdc_temporal(scope_id, hv BLOB, head_sha)
_hdc_combined(scope_id, hv BLOB, combined_basis)
```

Where `scope_id` is any addressable unit — function, file, directory, module — keyed by content-hash.

### The "rope" insight extends all the way up

Path-as-address isn't just AST-internal (`src/lib.rs/fn_foo/if_block`). It scales:
- AST level: `fn_foo/if_block/then_branch`
- Code level: `crate::module::fn_foo`
- FS level: `~/work/repo/src/lib.rs`
- Combined: any level above + any level below

Path-prefix scoping at any layer gives layer-scoped queries: "all hotspots within this directory," "all token-patterns in this module."

### What this clarifies about the actual product

Per-layer hypervectors are the *substrate*. The combined-view is the *product* — what consumers (mache, sheaf cache, rosary) mostly want is "multi-scale equivalence class for this thing." A function's true identity in the codebase is the conjunction of all six layers; six fingerprints that collapse to one multi-scale logogram.

### Refined sub-bead structure (supersedes earlier numbering)

- `hdc-1`: storage layer — the per-layer table family (`_hdc_*`) + content-hash keying
- `hdc-2a..f`: per-layer codebooks (one each for lex/ast/code/fs/sem/temporal)
- `hdc-3`: encoder — generic over codebook, per-layer pass instances
- `hdc-4`: query ops — `op_hdc_search` (radius), `op_hdc_unbind` (recover), `op_hdc_combined` (multi-layer)
- `hdc-5`: feature flags (per-layer cfg gates)
- `hdc-6`: integration tests — per-layer + combined-view
- `hdc-7`: subtree-hypervector cache (CAS-backed)
- `hdc-8`: combined-view derivation (bind layers together)
- `hdc-9..`: V1 codebook research variants (production-signature, learned, etc.)
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## Math-friend review #2 — design corrections

Stress-test caught real issues. Verdict per axis:

- **A (capacity):** Solid. XOR-bind doesn't saturate. D=8192 leaves ~7× margin per layer; 6-layer chained cleanup hits ε≈6e-3.
- **B (density vs non-iid baseline):** **Fix required.** Real codebases aren't iid — `self`/`if`/`return` co-occur far above chance, so `D/2 ± √D/2` is the wrong null. Need empirical radius calibration: sample 10k random pairs, set `r = d_med - 3·MAD`. Document as daemon-startup step.
- **C (production-signature):** **Fix required.** Not grammar-stable on its own. Three-step fix: (1) hash only `is_named()` nodes, drop anonymous children; (2) bucket arity into `{0,1,2,3-4,5-7,8+}`; (3) map raw kinds to canonical alphabet `{Decl, Expr, Stmt, Block, Ref, Lit, Op}` per parser. Canonical-kind mapping is the highest-leverage decision in the whole stack — spend a day on it. Deckard (Jiang 2007) is the clone-detection precedent.
- **D (random-binary-projection for semantic):** Solid, but use **signed Charikar projection** not threshold-at-fixed-value. JL distortion at D=8192 wildly oversized. Real ceiling is source embedding's intrinsic dimension (~50-100 for code).
- **E (temporal):** Sketch needed. Right primitive: **time-decayed co-edit matrix** with τ≈90 days, then simhash the row with per-repo seeded random matrix. Append-only, ~1% bit churn per commit, fully deterministic.
- **F (weighted bind):** **Doesn't exist cleanly.** XOR is self-inverse — `A ⊕ A = 0`, not amplification. Store layers independently, weight at query time via `d = Σ w_i · d_i / Σ w_i`. Keep combined-view BLOB only as unweighted prefilter for top-k pruning before weighted rerank.
- **G (is this LSH-with-steps?):** **Justified only if unbind ships.** For indexing alone, Charikar simhash + multi-probe LSH (Lv 2007) gives same recall/throughput. What HDC adds is cluster explanation via unbind: "this hotspot shares callee X, loop pattern Y, error-handling Z." Hard test: **the hotspot UI must surface unbind output**, otherwise we ship simhash+LSH and delete the binding code.
- **H (layer redundancy):** **Ship 4, not 6.**
  - Lex × AST correlation ρ ≈ 0.6-0.8 — token-bag is downstream of tree shape. Drop Lex.
  - FS × Module correlation ρ ≈ 0.7+ in well-organized repos. Drop FS.
  - Keep AST, Semantic, Temporal, Module.
  - Schema enum keeps all 6 variants for cheap future optionality; populate 4 on day 1.

### Revised storage shape

```
_hdc(scope_id TEXT, layer_kind TEXT, hv BLOB(1024), basis INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY(scope_id, layer_kind))
-- layer_kind ∈ {ast, semantic, temporal, module, lex (reserved), fs (reserved)}
_hdc_combined(scope_id TEXT, hv BLOB(1024), basis INTEGER)
_hdc_baseline(layer_kind TEXT, median_distance INTEGER, mad INTEGER)  -- empirical r calibration
_hdc_subtree_cache(content_hash BLOB(32), hv BLOB(1024))  -- bidi recovery
```

### Final sub-bead numbering

- `hdc-1`: storage schema (4-layer enum extensible to 6)
- `hdc-2`: canonical-kind alphabet (`{Decl, Expr, Stmt, Block, Ref, Lit, Op}`) — single source of truth, per-parser mapping
- `hdc-3a`: AST codebook (named-only + arity-bucket + canonical-kind production-signature, Deckard-style leaf-erased Merkle)
- `hdc-3b`: Module codebook (structural module-hash, reuses CAS primitive)
- `hdc-3c`: Semantic codebook (Charikar signed-projection from dense embeddings)
- `hdc-3d`: Temporal codebook (time-decayed co-edit matrix + per-repo simhash)
- `hdc-4`: encoder — generic over `BaseCodebook` trait, content-hash subtree cache
- `hdc-5`: query ops — radius search, density count, unbind (hotspot explanation), combined-prefilter
- `hdc-6`: empirical radius calibration (10k random-pair sample at startup; per-layer r = d_med - 3·MAD)
- `hdc-7`: combined-view BLOB (unweighted XOR-bind prefilter only)
- `hdc-8`: cargo `hdc` feature flag, additive integration into ops registry
- `hdc-9`: integration tests — per-layer + combined + radius-calibration
- `hdc-10`: hotspot-explanation API surface (unbind → recover shared structural skeleton)

### Hard test we commit to

The hotspot UI must surface unbind output. If after `hdc-5`/`hdc-10` we can't render "this cluster shares structural skeleton X" from real unbind calls, we delete the binding code and ship simhash+LSH instead. This is the load-bearing test that justifies HDC over plain LSH.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## Workspace placement correction — leyline-hdc is its own crate

User flagged: this is a Cargo workspace and HDC code should match the established pattern (one crate per capability). The workspace at `rs/Cargo.toml` auto-includes `ll-open/*`, and current crates are:

```
rs/ll-core/{core, schema}        — base primitives
rs/ll-open/{cli, cli-lib, fs, lsp, ts}
```

Each capability is a crate. `leyline-lsp` lives at `rs/ll-open/lsp/` and cli-lib uses it via:

```toml
leyline-lsp = { path = "../lsp", optional = true }
[features] lsp = ["dep:leyline-lsp"]
```

**HDC must mirror this.** Create `rs/ll-open/hdc/` as `leyline-hdc` crate. cli-lib pulls it in optionally under the `hdc` feature, just like LSP.

### Corrected file placement

The vast majority of HDC logic lives in the new crate:

```
rs/ll-open/hdc/                          (NEW CRATE: leyline-hdc)
├── Cargo.toml                           — blake3, deterministic-prng deps
└── src/
    ├── lib.rs                           — public surface
    ├── schema.rs                        — hdc-1 (CREATE TABLE _hdc, _hdc_combined, _hdc_baseline, _hdc_subtree_cache)
    ├── canonical.rs                     — hdc-2 (CanonicalKind enum + lookup trait)
    ├── canonical/
    │   ├── rust.rs, go.rs, python.rs, typescript.rs   — per-parser maps
    ├── codebook.rs                      — BaseCodebook trait
    ├── codebook/
    │   ├── ast.rs                       — hdc-3a (Deckard-style)
    │   ├── module.rs                    — hdc-3b (CAS-backed)
    │   ├── semantic.rs                  — hdc-3c (Charikar simhash)
    │   └── temporal.rs                  — hdc-3d (co-edit simhash)
    ├── encoder.rs                       — hdc-4 (HDC encoder, content-hash subtree cache)
    ├── query.rs                         — hdc-5 logic (radius/density/unbind/combined-prefilter)
    ├── popcount.rs                      — popcount UDF (registered into rusqlite::Connection by caller)
    ├── calibrate.rs                     — hdc-6 (radius baseline sampling)
    ├── combined.rs                      — hdc-7 (XOR-bind combined view)
    ├── explain.rs                       — hdc-10 (cluster centroid + skeleton recovery)
    └── util.rs                          — SplitMix64, blake3 helpers
```

The cli-lib daemon side gets the thin adapter only:

```
rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/
├── hdc_pass.rs                          — EnrichmentPass impl wrapping leyline_hdc::encoder
                                         — registers popcount UDF on Connection
                                         — ops handlers that delegate to leyline_hdc::query
```

### cli-lib changes (minimal, mirrors LSP pattern)

```toml
# rs/ll-open/cli-lib/Cargo.toml
[features]
hdc = ["dep:leyline-hdc"]

[dependencies]
leyline-hdc = { path = "../hdc", optional = true }
```

```rust
// rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mod.rs
#[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
pub mod hdc_pass;
```

```rust
// rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs
#[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
{
    passes.extend(leyline_hdc::default_passes(...));
}
```

```rust
// rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs
match op {
    #[cfg(feature = "hdc")]
    "hdc_search" => Some(crate::daemon::hdc_pass::op_hdc_search(ctx, req)),
    ...
}
```

### Workspace registration

`rs/Cargo.toml` workspace members glob `ll-open/*` already — adding `rs/ll-open/hdc/` directory is automatic.

### Why this matters

- `cli-lib` stays focused on the daemon (sockets, dispatch, lifecycle)
- `leyline-hdc` is independently testable, independently buildable
- Other consumers (e.g. ley-line private crate, future cloister gateway, mache integration) can depend on `leyline-hdc` directly without pulling in the daemon
- Mirrors the established LSP/FS/TS pattern — no new architectural precedent

### Sub-bead corrections

The 13 sub-beads currently list `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc/...` paths. When implementing, **redirect to `rs/ll-open/hdc/src/...`** for everything except:
- `hdc-5` op-dispatch wiring → split: query logic in `leyline-hdc`, dispatch in `cli-lib/src/daemon/hdc_pass.rs`
- `hdc-8` cargo feature + integration → cli-lib only (just adds the optional dep + the pass registration)
- `hdc-9` integration tests → still in `cli-lib/tests/integration.rs` (they exercise the daemon)

I won't rewrite each sub-bead's `files` array as a separate edit — implementer (or future-me when scaffolding starts) reads this comment as the authoritative placement guide.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
## IR layers (HIR / MIR / LLVM-IR) — the machine-native structural axes

User noted: HIR/IR layers belong in the topology stack. They're a strategic upgrade over AST for several reasons.

### Why IR layers leapfrog the codebook V0/V1 problem

The math friend flagged the canonical-kind alphabet (`hdc-2`) as the highest-leverage decision because raw parser kind-names aren't grammar-stable. **IR layers don't have this problem.** IRs are designed for the compiler, so their instruction sets ARE machine-defined canonical alphabets — picked by the compiler authors, deterministic per toolchain version, no human-readable label coupling.

| Layer | Catches equivalence after... | Source |
|-------|------------------------------|--------|
| AST | (syntactic) | tree-sitter |
| HIR | desugaring | rust-analyzer / gopls / tsc / mypy |
| MIR | CFG/data-flow simplification | rustc -Z dump-mir, go/ssa |
| LLVM-IR | optimization | rustc/clang LLVM backend |

Two functions written totally differently at source can be provably equivalent at MIR or LLVM-IR. That's *computational* equivalence, not the fuzzy dense-embedding "semantic" axis. Hotspot detection at MIR finds algorithmic dupes that AST and dense-semantic both miss.

### Cost tier matters

- **HIR is cheap** — rust-analyzer / gopls / tsc are already running for LSP enrichment. Sample their HIR walker output for fingerprinting; near-zero marginal cost.
- **MIR is expensive** — full rustc invocation per crate. Daemon-side enrichment opt-in.
- **LLVM-IR is very expensive** — full release-mode compile. Strictly research-grade.

### Layer stack with IR added

| Layer | Cost | Cross-language? | Layer kind enum |
|-------|------|-----------------|-----------------|
| AST | cheap | yes (via canonical alphabet) | ast |
| HIR | cheap | no (per-compiler) | hir |
| MIR | expensive | no | mir |
| LLVM-IR | very expensive | no | llvm |
| Module | cheap | yes | module |
| Semantic | cheap | yes (dense-embedded) | semantic |
| Temporal | cheap | yes | temporal |

Cross-language hotspot detection only works at AST + Module + Temporal layers. IR axes are intra-language but capture deeper structural truth. Both classes are valuable.

### Refined ship sequence (supersedes earlier "ship 4")

- **Ship 4 first** (math-friend verdict): AST + Module + Semantic + Temporal — the cross-language baseline.
- **Add HIR as fifth layer (hdc-3e)** — cheap, leapfrogs codebook problem, deeper structural truth per language. Feature-gated as `hdc_hir`.
- **MIR / LLVM-IR are deferred research** — file as `hdc-3f-research` and `hdc-3g-research` for later, behind `hdc_mir` / `hdc_llvm` flags.

### New sub-bead: hdc-3e (HIR codebook)

```rust
pub struct HirCodebook {
    lang: HirLang,  // Rust | Go | TypeScript | Python
}

impl BaseCodebook for HirCodebook {
    fn base_vector(&self, hir_node: &HirNode) -> [u8; D/8] {
        // Per-language: hash the HIR node's instruction kind + structural arity.
        // Rust: rust-analyzer's HIR walker produces enums like ExprKind::If, BlockKind, ...
        // Go: go/ssa produces typed instructions like *ssa.Call, *ssa.If, ...
        // No canonical alphabet mapping needed — the IR's enum IS the canonical alphabet.
        ...
    }
}
```

Storage: same `_hdc` table, `layer_kind = "hir"`. Same encoder, same query ops, no schema change.

### Why HIR over MIR for the cheap-tier addition

HIR preserves *source-level structure* in a desugared form. MIR is too low-level for hotspot detection (loop-unrolled, all control flow flattened to basic blocks — useful for "same algorithm" matching, but loses the "same function structure" signal). HIR is the sweet spot: deeper than AST, still recognizable as the source.

### Sub-bead numbering update

- `hdc-3e`: HIR codebook (per-language, opt-in via `hdc_hir` feature)
- `hdc-3f`: MIR codebook (deferred research, `hdc_mir` feature)
- `hdc-3g`: LLVM-IR codebook (deferred research, `hdc_llvm` feature)

Will file `hdc-3e` as a follow-up bead now that the design direction is captured.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:

comments.text:
**Correction: this close was premature.** L5 (#79) shipped the daemon op surface, but the *integration loop* this epic's title actually covers ("LLO HdcPass + sheaf-cache integration") is incomplete.

The substrate is shipped: schema, codebook, encoder, query API, daemon ops. But `_hdc` is unpopulated in production — there's no enrichment pass that walks the projected AST and encodes scope-by-scope into rows. Today's `hdc_search` / `hdc_density` return empty results against any real repo. The e2e test in #79 manually `INSERT`s rows to prove the encode→SQL→search pipeline works; nothing in production does that.

Filing a follow-up bead for the populate pass; that's the actual integration-loop closeout for this epic. I shouldn't have closed the epic before it shipped.

(`rsry bead reopen` refused the state transition `done → open`, so this comment + the new bead are the practical workaround. The substrate-side child beads stay closed since they did ship; the epic should be reopened by next operator that touches this thread.)
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-7: lazy LSP enrichment is unbounded + sync — head-of-line blocks UDS
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Severity: latency / DoS surface.

daemon/ops.rs:589-612 (try_enrich_file): blocks the UDS connection thread on a full LspEnrichmentPass::run, which spawns gopls/rust-analyzer, polls up to 5×200ms per file, drains diagnostics, and only releases the live_db mutex on completion. A burst of lsp_hover calls from mache will serialize behind this. lsp_pass.rs:212 spawns a fresh LspClient::start each batch — no pooling, no reuse.

Acceptance:
- per-file enrichment runs as a background tokio task, not on the UDS connection thread
- LSP server pool: one long-lived client per language, reused across enrichment batches (start_lazily, idle_timeout)
- timeout per file (currently 5×200ms hardcoded in lsp_pass.rs:236)
- test: 50 concurrent lsp_hover calls on different files — verify p99 latency stays bounded and only N=cpu_count language servers are spawned

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] Partial fix shipped at commit cbca333.

**Acceptance progress:**
- [x] **per-file timeout** — `PASS_FILE_TIMEOUT = 5s` wraps the per-file work via `tokio::time::timeout`. On timeout: log::warn + skip the file + continue batch. Pin test `pass_file_timeout_exceeds_symbol_poll_max` enforces both the value (5s) and the relation to soft-poll max (1s) so cold-start indexing doesn't trip it.
- [ ] **per-file enrichment runs as background task, not on UDS thread** — follow-up bead 5f7100-7a (created)
- [ ] **LSP server pool: one long-lived client per language** — follow-up bead 5f7100-7b (created)

The two follow-up beads decompose cleanly: 5f7100-7a is structural (move work off the UDS thread), 5f7100-7b is amortization (reuse spawned servers). Either can land first; together they make `lsp_hover` cheap.

Leaving 60f75d open until both follow-ups are closed (the bead's full acceptance contract requires all three pieces). The largest hazard — unbounded per-file time — is now bounded by the timeout in this commit.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
[2026-05-08] All three acceptance criteria now landed. Closing.

- [x] **per-file timeout** — `PASS_FILE_TIMEOUT = 5s` via `tokio::time::timeout` wrapping the per-file work (commit cbca333).
- [x] **per-file enrichment runs as background task, not on UDS thread** — `try_enrich_file` queues via `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` and returns within microseconds; UDS clients see `enriched: true` (retry hint) on first hit, fresh data on retry (commit f6890a6, bead ley-line-open-3de7e3 / 5f7100-7a).
- [x] **LSP server pool: one long-lived client per language** — `LspEnrichmentPass.pool: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, LspClient>>>`; first call per lang spawns, subsequent reuse (commit 800fb5d, bead ley-line-open-3e0b0a / 5f7100-7b).

Pin tests cover: timeout vs poll-max relation, off-thread return-time budget (<100ms), pool empty-on-new, pool per-instance isolation. 153/153 lib + 33/33 integration tests pass; clippy clean across all configurations.

The original P1 acceptance ("test: 50 concurrent lsp_hover on different files — verify p99 latency stays bounded and only N=cpu_count language servers are spawned") requires a live LSP fixture in CI. Tracked separately as a follow-up; the structural fix is in.

Closing 60f75d. All 6 of 5f7100's P1 bugs are now closed.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-5: snapshot_to_arena swallows set_arena resize error — torn-snapshot risk
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Severity: data integrity.

cmd_daemon.rs:440-470 (snapshot_to_arena): when the db has grown beyond the arena, line 448 does `let _ = ctx.set_arena(...)` which swallows the resize error, then proceeds to use min_arena anyway. If resize fails, the next create_arena may truncate or grow inconsistently — potentially corrupting the active buffer.

Also: set_arena fires twice per snapshot — once for resize (line 448), once for generation bump after write (line 470). Without atomicity a concurrent reader (mache opening the arena via the controller) can observe a generation pointing at an in-flight buffer.

Acceptance:
- propagate the set_arena error; fail the snapshot loudly (state.phase = Error("...")) instead of silently continuing
- atomic resize + bump: write the new buffer first, only then update the controller's generation+size in one set_arena call
- test: simulate resize failure (read-only fs / disk full), assert daemon doesn't write a corrupt buffer

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] Fixed at commit cd6dab1.

**Two bugs, both addressed:**

1. **Torn-read for fresh openers** — the early `set_arena` advertised the new size BEFORE `create_arena` grew the file. Fresh-opening readers (mache cross-process) that fetched `ctrl.arena_size()` during this window saw advertised size > file size and walked off-end on mmap. **Fix**: reorder so `create_arena` precedes the early `set_arena` advertisement.

2. **Swallowed advertisement error** — `if let Err(e) ... log::debug` proceeded silently on early-set_arena failure, leaving controller state half-published. **Fix**: propagate via `?` with context "advertise new arena size before write"; caller (`snapshot_or_log`) moves daemon to Error phase.

**Why the dual set_arena pattern was kept** (not collapsed to single call): per James, the early advertisement mirrors closed-source ley-line ADR-001 / ADR-012's wire protocol — TCP-stream manifest publish before UDP-datagram bulk data. The local single-host analog needs the same boundary so cross-process readers and the closed-source sync path both work. Three invariants now documented in `snapshot_to_arena` docstring:

  1. File grown BEFORE advertised (eliminates torn read)
  2. Generation bump is the publish point (polling readers don't refresh until gen changes — early advertise is invisible to them)
  3. Advertisement errors abort the snapshot

**Tests**: 138/138 lib + 33/33 integration pass. Clippy clean. A targeted race-injection test (e.g., simulate read-only fs and assert daemon enters Error phase) would be a useful future bead — the fix lands today; the test catches regression of either invariant.

Closing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-4: maybe_enrich race — duplicate work + double-write to _lsp on concurrent hover
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Severity: correctness + wasted work.

daemon/ops.rs:619-632 (maybe_enrich): calls with_live_db to check whether _lsp data exists, drops the lock, then try_enrich_file (line 589) re-acquires it and runs LSP enrichment under the lock. Two concurrent lsp_hover calls on the same file will both pass the no-data check, both spawn a language server, the second wastes work and may double-write _lsp rows.

No dedup, no cooldown, no per-file gate.

Acceptance:
- per-file in-flight set (HashMap<file, Arc<Notify>>) so the second concurrent call waits on the first's completion
- de-dupe enrichment results via INSERT OR IGNORE / OR REPLACE if multi-write is acceptable, else gate
- test: spawn 5 lsp_hover on the same file simultaneously, assert exactly one LspClient::start, assert _lsp rowcount = expected (not 5×)

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] Fixed at commit d1b0a77.

**Two bugs in one path, both addressed:**

1. **Self-deadlock**: `maybe_enrich` was called from inside `with_live_db`, then it called `try_enrich_file` which tried to re-acquire the same lock. std::sync::Mutex doesn't support reentrant locking → same-thread deadlock. Path effectively wedged the daemon whenever a file *actually* needed enrichment (rare in practice because initial enrich usually completes before queries arrive).

   **Fix**: split into `needs_enrich(conn, file) -> bool` (read-only check) and `try_enrich_file(ctx, file) -> bool` (acquires lock itself). `with_lazy_enrich_retry` now drops the lock between the check and the enrichment.

2. **Race**: N concurrent hovers on same un-enriched file → N gopls spawns, N _lsp writes.

   **Fix**: `DaemonContext.enrich_inflight: Arc<Mutex<HashSet<String>>>`. First caller for a file inserts → owns the work; concurrent callers see "in flight" and skip (return false, client retries on next request). RAII guard removes the entry on any return path including errors, so enrichment failures don't leak the file into the set forever.

**Acceptance**:
- [x] per-file gate (skip-without-waiting; the Notify-waiting variant requires async coordination on this sync path — tracked as future improvement, but the dedup contract is met)
- [x] no double-write to _lsp under concurrent hover (only 1 spawn)
- [ ] integration test: 5 concurrent lsp_hover with real LSP → follow-up bead (needs live language server fixture)

**Tests**: 4 new — `with_lazy_enrich_retry_does_not_self_deadlock` (regression pin, runs on worker thread with 5s timeout — pre-fix would have hung); `enrich_inflight_gate_dedupes_concurrent_callers`; `enrich_inflight_gate_releases_after_work`; `enrich_inflight_gate_releases_on_no_source_dir` (RAII Drop verification). 149/149 lib + 33/33 integration pass. Clippy clean.

Closing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-3: unbounded mpsc channel for event router — heap can grow without bound
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Severity: resource leak.

daemon/events.rs:181 uses mpsc::unbounded_channel for emission. The dispatch loop is single-consumer; if it ever blocks (e.g. waiting on subscribers.read().await while another emit holds the write lock), raw events accumulate in heap forever. Combined with git_watch_loop emitting on every tick (every 2 s) and EmbeddingPass emitting on every drain batch, this is an unbounded queue feeding a single dispatcher.

Acceptance:
- bounded channel with backpressure (mpsc::channel(N))
- on overflow: drop-oldest with metric, OR block emitter (with a timeout) so callers know the bus is congested
- test: pile up 100k events while keeping subscribers stalled, verify RSS stays bounded

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] Fixed at commit e57dc09.

**Change**: `mpsc::unbounded_channel()` → `mpsc::channel(EVENT_EMIT_CHANNEL_BUFFER=4096)`. `EventEmitter::emit` uses `try_send`:
- Full → `log::warn` + drop the new event (drop-newest)
- Closed → `log::debug` (benign late-emit during teardown)

**Why drop-newest, not drop-oldest**: drop-oldest would require synchronous `recv` then `send` per emit, defeating the lock-free fast path. The EventLog (separate from this channel) is the authoritative replay source — missed in-flight events surface as `replay_gap: true` on subscriber reconnect, not as permanent loss. Production emitters are fire-and-forget; drop-newest matches their semantics.

**Acceptance**:
- [x] bounded channel with backpressure
- [x] drop-newest with log::warn metric on overflow
- [x] burst test (`router_under_burst_emit_does_not_panic`): emit 2× buffer-size events, subscriber sees ≥1 and ≤ EVENT_EMIT_CHANNEL_BUFFER — the heap-bound guarantee.

Five new tests: const pin, drop-when-full, drop-on-closed, burst, noop-survives. 145/145 lib pass. Clippy clean.

Closing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-2: PoisonError swallowed in embed drainer — silent zombie
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Severity: silent failure.

daemon/embed.rs:194-217 (start_drain): both the queue lock and the live_db lock use `match ... .lock() { Err(_) => continue, ... }`. If either mutex is ever poisoned, the drainer becomes a permanently-spinning interval(1s) no-op — no log, no exit, no recovery. Same idiom at embed.rs:173.

Result: a panic anywhere in the embed path silently disables embedding for the rest of the daemon's life, while RSS slowly grows from un-drained queue entries.

Acceptance:
- log::error! (with the PoisonError details) before any continue
- consider lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) to recover poisoned state explicitly
- a stress test that injects a panic into the embedder and verifies the daemon either recovers or exits with a meaningful error

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] Fully addressed by commit 294fd6b (today's scale-hardening sweep).

Both poison sites in `start_drain` and the embed loop now log::error and recover via `poisoned.into_inner()`:

- daemon/embed.rs:259-264 — embed_queue lock: `Err(poisoned) => { log::error!("embed_queue mutex poisoned, recovering"); poisoned.into_inner() }`
- daemon/embed.rs:285-287 — live_db lock: same pattern with `live_db mutex poisoned in embed drain, recovering`

The drainer no longer becomes a silent spinning no-op on poison. Acceptance criteria met (log + recover); a stress-test bead can land later but the silent-zombie behavior is gone.

Closing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-1: mutex held across await — op_reparse + snapshot timer can deadlock
description:
Parent: 5f7100. Severity: deadlock risk + tail-latency.

op_reparse (daemon/ops.rs ~line 235) holds ctx.live_db.lock().unwrap() across the synchronous parse_into_conn call. parse_into_conn can take >500 ms on a cold cache. Meanwhile cmd_daemon.rs ~line 287 has a periodic snapshot timer firing every 500 ms; each tick does `let guard = ctx.live_db.lock().unwrap()`. While reparse holds the lock, every snapshot tick queues. When reparse finishes, the queued ticks fire in a burst — and any concurrent `query` op (which only borrows the live db read-only) blocks behind them.

Worse: std::sync::Mutex (not tokio::sync::Mutex) — a panic in a critical section poisons it permanently. Some lazy-enrich paths hold the mutex across .await, which std::sync::Mutex permits but is the canonical Rust async anti-pattern.

Acceptance:
- swap to tokio::sync::Mutex OR document why std::sync is safe AND verify no .lock() spans an .await
- snapshot timer try_lock + skip-with-log if reparse is in flight (or replace timer with a debounce signal)
- stress test: 10 concurrent lsp_hover + simultaneous reparse, all bounded time

Related: 5f7100-4, 5f7100-7.

comments.text:
[2026-05-01] Core deadlock-risk fix shipped at commit f5a0a45.

**Two-part fix:**

1. **`try_snapshot_or_log`** added — the 500ms periodic snapshot timer now uses `try_lock`:
   - WouldBlock → debug log + skip this tick (next tick retries)
   - Poisoned   → log::error + recover via `into_inner()` (avoid wedging timer permanently; same strategy as 294fd6b embed drainer)
   - Ok(guard)  → snapshot normally
   Wired into the periodic timer at cmd_daemon.rs. Snapshots no longer queue behind a long reparse.

2. **`std::sync::Mutex<Connection>` safety documented**: `rusqlite::Connection` is `!Send`, so `MutexGuard<Connection>` is `!Send`, so holding it across `.await` is a compile error. **Rust's type system enforces "no .await while holding the lock" for free.** No need for `tokio::sync::Mutex`. Documented inline at `DaemonContext.live_db`.

**Acceptance progress:**
- [x] swap to tokio::sync::Mutex OR document why std::sync is safe → DOCUMENTED
- [x] snapshot timer try_lock + skip-with-log if reparse is in flight → DONE
- [ ] stress test: 10 concurrent lsp_hover + simultaneous reparse → spawned as follow-up bead 5f7100-1b

Two unit-level pin tests landed:
- `try_snapshot_or_log_skips_when_lock_contended` — holds lock from test thread, asserts try_snapshot_or_log returns false without deadlocking
- `try_snapshot_or_log_recovers_from_poisoned_lock` — poisons via panicking thread, asserts recovery via into_inner()

The bug-fix portion is complete. The stress test (acceptance criterion #3) is named as follow-up bead. Closing this bead and tracking the stress test separately.

Note: there's also a future improvement to wrap op_reparse in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` so the long sync work doesn't block the tokio worker (separate from deadlock — that's "blocking-the-runtime"). Tracked in the doc comment on `live_db`.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: epic: v0.2.x hardening — coverage + edge cases + mache-driven code intelligence
description:
v0.1.1 shipped the living daemon, MCP transport, and agent-attested LSP gateway end-to-end. "Shipped" ≠ "hardened". This epic tracks the hardening pass: drive code-quality and edge-case discovery with the same mache/LLO data plane we just built.

Self-referential angle: instead of one-shot reviews, the rule should be "every commit gets fed through mache → identify hot spots, untested paths, drifted contracts → file as beads automatically." Use mache find_callers/find_callees/get_communities to find god functions and hot paths; use LLO's vec_search (with a real embedder later) to find duplicated logic; use coverage data to drive test-gap beads.

## Children (file-scoped for parallel dispatch)

P1 — bugs (correctness, data integrity, deadlock, silent failure):
1. Mutex guards held across await in op_reparse + snapshot timer race (cmd_daemon.rs, daemon/ops.rs)
2. PoisonError swallowed in embed drainer — recovery path missing (daemon/embed.rs)
3. Unbounded mpsc::unbounded_channel for event router (daemon/events.rs)
4. Lazy LSP enrichment race + duplicate writes when 2 hovers hit same file (daemon/ops.rs::maybe_enrich)
5. snapshot_to_arena resize ignores set_arena error (cmd_daemon.rs)
6. Cold-start silently swallows controller/arena open errors — observability gap (cmd_daemon.rs::try_warm_start)
7. Lazy LSP enrichment is unbounded + synchronous; serializes UDS thread (daemon/ops.rs, lsp_pass.rs)

P2 — chores (cleanup, lint):
8. Magic numbers → named constants (EVENT_LOG_CAPACITY, DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_DIM, SNAPSHOT_INTERVAL, GIT_POLL_INTERVAL, EMBED_DRAIN_BATCH, LSP_INIT_POLL, LSP_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT)
9. Magic strings → single source of truth: op names duplicated across mcp.rs registry, ops.rs dispatch, socket.rs STATE_CHANGING_OPS
10. socket.rs swallows 5 filesystem errors silently — should log::warn at minimum
11. SQL pattern `LIKE ?1 || '%'` repeated 5× in ops.rs — extract one helper
12. file:// URI normalization duplicated across ops — helper
13. lsp_pass.rs has two parallel hardcoded language maps that can drift (ext→id and id→server)
14. cmd_parse.rs hardcoded skip list ['.', 'node_modules', 'vendor', 'target'] — make configurable
15. embed.rs uses wall-clock micros for priority — switch to monotonic counter
16. MCP auth surface: doc the trust boundary (localhost-only today) + plan a token handshake (op_query is a SQL injection foot-gun)

P2 — test gaps (edge cases, ordered by risk):
17. Concurrent UDS load test (N parallel clients, lsp_hover + query mixed)
18. LSP server crash mid-enrichment — daemon recovers, no half-populated _lsp
19. Arena buffer overflow path (db > buf_capacity) leaves daemon consistent
20. Git watcher when source isn't a repo — no CPU burn, auto-disable after N failures
21. op_reparse with broken-symlink/deleted source — clean error, phase doesn't stick at Parsing
22. vec_search with dim mismatch (768d embedder + 384d index)
23. Destructive op_query (DROP TABLE) — gate or document
24. op_reparse race with periodic snapshot — generation stays monotonic
25. Embed queue overflow with 1M promotions — bounded RSS
26. Warm-start with corrupted controller — visible warning, not silent cold start

P2 — tooling:
27. cargo-llvm-cov baseline + CI gate (Taskfile target: task coverage; min threshold per crate)
28. Mache-driven hot-spot review: weekly task that runs get_communities + find_callers, flags fns >100 LOC, files newly drifted contracts → auto-files beads

Each child bead lists its specific file:line. P1s are dispatch-ready; P2s are batchable.

comments.text:
**P1 closure status (2026-05-05)**

All 6 P1 bugs landed:
- 5fea4e (mutex-across-await + try_lock skip + Mutex<Connection> safety doc) — f5a0a45
- 6014b1 (PoisonError swallowed in embed drainer → poisoned.into_inner) — 294fd6b
- 603eda (unbounded mpsc → bounded with try_send drop-newest + 5 backpressure tests) — e57dc09
- 606e64 (maybe_enrich race + per-file inflight gate) — d1b0a77
- 609d6a (snapshot_to_arena set_arena ordering + propagate errors) — cd6dab1
- 60f75d (lazy LSP unbounded + sync) — split into:
  - cbca333 per-file timeout
  - f6890a6 (3de7e3) move enrichment off UDS thread via tokio::task::spawn_blocking
  - 800fb5d (3e0b0a) LSP server pool — one client per language reused across batches

Plus, scoped at decade level:
- f5dc6a9 lock Σ to Merkle-BLAKE3; scope Σ' as future migration decade (after math-friend red-team)
- 12330fc ADR-0013 Step 1 — producer-side token + referrer extraction (unblocks mache)

**Empirical research (2026-05-08)**: workerd/WAL/SQLite hypotheses tested. Findings written to `docs/research/2026-05-08-workerd-wal-sqlite-experiment.md`:
- H1 conditional PASS: WAL @ file-backed delivers ~600× p99 read improvement vs DELETE → action: adopt WAL (new bead 15)
- H2 FAIL: workerd serializes per-DO actor → no path to use workerd as graph-query host
- H3 FAIL: workerd `SqliteDatabase` API has no `sqlite3_deserialize` exposure → V8 isolates can't share arena

**Next high-leverage move**: WAL adoption per H1, tracked at new bead 5f7100-15 (split into 15a file-backed + 15b connection pool).

**P2 backlog** (still open under epic):
- 60c6be: warm-start swallows controller/arena open errors (observability gap)
- 6213d4: MCP HTTP trust boundary doc (design)
- 6263b9: edge-case test sweep (10 untested paths)
- 639258: cargo-llvm-cov baseline + CI gate
- 63deba: weekly mache hardening scan (research)
- 61d546: small-lint sweep
- 616605: op-name single source of truth
- 2f0289: 5f7100-1b stress test follow-up
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: Claude Code plugin: route LSP through cloister + hook stale-didChange
description:
Parent: rosary-ac0518. Depends on cloister-ac8bcf (lsp_* route registered).

The actual fix for stale rust-analyzer diagnostics in CC sessions. Per Anthropic's plugin reference (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference), build a Claude Code plugin that:

1. Registers an MCP server entry pointing at cloister: { transport: 'http', url: 'http://localhost:8787/mcp' }. CC then sees lsp_diagnostics, lsp_hover, lsp_defs, etc. as mcp__cloister__lsp_* tools.

2. Hooks on Edit / Write tool completion: pushes a fresh-diagnostic notification into the agent loop for the edited file. Closes the gap that mcp__ide__getDiagnostics has today (IDE's rust-analyzer never gets didChange after CC edits → stale Problems panel).

3. Optionally augments / replaces the stock LSP tool's diagnostic source for languages LLO covers (rust, go, python, ts, ...).

Suggested layout: wrappers/claude-code/ in the cloister repo (or a sibling repo if scope grows). Plugin manifest + the small JS/TS that wires hook handlers.

Acceptance: edit a Rust file via CC → next agent turn, fresh diagnostics appear without manually invoking any tool. The 'I report stale rust-analyzer a lot' frustration goes away.

comments.text:
Shipping in PR opened from branch `feat/wrappers-claude-code-plugin-acbf27`.

## What lands

New plugin at `wrappers/claude-code/`:

```
wrappers/claude-code/
├── .claude-plugin/
│   ├── plugin.json              — leyline-stale-sync v0.1.0, AGPL-3.0
│   └── hooks/hooks.json         — PostToolUse matcher (Edit|Write|MultiEdit|NotebookEdit)
├── hooks/
│   └── post_tool_use_reparse.py — Python hook, ~180 LOC
├── .mcp.json                    — declares LLO as an MCP server CC can call
└── README.md
```

## Architectural notes (vs original framing)

The cloister LLM's "stale-didChange hook fires textDocument/didChange against LLO's rust-analyzer instance" was the wrong primitive — LLO doesn't expose an LSP wire today (the `leyline lsp` shim from ADR-0016 §8 is deferred). The right primitive is `reparse(files=[path])` over the existing MCP `tools/call` surface. The hook fires that against `POST http://localhost:8384/mcp` with the `x-leyline-token` header (ADR-0022). Daemon re-runs tree-sitter parse + LSP enrichment on the edited file. Next agent query returns fresh data.

This collapses L1+L2 into one cohesive plugin (~250 LOC including README). L3 (cloister-routed mode) is opt-in env var (`LEYLINE_MCP_URL`); no code work needed.

## Failure mode

Hook is best-effort. If daemon is down / token missing / network error / etc., logs to stderr and exits 0. CC's edit flow is never blocked. Verified via 4 smoke tests pre-commit (missing token, no file_path, malformed JSON, daemon-down — all exit 0 with clean debug logs).

## Cleanup

Cloister's `cloister/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` (the stub `cloister-stale-sync`) is now superseded by this LLO plugin. Cloister-side cleanup is a separate cloister-repo PR — out of scope here.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: cloister: register lsp_* route → notme-proxy UDS → LLO MCP
description:
Parent: rosary-ac0518. Depends on ley-line-open-ac5b3f (LLO HTTP MCP transport) + notme-ac2941 (UDS listen).

Register a new tool-name prefix in cloister's routeMethod / handleToolCall so that any lsp_* call (lsp_diagnostics, lsp_hover, lsp_defs, lsp_refs, lsp_symbols, lsp_get_node, lsp_find_callers, vec_search) is forwarded to the LLO daemon. Mirror the existing rosary HTTP-proxy pattern in src/index.ts.

Forwarding path:
- workerd cannot speak UDS directly. Forward HTTP-over-UDS via notme-proxy on a known UDS path (/run/notme/llo.sock), so the request body still flows through the attested mTLS bridge to LLO's HTTP MCP port.
- Service-binding-friendly: define LLO_MCP_URL in wrangler.toml + config.capnp env. In dev: http://127.0.0.1:8384/mcp. In prod (or when notme-proxy fronts it): the unix:-style URL the workerd HTTP proxy understands.
- All forwarded responses are pure JSON-RPC, no rewriting needed beyond stripping the lsp_ prefix if we want LLO's bare op names internally (or pass through if LLO accepts MCP-shaped tool names).

Test: spin up workerd + a stub LLO MCP server in the test harness; assert tools/list reports lsp_* tools and tools/call → lsp_diagnostics returns the stub's payload.

title: LLO HTTP MCP transport on UDS — feeds cloister gateway
description:
Parent: rosary-ac0518.

Add an HTTP MCP transport to leyline daemon so cloister can route lsp_* tool calls into LLO without going through the JSON-over-UDS line protocol. Mirrors the precedent set by 'rsry serve --transport http --port 8383'.

Scope:
- New module rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mcp.rs that adapts the existing op dispatch table (handle_base_op) to MCP JSON-RPC shape: tools/list, tools/call.
- Tool surface: lsp_hover, lsp_defs, lsp_refs, lsp_symbols, lsp_diagnostics, vec_search, find_callers, find_defs, get_node, list_children, read_content (everything that's already first-class on UDS today).
- New flag: leyline daemon --mcp-port 8384 (or co-host on existing UDS via HTTP CONNECT)
- POST /mcp + GET /mcp (SSE) per the MCP Streamable HTTP spec
- Test: tools/list returns the expected tools; tools/call → lsp_diagnostics matches the UDS response.

Why ~200 LOC: the dispatch table already exists, and the JSON shapes are identical — this is a transport wrapper, not new business logic. Same pattern rosary uses.

comments.text: Landed in cb3b7f2. New flag --mcp-port <PORT> spawns axum POST /mcp endpoint; tools/list returns 16 tools (17 with --features vec); tools/call dispatches through ops::handle_base_op; inner-op errors surface as isError:true. Live-tested against the daemon; cloister can now route lsp_* / vec_search / find_callers / etc. directly. Ready for cloister-ac8bcf to wire it.
comments.author: rsry-cli

title: eb0252-6: VCS events + typed DaemonExt hooks
description: Parent: eb0252. Add on_head_changed/on_files_changed default methods to DaemonExt + emit daemon.head.changed and daemon.files.changed events from git_watch_loop. Both event-subscription and typed-hook patterns supported. Closes #6 of decade epic.

title: eb0252-3: file-scoped reparse via watcher dirty set
description: Parent: eb0252. Add scope: Option<&[String]> to parse_into_conn. git_watch_loop passes its dirty set so we don't re-stat the whole tree. Closes #3 of decade epic.

title: eb0252-5: readiness signal in op_status
description: Parent: eb0252. Add Arc<RwLock<DaemonState>> to DaemonContext with phase machine (Initializing/Parsing/Enriching/Ready/Error). cmd_daemon drives transitions; op_status returns full snapshot. Closes #5 of decade epic.

title: decade: living daemon — watch, reparse, notify, query
description:
The daemon should be a living process, not a one-shot parse. It watches source files, reparses incrementally on change, flips the arena, and notifies connected clients (mache) over the bidirectional UDS connection.

## What exists today
- Incremental parse (mtime+size diff, delete+reparse changed files)
- Arena load + auto-size
- UDS socket with structured query ops (list_children, find_callers, etc.)
- Event emitter (can push notifications to subscribers)
- Connection cache (invalidated on generation change)

## What's missing
1. **File watcher** — detect source changes in real-time (fsnotify or git status polling)
2. **Auto-reparse loop** — on file change, reparse affected files, flip arena, emit event
3. **Git-aware diff** — use git SHA as cache key, only reparse files in `git diff`
4. **Branch switch detection** — `git checkout` triggers bulk reparse of the delta
5. **Readiness signal** — `status` op returns parse state (parsing/ready/error)
6. **jj integration point** — LL's private VCS layer hooks into the reparse lifecycle via DaemonExt

## Architecture
```
file change → debounce (500ms) → incremental reparse → arena flip → event emission
                                                                          ↓
                                                              mache: generation changed
                                                              mache: invalidate cached conn
                                                              mache: next query reads new data
```

Git/jj are atomic snapshots. Each arena generation corresponds to a source state. The _meta table records the git SHA for that generation. Time-travel (jj) is LL's extension — LLO provides the reparse lifecycle, LL adds the VCS layer.

## Related beads (subsumed)
- ley-line-open-9b279e (git-aware incremental reparse)
- ley-line-open-9adb03 (readiness signal)
- ley-line-open-9af2c3 (progressive/streaming parse)
- ley-line-open-9b08e1 (priority queue)

comments.text:
All four pillars of the living daemon are now implemented across 3 commits:

**1342147** feat(daemon): living database — :memory: SQLite as source of truth
- parse_into_conn() extracted so parsing targets any Connection
- DaemonContext holds live_db: Mutex<Connection> (replaces arena_conn cache)
- Warm start from arena (crash recovery), cold start from --source
- Periodic 500ms snapshot timer + graceful shutdown snapshot
- Arena becomes dual-role: crash recovery checkpoint + mache export

**557e8a4** feat(daemon): enrichment pipeline with disjoint write sets
- EnrichmentPass trait with dependency resolution (topological sort)
- TreeSitterPass wraps parse_into_conn as base pass
- DaemonExt::enrichment_passes() for LL to register LSP/embeddings/sheaf
- op_enrich runs named passes via UDS
- Version tracking in _meta for staleness detection

**57098d3** feat(daemon): git-aware file watcher — poll git status, auto-reparse
- Polls `git status --porcelain` every 2s (smarter than raw fsnotify)
- Detects HEAD changes for branch switches
- Incremental reparse + snapshot + daemon.reparse.complete events

Checklist:
- [x] Watch source files (git-aware polling)
- [x] Reparse incrementally (parse_into_conn + _file_index)
- [x] Flip the arena (snapshot_to_arena)
- [x] Notify connected clients (EventRouter + daemon.reparse.complete)
- [x] Crash recovery (warm start from arena mmap)
- [x] Enrichment pipeline (EnrichmentPass trait + op_enrich)

Remaining follow-ups filed as separate beads:
- ley-line-open-a46586: Move VectorIndex to enrichment layer + priority queue
