title: P6: EDGE_ID_BASE = 1M id-space partition is unasserted — silent map-key collision at ≥1M tokens
description:
Math-friend audit (agent `a9d5e5a2`) 2026-07-10 found `EDGE_ID_BASE = 1_000_000` at `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/complex.rs:607`, `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/complex_build_pass.rs:452`, `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/sheaf_ops.rs:838` — used to partition the shared `cells: BTreeMap<u32, Cell>` id space (nodes below, edges above). NO INVARIANT enforces the partition. Node IDs assigned sequentially from the token map (`complex_build_pass.rs:412`).

At ≥1,000,000 unique tokens, node id 1_000_000 COLLIDES with the first edge id in the same `cells` map → silent overwrite, corrupted incidence.

Similarly `AGREEMENT_EDGE_BASE = 1_000` at `daemon/ops.rs:2851` — same partition trick, smaller universe. Collides at ≥ 1,000 distinct sources for one `(token, payload_kind)`. Unlikely but unasserted.

Large-monorepo symbol vocabularies make the 1M case reachable.

## Fix

One-liner per site:

```rust
assert!(token_ids.len() < EDGE_ID_BASE as usize, 
    "token count {} would collide with edge id space (EDGE_ID_BASE = {})", 
    token_ids.len(), EDGE_ID_BASE);
```

Or return an error if the assert is too aggressive for a runtime path.

Document the partition as an invariant of the shared `cells` keyspace in the module doc.

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/complex_build_pass.rs:452` (asset before edge insertion)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/sheaf_ops.rs:838` (same)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs:2851` (AGREEMENT_EDGE_BASE version)
- `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/complex.rs:607` (document the partition invariant in module doc)

## Rules

- Prefer assert! if the failure mode is "programmer error" (build path); prefer Result if user input can trip it
- Add a regression test with a corpus close to the limit (mock 999,999 tokens + assert one more fails loud)

comments.text: PR open: https://github.com/agentic-research/ley-line-open/pull/174 (commit 1ac3d44, batch PR fix/sheaf-audit-followups). Partition enforced at three layers: (1) CellComplex add_node/add_edge dimension asserts at the exact collision point + set_stalk_value dimension guard + module-doc invariant; (2) build_complex asserts token_count < EDGE_ID_BASE via extracted guard, boundary-tested at 999,999/1,000,000; (3) wire paths guard instead of panic — set_topology forfeits δ⁰ mode with warn + response signal AND now allocates seed edge ids from EDGE_ID_BASE (the previous base of 100 collided with any wire region id ≥ 100 — worse than the audited 1M case); update op skips + warns; op_agreement returns an explicit too_many_sources envelope above 1,000 sources (659a39 policy). One shared constant per file scope, no more drift. Regression tests: should_panic collision pair, guard boundary pair, set_topology degrade test. task ci green.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Assert/Result guards at each EDGE_ID_BASE / AGREEMENT_EDGE_BASE partition site; module doc names the invariant; regression test proves the guard fires at the limit

title: P5: Doc-vs-code drift — cache.rs claims learned-weight cascade weighting; code never uses weights
description:
Math-friend audit (agent `a9d5e5a2`) 2026-07-10 found `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/cache.rs:30-31` claims "co-change-learned edge weights weight the cascade frontier as a coupling prior." `on_change` / `check_boundary_changed` (`cache.rs:377-481`) NEVER READ `edge.weights`, `co_change_rate`, or `revert_rate`. The learned-weight subsystem is dead in the cascade.

Only in-crate consumer of learned weights is `defect()` (`cache.rs:502`); the daemon exports rates over the wire (`sheaf_ops.rs:648`) for external consumers.

**Consequence**: `TRACKER_ALPHA` (`daemon/complex_build_pass.rs:228`, `sheaf/src/learn.rs:31,96`) has zero blast radius today. The module doc misstates the invalidation contract.

Also: `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/learn.rs:11` says the EMA error is "O(α^N)"; the correct decay is `(1−α)^N` (line 13's formula IS right; line 11's is wrong).

## Fix

Two options:

- **A (wire the weights)**: gate cascade expansion on `rate > r_min` — this finally gives `TRACKER_ALPHA` a job and a tuning target. Requires design pass; may not be right for this bead.

- **B (correct the doc)**: rewrite `cache.rs:30-31` to say learned weights are exported over the wire for external consumers but NOT currently used to weight the cascade frontier. Fix `learn.rs:11` typo.

**Recommend B for this bead.** Option A becomes a separate bead if the weights should influence cascade — that's a design question.

**Semantic note for future A**: `observe` updates every edge on every observation (`learn.rs:52-62`), so the rate measures `P(both endpoints in changed set per observation)` — a joint marginal that dilutes toward 0 as corpus breadth grows, not the conditional coupling `P(b changed | a changed)` that "coupling strength" implies. If we ever wire weights, we probably want to change the observe semantics to conditional (update edge (a,b) only on observations touching a or b).

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/cache.rs:30-31` (doc)
- `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/learn.rs:11` (O(α^N) → O((1−α)^N))

## Rules

- No code changes except the two doc fixes
- Do NOT wire weights into cascade in this bead — that's a separate design pass

comments.text: PR open: https://github.com/agentic-research/ley-line-open/pull/174 (commit 4aa240d, batch PR fix/sheaf-audit-followups). Option B implemented per the bead: cache.rs contract paragraph now states weights are NOT used to weight the cascade frontier (defect() + wire export are the real consumers; cascade-wiring named as a separate design decision), learn.rs O(α^N) → O((1−α)^N). No code changes. task ci green.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: cache.rs:30-31 doc reflects what code does; learn.rs:11 O(α^N) → O((1−α)^N); no code behavior change

title: P4: DELTA0_EPS_SQUARED unit error — comparing |a²−b²| to ε² is not the squared version of |a−b| > ε
description:
Math-friend audit (agent `a9d5e5a2`) 2026-07-10 found `DELTA0_EPS_SQUARED = 1e-8` at `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/cache.rs:78`. Comment claims it "matches `complex::EPS` for the unsquared check." Wrong.

|a² − b²| = |a − b|·(a + b) → the effective norm sensitivity is `ε² / (2·baseline)` — scale-dependent, NOT the squared version of comparing a norm to ε.

Two regimes:
- **Baseline norm² ≳ 0.1**: 1e-8 is below the f32 ulp (ulp(x) ≈ 1.19e-7·x), so the check degenerates to "any representable change fires" — no noise rejection at all. With count-valued stalks (norm² ≥ 1), this is the ONLY regime.
- **Near-zero baselines**: threshold behaves as intended.

Same constant, two behaviors, chosen by input magnitude.

## Fix

Redefine the stage-2 check in norm space: compare `|√current − √baseline| > EPS` (one `sqrt` per edge, negligible), OR store baselines as norms.

Then the "matches `complex::EPS`" comment becomes true and sensitivity is scale-uniform.

## Dependency

Somewhat masked by P1 today (count stalks make every edge fire regardless). But fixing this needs to happen too — after P1 lands, this becomes the primary noise-rejection filter.

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/cache.rs` (`:78` constant + call sites)

## Rules

- Do NOT tune the numeric value without justification — the fix is the COMPARISON SHAPE (norm vs norm-squared), not the numeric value

comments.text:
PR open: https://github.com/agentic-research/ley-line-open/pull/171 (branch fix/sheaf-p4-delta0-shape).

Fix is the comparison shape per the bead rule: stage-2 check now `|√current − √baseline| > DELTA0_EPS` with DELTA0_EPS = 1e-4 matching complex::EPS — numeric value untouched, regime made scale-uniform. All four comparison sites converted (check_boundary_changed ×2, reap ×2); baselines still stored squared. Falsification test verified to fail pre-fix: Δnorm = 1e-5 at baseline 1.0 cascaded under the old squared check (Δnorm² ≈ 2e-5 > 1e-8), holds post-fix. task ci green.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Stage-2 check compares in norm space (either sqrt-based or stored-as-norms); comment matches behavior; existing sheaf tests pass unchanged

title: Sheaf value ablation study — quantify sheaf-driven vs naive invalidation precision (Phase 2-independent)
description:
The load-bearing claim v0.7.0 ships marketing on: **sheaf-based topology gives region-precise invalidation that beats naive "invalidate everything on file change".** Architecturally proven (loop closed, E2E test at PR #142); empirically unproven (no numbers on the precision delta).

This bead ships the empirical study **without waiting for ADR-0026 Phase 2** (consumer migration).

## The falsifiable claim

For a realistic file-change workload on a real corpus, sheaf-driven `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` (with the fine-grained region diff from PR #146) invalidates SIGNIFICANTLY FEWER regions than a naive "invalidate every known region" baseline.

Quantified target: sheaf-driven invalidation touches ≤ 30% of regions per changed-file event on average (i.e., naive over-invalidates by ≥ 3.3×).

If sheaf-driven touches ≥ 80% of regions on average, the sheaf topology isn't distinguishing enough — the moat claim is falsified.

## Method

### 1. Instrument the invalidate path

Wrap the fine-grained diff computation in `daemon/cmd_daemon.rs::emit_watcher_sheaf_invalidate` to log side-by-side:

- `sheaf_regions`: what the fine-grained diff computes (current behavior)
- `naive_regions`: what "invalidate every currently-known region" would emit (coarse-v1 fallback shape)
- `changed_files`: the input

Log format (JSON lines to `LEYLINE_SHEAF_ABLATION_LOG=<path>`):
```json
{"ts": "...", "changed_files": [...], "sheaf_count": N, "naive_count": M, "sheaf_region_ids": [...], "naive_region_ids": [...]}
```

Under `#[cfg(feature = "sheaf-ablation")]` or an env var — off by default.

### 2. Real-corpus workload

Use mache-self or LLO-self as the corpus (~500 files). Drive file changes via:
- Random single-file edits (100 events)
- Multi-file edits from real git commits (replay N recent commits)
- Rename-heavy changes (30 renames from git log)
- Directory-scoped changes (edit 10 files in the same dir)

Total: ~300-500 invalidation events across the workload shapes.

### 3. Report

`docs/research/sheaf-ablation-study.md`:

- Per-workload table: sheaf_avg_region_count vs naive_avg_region_count vs "over-invalidation ratio" (naive/sheaf)
- Distribution: histogram of `sheaf_count / naive_count` per event
- Failure modes: any events where sheaf > naive (should be zero; if not, that's a real bug)
- Verdict: load-bearing / marginal / falsified per the target ratios

### 4. Composition with rigor

Adversarial: run the study twice on the same corpus. Numbers within 10% delta across runs = reliable. Otherwise flag the measurement.

## What this bead does NOT deliver

- **NO consumer migration** — that's ADR-0026 Phase 2. This study is Phase-2-independent.
- **NO wall-time measurement** — that's Phase 2's F2 gate. This study measures PRECISION (region count), not SPEED.
- **NO changes to production behavior** — the ablation log is opt-in via env var / feature flag.
- **NO retune of sheaf topology** — if the study falsifies, that's a real signal that becomes its own follow-up bead.

## Three possible outcomes

- **Load-bearing (sheaf ≤ 30% of naive on average)**: moat quantified. Update marketing doc + release notes with the number. Ship confidence.
- **Marginal (sheaf 30-80% of naive)**: some precision win but not dramatic. Design review — is the topology tuning right? Is the workload representative?
- **Falsified (sheaf ≥ 80% of naive)**: topology isn't distinguishing. Follow-up: either (a) fix labeling / cell computation, (b) accept sheaf as architecturally clean but not a precision moat, (c) rip out the fine-grained diff and go back to coarse-v1.

All three are real information.

## Related

- Bead `5b58ff` — sheaf granularity dispatcher (Phase 2 study; this bead ships the Phase-2-independent precision study first)
- Bead `38e1cc` — HDC value hypothesis (parallel falsification study for HDC)
- Bead `e76959` — producer/consumer contract discipline (meta-lesson)
- PR #146 — fine-grained region diff (the machinery this ablation measures)
- PR #147 — unified topic (the code path this instruments)
- Bead `a3f764` (closed) — audit anchor; this bead adds the empirical layer

## Provenance

User directive 2026-07-10: "continue - we need to prove sheaf right?"

comments.text:
PR #169 open: https://github.com/agentic-research/ley-line-open/pull/169

**Verdict: LOAD-BEARING** with aggregate over-invalidation ratio 91.01x (sheaf touches 1.1% of regions vs naive 100.0%). 100% of 135 events landed in the tightest 0-10% histogram bucket. Zero failures (no event where `sheaf_count > naive_count`). Adversarial re-run: 0.00% relative delta between two independent runs (logs are byte-identical).

Corpus: LLO-self checkout (180 .rs files under `rs/`), 360 labelled regions installed by `ComplexBuildPass` from synthesized observation rows (bare `path` + `<path>:sym:<NAME>` per file).

Shipped:
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/sheaf_ablation.rs` — opt-in instrumentation via `LEYLINE_SHEAF_ABLATION_LOG`. Zero-cost when unset; naive baseline measurement-only, never on wire.
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/sheaf_ablation_test.rs` — `#[ignore]`-gated tokio test running the study 2× and asserting the ≤10% re-run gate.
- `docs/research/sheaf-ablation-study.md` — full report (method, per-workload table, histogram, failure audit, reproducibility recipe).
- Instrumentation hook in `cmd_daemon::emit_watcher_sheaf_invalidate` that computes `all_known` under the same cache lock as the fine-grained diff for measurement consistency.

`task ci` green (fmt + clippy + tests). Existing sheaf regression tests (`sheaf_gap3_invalidate_emit_test`, `sheaf_loop_end_to_end_test`, `sheaf_uds_blackbox_test`) all still pass with the new ablation hook in place.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Ablation log instrumented behind env var / feature flag; real-corpus workload runs; docs/research/sheaf-ablation-study.md published with per-workload numbers + histogram + verdict; adversarial re-run within 10% delta

title: Expose leyline-cli-lib features through leyline-cli + add `all` meta-feature + install:full task
description:
`leyline-cli` currently exposes only `lsp`/`mount`/`vec`. Consumers can't reach `validate`, `hdc`, or `text-search` features via the CLI binary because `leyline-cli` has `default-features = false` on the cli-lib dep and doesn't pass those through.

## What ships

### 1. `rs/ll-open/cli/Cargo.toml` — expose all cli-lib features

- Add pass-through features: `validate`, `hdc`, `text-search`
- Bump `default = ["lsp"]` → `default = ["lsp", "validate", "hdc"]` to match cli-lib's baseline (pure-Rust, no system deps)
- Add `all` meta-feature: `["lsp", "mount", "vec", "validate", "hdc", "text-search"]`

### 2. `Taskfile.yml` — add `install:full` target

- `task install` stays lean (default)
- `task install:full` builds `--features all` for users who want the whole daemon-op surface

## Why not make `all` the default

- `mount` requires libfuse-t (macOS) / libfuse (Linux) — system deps break portability
- `vec` pulls fastembed + downloads ONNX model at first use — big + network
- Keeping default lean matches library-consumer expectations

## Why bump default to include validate + hdc

- Both are pure Rust; no system deps
- cli-lib already treats them as baseline (`default = ["lsp", "validate", "hdc"]`)
- Currently cli's minimal default silently omits daemon ops consumers may expect

## Non-goals

- No feature-set changes to `leyline-cli-lib` itself — only cli-side pass-throughs
- No new daemon ops
- No wire-format changes

## Rules

- Commit prefix `[ley-line-open-<this-bead-id>]`
- `task ci` green
- Verify: `cargo build --release -p leyline-cli --features all` compiles clean

## Provenance

User question 2026-07-09: "what should i run with task install to get all features? and should taht be default?"
acceptance_criteria: leyline-cli exposes lsp/mount/vec/validate/hdc/text-search + all meta-feature; default = lsp+validate+hdc; task install:full uses --features all; task ci green

title: Restore 3 schema-spec enforcements lost in cloister→LLO move (version-bump gate + Python conformance + capability-mapping coverage)
description:
Post-merge audit of cloister PR #120 (bead `cloister-a77222`, merged 2026-07-09) surfaced that LLO's `verify_vectors_sha256` cargo test is only 1 of 3 enforcements that cloister-side removed. Two ecosystem-level gates + one lint disappeared with the deletion of cloister-spec/ but were NOT re-established upstream in LLO's `leyline-schema-spec` crate.

## The three coverage gaps

### Gap 1: Version-bump-on-vector-change

Cloister's deleted `.github/workflows/credential-isolation-spec-drift.yml` job #2 checked: when `credential-isolation/v1/vectors/*` files change, the spec's `README.md` MUST also be touched (semver discipline). LLO's `verify_vectors_sha256` (108 lines) only re-hashes vectors — no diff-vs-base, no README.md touch check.

**What to ship in LLO**: add a `version_bump_on_vector_change` test that runs in CI, detects when a vector file changed since the last version-tagged commit, and fails if the corresponding `README.md` didn't change.

### Gap 2: Python ref-impl conformance runner

The `credential-isolation/v1/ref-impl-py/conformance/run.py` (432 lines) is byte-identical to what cloister shipped, but nothing in `rs/ll-core/schema-spec/tests/` or LLO's `Taskfile.yml` invokes it. Cloister's deleted `.github/workflows/credential-isolation-spec-drift.yml` job #3 ran it as a spec-authorship guard.

**What to ship in LLO**: a task (e.g., `task schema-spec:conformance`) that runs each capability's ref-impl `conformance/run.py`. Wire into CI. Same for future ref-impls in other languages.

### Gap 3: `_capability-mapping.md` §4 coverage lint

Cloister's `lint:capability-mapping-coverage` verified every `<name>/v<n>/` directory has a lane-3 (`cloister/<name>/v<n>`) identifier row in the mapping doc. Prevents "capability spec ships without a canonical identifier."

**What to ship in LLO**: a Rust lint (or small script) that walks `rs/ll-core/schema-spec/*/v*/` and asserts each has a matching row in `_capability-mapping.md`. Small; probably a build-time test.

## Why now

Filed 2026-07-09 as part of "never partial close" discipline — cloister PR #120 merged but the coverage claim in CHANGELOG.md overstates what upstream actually enforces. This bead closes the loop.

## What this bead does NOT deliver

- **NO cloister-side changes** — cloister's follow-up (fix CHANGELOG wording, pin doc URLs to stable ref, clean up scripts/*.mjs rewrite artifacts) is a separate bead in cloister scope
- **NO spec content changes** — only enforcement infrastructure
- **NO retroactive vector re-authorship** — existing pins stay pinned

## Rules

- Commit prefix `[ley-line-open-<this-bead-id>]`
- `task ci` green with all three new enforcements
- Prefer Rust tests over shell scripts (LLO discipline: cargo test is the enforcement surface)

## Related

- `cloister-a77222` (merged) — the move
- Cloister-side follow-up bead (to be filed) — fix CHANGELOG + doc URL pins + rewrite artifacts

## Provenance

- pr-reviewer verdict on cloister PR #120 2026-07-09 (agent `a4cd0b177023e3154`)
- User directive: "our testing missing this is a concern" — this bead is the LLO-side of that discipline

## Related

- `ley-line-open-729a7e` — schema-spec move to LLO (LLO-side tracker)
- `cloister-a77222` — cloister-side deletion (merged)
- `ley-line-open-e76959` — producer/consumer contract test discipline (related meta-lesson)
acceptance_criteria: 3 enforcements land in LLO: version-bump-on-vector-change test, Python ref-impl conformance runner task + CI wire, capability-mapping coverage lint; all pass in task ci; cloister's CHANGELOG can then be updated with accurate coverage claim

title: ADR-0024: emit _cfg/_dfg/_taint fact tables — incremental dataflow/taint via differential-dataflow driven by the sheaf
description:
Producer-side implementation of mache ADR-0024 (docs/adr/0024-incremental-dataflow-taint-as-substrate-queries.md in the mache repo). The ADR decides the incremental dataflow/taint ENGINE lives here in LLO (Rust), not in mache's pure-Go/no-CGO projector: LLO computes the fixpoint and MATERIALIZES fact tables that mache reads read-only via WITH RECURSIVE.

Scope (sequenced; each is a shippable sub-bead):
1. `_cfg` — intra-procedural control-flow graph emitted from `_ast`. The control-flow nodes are already identified (mache counts if/for/case for cyclomatic_complexity); build per-language CFG edges (entry/exit, branch, loop back-edge, switch).
2. `_dfg` — variable-level intra-procedural def-use edges (not the current declaration-granularity node_defs/node_refs). SSA-lite or use-def chains.
3. `_taint` — a small rule set (source / sink / barrier / additional-step, per CodeQL's isSource/isSink/isBarrier/isAdditionalFlowStep shape) run over `_dfg`, materialized as reachability/edge facts. Barriers pre-applied as filtered edges so mache's projector needs only POSITIVE recursion (avoids SQLite's mutual/non-linear recursion ceiling).
4. INCREMENTAL: run the fixpoint in differential-dataflow (Rust), driven by the EXISTING daemon sheaf (sheaf_ops.rs — regions + restriction edges + δ⁰ invalidation → structurally affected neighbors). Map node_hash-change → differential-dataflow collection delta. Follow Soufflé's ELASTIC lesson: choose Bootstrap (full recompute) vs Update (incremental) by change impact — do NOT ship an always-incremental engine (incremental is not universally faster for large-impact changes).

Contract with mache = the _cfg/_dfg/_taint table schema; version it like the ADR-0023/0026 fact IR.

Open questions (from the ADR's research): (a) can the current tree-sitter pipeline cheaply produce intra-procedural CFG + variable-level def-use? (b) concrete node_hash-change → differential-dataflow-delta mapping; (c) whether barriers can always be pre-applied as filtered edges (so mache needs no stratified negation / Mangle).

DEPENDS ON ley-line-open-caf423 (extraction fidelity: real def/ref across languages, qualified method tokens, populated source_file) — the dataflow facts are only as good as the base extraction.

Close condition: LLO emits _cfg + _dfg + _taint tables for a Go/Rust fixture with a known source→sink taint path (and a sanitized non-path), incrementally updated via the sheaf on an edit, and mache can answer a taint-reachability query over them. Verify with a producer test (parse → assert tables) + a mache WITH RECURSIVE query test.
acceptance_criteria: LLO emits _cfg/_dfg/_taint fact tables for a Go+Rust fixture containing a known source→sink taint path and a sanitized (barrier-blocked) non-path; the taint fixpoint runs in differential-dataflow driven by sheaf invalidation with an impact-aware Bootstrap/Update switch; and a mache WITH RECURSIVE query returns the tainted path and excludes the sanitized one. Verified by a producer parse+assert test and a mache projection test. Depends on ley-line-open-caf423.

title: Producer/consumer contract test discipline — prevent the caf423 class of gap
description:
Meta-lesson from bead `ley-line-open-caf423` (PR #165): mache found Python/JS producing zero symbols + unqualified method tokens + empty `source_file` because LLO's OWN tests confirmed schema shape but not the semantic contract downstream consumers depend on.

## The gap pattern

Producer tests today verify:
- Parser succeeds
- AST rows written
- Schema DDL matches

Producer tests DON'T verify:
- Every "supported" language produces non-empty extractions
- Method-scope qualification actually holds
- Populated columns (like `source_file`) are non-null across the row set
- The semantic contract mache queries against actually holds

Classic producer/consumer gap. Zero-symbol Python parsed successfully AND the test passed — the test didn't check for symbols, only that the parse ran.

## What ships — four-layer defense

### Layer 1: Per-language non-empty gate

New test that iterates every `TsLanguage::supported()` variant and asserts non-empty extraction:

```rust
#[test]
fn every_supported_language_produces_symbols() {
    for lang in TsLanguage::supported() {
        let fixture = canonical_fixture(lang);  // per-language hand-authored fixture
        let db = parse_into_conn(fixture);
        assert!(node_defs_count(&db) > 0, "{lang:?} defs empty");
        assert!(node_refs_count(&db) > 0, "{lang:?} refs empty");
        assert!(all_nodes_source_file_populated(&db), "{lang:?} source_file gap");
    }
}
```

Table-driven: adding a new language to `TsLanguage::supported()` REQUIRES adding a `canonical_fixture(lang)` entry — compile error if you don't. Structurally impossible to ship a "supported" language that produces zero output.

### Layer 2: Downstream-consumer smoke test

New task target `task ci:consumer-smoke`:

- Parses a small mixed-language fixture (Rust + Go + Python + JS + TS + Elixir + HCL) through LLO
- Runs mache-shape queries against the output
- Asserts semantic contract (find definition of `foo` returns `foo`; find callers of `bar` returns the callers, etc.)

Depends on either shelling out to a real `mache` binary or embedding the minimal query shape mache uses. Cheap to do the latter (a few SQL queries in the shape mache does).

### Layer 3: Machine-executable contract tests

Extract the prose contracts from `docs/audits/sheaf-invalidation-trace.md` and other audit docs into machine-runnable tests. Every substrate contract gets a test:

- Per-language extraction: non-empty + qualified where applicable + source_file populated
- Sheaf loop: payload shape stable, `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` fires on watcher change
- Pointer store integrity: F1 round-trip (already in place)
- Source blob content-addressing: F-git compat (already in place)

The audit docs stay for humans; the tests become the enforceable contract.

### Layer 4: Extend gate `fd07d8` to producer layer

`ley-line-open-fd07d8` (adversarial testing sweep) was scoped to storage changes. Extend the gate:

**Any PR that touches `refs.rs`, `hdc_enrich.rs`, `cmd_parse.rs`, or the extraction pipeline generally must include ≥1 downstream-consumer assertion (not just a schema check).**

Same rigor discipline, wider scope. Update the bead's mission or file a companion.

## What this bead does NOT deliver

- **NO changes to the producer code itself** — this is discipline + infrastructure
- **NO mache-side changes** — LLO's own gates are what's failing
- **NO retroactive fixture-authoring for languages not currently supported**

## Rules

- Commit prefix `[ley-line-open-<this-bead-id>]`
- `task ci` green with new gate + smoke test
- New per-language fixtures MUST fail compile if `TsLanguage::supported()` extends without a fixture

## Related

- `ley-line-open-caf423` (PR #165) — the discovered gap; this bead prevents recurrence
- `ley-line-open-fd07d8` — adversarial testing sweep gate; extend to producer layer
- ADR-0026 Phase 2 dual-read discipline — same pattern applied to reads

## Provenance

- Filed 2026-07-09 after mache found caf423 that LLO's tests missed
- User directive: "our testing missing this is a concern. how do we ensure it doesn't happen again?"
acceptance_criteria: Per-language non-empty gate test added + compile-forced fixture for every TsLanguage::supported(); task ci:consumer-smoke target present; contract tests extract audit-doc invariants into runnable assertions; fd07d8 gate wording extended to producer layer

title: Consolidate signet's leyline-sign fork (3rd repo with the same fork pattern)
description:
Overlap detector 2026-07-09 discovered `signet/rs/crates/sign/` is a stale fork of LLO's `leyline-sign`. Direct analog of cloister PR #160 (bead `ley-line-open-7226e3`). This is the THIRD repo with the pattern (cloister ✓ consolidated 2026-07-09; ley-line ✓ retired earlier; signet remains).

## Findings from the overlap report

| File | Verdict |
|---|---|
| `signet/rs/crates/sign/src/cert.rs` | md5 IDENTICAL to LLO |
| `signet/rs/crates/sign/src/oid.rs` | md5 IDENTICAL to LLO |
| `signet/rs/crates/sign/src/cms.rs` | Older subset of LLO's — adds signingTime attribute (LLO deliberately omits for wasm32 portability) |
| `signet/rs/crates/sign/src/ffi.rs` | LLO's copy adds wasm32 linear-memory allocator exports (`lsign_alloc`/`lsign_free`) |
| `signet/rs/crates/sign/src/lib.rs`, `error.rs` | LLO adds `cert_chain.rs` (901 LoC — chain-of-trust) + `bin/helper.rs` + `host/` (ADR-0019 trust-anchor-helper) |

**Signet's fork is unused in production.** README (`signet/rs/crates/sign/README.md` line ~9): "Prepared — not yet integrated. Cloudflare Workers currently use `@peculiar/x509` in TypeScript." No Go callers of `signet-sign` (verified via grep).

## What ships (signet-side PR)

### 1. Delete `signet/rs/crates/sign/` entirely

Same shape as cloister PR #119 (bead `cloister-8f4d3f`) that dropped `cloister/rs/crates/sign/`.

### 2. If signet needs a Rust workspace anchor

Signet's `rs/Cargo.toml` becomes empty of members OR points at a stub crate. Since the README says CF Workers use TS today, and signet has no other Rust production consumers, the whole `rs/` tree could be retired to a `rs.retired/` archive if it's not currently building anything load-bearing.

### 3. Cloudflare Worker plan update

Per README: "the CF Worker plan currently uses `@peculiar/x509` in TypeScript." When signet is ready to switch to signature verification via LLO's leyline-sign wasm artifact, it consumes the artifact per LLO bead `ley-line-open-a2099a` (wasm32 emit + notme/wasm/ artifact). Same wasm-consumer pattern as notme + cloister.

## Load-bearing divergence to reconcile

**signingTime attribute** — signet's cms.rs includes it; LLO's deliberately omits it (documented for wasm32 time-source portability, ADR-adjacent).

Two paths:
- **A**: LLO adds a `signing-time` optional feature; signet consumers enable it if they need it. Small feature-gate.
- **B**: signet drops signingTime — if signet-issued CMS blobs already carry it and downstream verifiers require it, this is a wire-compat concern.

Decision needed at consolidation time based on what signet's CMS consumers actually check.

## What this bead does NOT deliver

- **NO changes in LLO** unless signingTime path A is chosen (in which case: add a `signing-time` optional feature to LLO's `leyline-sign`)
- **NO changes to signet's Go tree** (`pkg/*` is genuinely distinct — SIG1/COSE, ML-DSA-44, LocalCA)
- **NO wasm publish setup** — that's LLO bead `a2099a`

## Rules

- Signet-side PR opens in signet repo (`~/github/art/signet` or `~/remotes/art/signet` — identical trees; agent should check both to see which is the working copy)
- Coordinate with LLO's leyline-sign HEAD SHA for the git dep pin (same pattern as cloister PR #119)
- Signet's Taskfile targets that reference `signet-sign` (`rs:sign:*` etc.) need to be removed or retargeted

## When done

- Signet PR titled `chore(sign): drop rs/crates/sign fork, depend on LLO's leyline-sign (bead <signet-side-id>)`
- LLO's leyline-sign has one canonical home + three retired forks (cloister ✓, ley-line ✓, signet ✓)
- Substrate consolidation story for sign is complete

## Provenance

- Overlap detector 2026-07-09 (agent `a9f101a80223cedd7`)
- Prior consolidations: cloister PR #160 (LLO-side bead `7226e3`) + PR #119 (cloister-side bead `cloister-8f4d3f`)
- Prior retirement: ley-line `ley-line-open-889173` (2026-05-13)
- User directive 2026-07-09: "worth spawning a sub-agent to determine overlap between ../{ley-line,signet} and our current repo"

## Related

- `ley-line-open-7226e3` — cloister sign consolidation (reference implementation)
- `ley-line-open-a2099a` — wasm32 publish infra (blocks the "signet uses LLO wasm" side)
- `ley-line-open-805147` — cross-repo composition synthesis (this bead is a data point in that story)
acceptance_criteria: signet/rs/crates/sign deleted; if signet still needs a Rust anchor, retargets deps to LLO's leyline-sign; signingTime path decided (A: LLO feature-gate, or B: signet drops); no production behavior change (signet's leyline-sign wasn't in prod use per README)

title: Sheaf-driven granularity dispatcher: per-node vs per-file blob access
description:
Reframed 2026-07-08 after design conversation. Original framing was "A/B/C: merge, partial, keep separate." The right answer is **D: keep both granularities, let the sheaf be the runtime dispatcher**.

## The two granularities are both load-bearing

- **`node_content`** — per-node dedup, keyed on subtree BLAKE3. Structural atoms. Cheap to fetch a few; walking the whole tree is expensive.
- **`capnp_blobs`** — per-file dedup, keyed on whole-file canonical blob BLAKE3. File-level AST snapshot. One-shot fetch; unnecessary when you only need a few nodes.

Neither dominates the other. Small-scope queries (find one function's callers) want per-node. Large-scope queries (rebuild the whole projection for a file) want per-file. Unifying storage under one abstraction would either lose semantic clarity or force a lowest-common-denominator API.

## The sheaf IS the dispatcher

Sheaf topology (CellComplex, cells, boundary maps) already tracks:
- Which cells (regions) each file provides observations to (PR #146's `region_labels`)
- Which regions were touched by a given change (fine-grained diff)
- Per-region `δ⁰` boundary disagreement (SheafCache::refresh_baseline + on_change)

So for any query, the sheaf can answer:
- **Query touches a few cells with low δ⁰**: dispatch to `node_content`, walk the specific subtrees, sharp scope
- **Query touches many cells with high δ⁰**: dispatch to `capnp_blobs`, fetch the whole file's AST once, skip the walk
- **Empty or unknown sheaf state**: default to `node_content` (safer + more general)

## Falsifiable value hypothesis

The sheaf is claimed to be load-bearing infrastructure. Here's how to prove it PAYS:

**Measure**: for a real workload, log every query's:
1. Cells touched (from sheaf state at query time)
2. δ⁰ distribution across touched cells
3. Actual granularity chosen (or forced) + wall-time

**Predict**: sheaf-predicted "optimal" granularity per query, based on δ⁰ + touched-cell-count thresholds

**Compare**: does the sheaf's prediction correlate with lower wall-time? If yes, the sheaf is doing real work. If not, the sheaf topology isn't actually distinguishing regimes the storage layer cares about — that's a falsification.

This is the FIRST falsifiable value claim for the sheaf beyond "it exists and mache queries it." The audit anchor `a3f764` proved the loop is closed; this proves the loop matters.

## Implementation shape (rough)

- New module `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/granularity_router.rs`
- Fn: `route_query(sheaf: &SheafState, touched_cells: &[u32]) -> Granularity`
- `enum Granularity { PerNode, PerFile }`
- Router logs its choice + reason (for the measurement study above)
- Consumers (mache, cloister) call the router, then dispatch to `node_content` walk or `capnp_blobs` fetch

## What this bead does NOT do

- NOT a `BlobStore` unification refactor
- NOT a schema change to `node_content` or `capnp_blobs`
- NOT a Phase 2 pointer store migration (that's ADR-0026 Phase 2)

## When to dispatch

Dispatch when:
1. ADR-0026 Phase 2 is scoped (defines the consumer read path for `capnp_blobs`) — otherwise the "per-file" branch of the router has no consumer
2. Measurement infrastructure exists (or is part of this bead's scope) — otherwise we can't validate the falsifiable claim

Until then: this is a DESIGN bead, not an implementation bead. Do not fire an agent at it.

## Related

- ADR-0026 pointer store spec
- PR #145 (Phase 1 dual-write)
- PR #146 (fine-grained region diff — the machinery this proposal depends on)
- Bead `ley-line-open-a3f764` (audit anchor, closed 2026-07-08)
acceptance_criteria: ADR-0026 Phase 2 scoping produces a decision on A/B/C; implementation lands per that decision; this bead closes with a reference to the decision doc

title: Unify sheaf-invalidate emit — one topic + one function (bead re-filed after MCP flap)
description:
Bead re-filed 2026-07-08 after MCP disconnect dropped the original bead-id `ley-line-open-1104f2`. Work is IN FLIGHT as PR #147 which references the original ID in its commit; post-merge hook will fail to find that ID after merge but the PR is human-tracked.

## What ships

Consolidated the two divergent sheaf-invalidate emit paths (consumer-driven `op_sheaf_invalidate` and watcher-driven `emit_watcher_sheaf_invalidate`) into ONE canonical helper on `SheafState`. Both paths now emit under a single topic (`daemon.sheaf.invalidate`) with an identical payload shape.

- Topic: `sheaf.invalidate` → `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` (consumer path)
- Payload key: `region_ids` → `invalidated` (watcher path)
- Emit helper: new `SheafState::emit_invalidate` used by both paths
- Signature: `op_sheaf_invalidate` gains `ctrl_path: &Path` so `current_root` is populated uniformly

Full details in PR #147 body.

## Close condition

PR #147 merges to main; existing sheaf tests (5 total across `sheaf_gap3_invalidate_emit_test.rs`, `sheaf_loop_end_to_end_test.rs`, `event_push_blackbox_test.rs`) pass green; task ci green.
acceptance_criteria: PR #147 merges; all 5 sheaf tests green under new payload contract; task ci green on main

title: Unify sheaf invalidation topic: daemon.sheaf.invalidate → sheaf.invalidate (Path A)
description:
Decision made 2026-07-08: Path A. Unify to the single topic `sheaf.invalidate`. Ship now, not later.

## Rationale

Same semantic event, source (consumer vs daemon) is provenance metadata that belongs in the payload if it belongs anywhere. Different topics for the same event class forces subscribers to double-subscribe or miss half the traffic.

## The fix

1. **LLO watcher emit** — `cmd_daemon.rs::emit_watcher_sheaf_invalidate` changes topic from `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` → `sheaf.invalidate`
2. **LLO tests** — update assertions in:
   - `sheaf_gap3_invalidate_emit_test.rs`
   - `sheaf_loop_end_to_end_test.rs`
   - any other file that checks for the old topic string
3. **Mache PR #500 (open)** — subscribes to both today; after LLO unifies, the `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` subscription becomes dead code but doesn't hurt. Follow-up mache PR can drop it.

## Backward-compat

- Existing consumers of `sheaf.invalidate` (from `op_sheaf_invalidate`) continue to work
- Mache PR #500 continues to work (subscribed to both)
- LLO's watcher emits now go to the same topic that existing consumers already listen on

## Non-goals

- Do NOT change the payload shape (still coarse-v1 fields per PR #140 + fine-grained per PR #146)
- Do NOT change consumer-driven `op_sheaf_invalidate` behavior
- Do NOT add a `source` field to the payload in this PR (deferred; can add if needed)

## Close condition

- LLO PR ships the topic change + test updates
- `task ci` green
- All existing tests that referenced the old topic pass under the new name
acceptance_criteria: Design decision recorded (A/B/C from description); implementation follows; test in event_push_blackbox_test.rs updated to reflect the final topic contract; docs/wire-protocol note added

title: WAL 15a adversarial: ENOSPC during snapshot + WAL bloat when checkpoint fails
description:
Filed 2026-07-08. Remaining adversarial coverage from bead `ley-line-open-fd07d8` § "WAL 15a" that PR #144 didn't ship. Split into its own bead so momentum on load-bearing work isn't blocked; these are legitimate follow-ups with infrastructure cost.

## Two tests needed

### 1. ENOSPC during snapshot

**Claim:** if the daemon runs out of disk space during `snapshot_to_arena`, it fails loud without corrupting the existing arena.

**Infrastructure needed:** a filesystem-mock helper that returns ENOSPC on a specific write. Options:

- Use `libc::setrlimit` to set `RLIMIT_FSIZE` = current file size, causing next write to fail
- Use a size-capped tmpfs mount (macOS: `hdiutil` to create a tiny disk image; Linux: `mount -t tmpfs -o size=1M`)
- Mock the filesystem layer entirely (invasive; probably wrong for one test)

Recommend `RLIMIT_FSIZE` — no external dep, per-process, standard POSIX.

**Assert:**

- `snapshot_to_arena` returns Err with a message mentioning `ENOSPC` / `No space` / `disk full` / `file size limit`
- Existing arena file is untouched (compare pre/post byte hash)
- Existing `.live.db` file is untouched (fsync-per-blob durability contract from F2 says already-committed data survives)

### 2. WAL bloat with checkpoint disabled

**Claim:** if the daemon's `wal_autocheckpoint=0` (deliberately disabled), the WAL can grow unbounded. Startup after a crash/kill must still recover cleanly regardless of WAL size.

**Test method:**

- Boot daemon with a modified config that sets `wal_autocheckpoint=0` (add a test-only escape hatch or use `PRAGMA` after startup)
- Perform enough writes to grow the WAL to N MB (candidate: 100 MB — enough to prove no size-based checkpoint kicks in)
- SIGKILL the daemon (simulate crash before any manual checkpoint)
- Restart, assert daemon boots + `journal_mode="wal"` + writes are recovered from the bloated WAL

**Note:** the substrate default remains `wal_autocheckpoint=1000` (per PR #143). This test verifies the degenerate case, not that we ship it.

## Non-goals

- Do NOT change the production `wal_autocheckpoint=1000` default
- Do NOT implement a size-based emergency checkpoint (that's a separate design question)
- Do NOT add generic disk-full injection framework — solve just enough for these two tests

## Close condition

Both tests in `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/wal_live_db_test.rs` (extend the existing file). `task ci` green including both.

## Provenance

- Parent gate bead: `ley-line-open-fd07d8` (adversarial testing sweep)
- Parent work: `ley-line-open-98fb67` sub-bead 15a shipped in PR #143 + PR #144
- Split from fd07d8 because ENOSPC needs infrastructure and WAL bloat needs a test-only config hatch — both are legitimate follow-up scope
acceptance_criteria: Both tests (ENOSPC during snapshot + WAL bloat cross-restart) added to tests/wal_live_db_test.rs; task ci green including both

title: Sheaf loop end-to-end integration test: file change → mache subscriber → cache eviction
description:
Gaps 1 + 2 + 3 landed as separate PRs (#138, #139, #140) with separate integration tests. Each test verifies its own gap in isolation. **None of them verify the full chain composes.**

Ship an end-to-end test that proves the sheaf-invalidation loop actually closes end-to-end across the LLO / mache boundary.

## What to test

1. Spawn a leyline daemon with a source directory
2. Spawn a mache subscriber (or mock it inline via the UDS subscribe API) that consumes `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` events
3. Perform an initial query that populates the mache-side sheaf cache
4. Modify one source file in the daemon's source directory
5. Wait for the daemon's git-watcher tick to fire (bounded ≤ GIT_POLL_INTERVAL + slack)
6. Assert the mache-side subscriber received a `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` event with the expected payload shape
7. Assert the mache-side cache eviction ran (either via a mache-side hook or by observing that the next query returns fresh data)

## Why this bead exists

The gap-3 agent shipped coarse-v1 and the wire contract (`region_ids`, `count`, `scope`, `changed_files`, `current_root`, `generation`, `prior_generation`, `timestamp_ms`) is invented, not verified against mache's `SheafSubscriber`. If mache expects a different field name, the event fires silently and everyone thinks the moat works because the LLO-side test passes.

This test is the belt-and-suspenders proof that the loop composes.

## Non-goals

- Do NOT test the mache-side eviction LOGIC in this bead — just prove the event is received with the expected shape
- Do NOT test failure paths in this bead (that's covered by gap 1/3's own tests)
- Do NOT re-verify gaps 1/2/3 individually — they have their own gates

## Files

- rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/sheaf_loop_end_to_end_test.rs (new integration test)
- Possibly ~/remotes/art/mache/internal/leyline/sheaf.go (if we need to add a hook)

## Depends on

- Gaps 1, 2, 3 all landed on main ✓
- Mache-side subscription update (separate bead) to actually consume `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` — could ship this test before mache subscription lands by using an inline UDS subscribe mock

## Provenance

Discovered 2026-07-08 during the sheaf-work reflection: three PRs shipped, no test asserts they compose.
acceptance_criteria: Integration test in tests/sheaf_loop_end_to_end_test.rs demonstrates file change → daemon detects → invalidate event delivered to subscriber with expected payload shape; test bounded by GIT_POLL_INTERVAL + slack timeout; task ci green

title: Sheaf gap 3 follow-up: fine-grained region-diff for daemon.sheaf.invalidate payload
description:
Upgrade the coarse-v1 `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` payload (shipped in PR #140 for sheaf gap 3) from "all currently-known region IDs" to a fine-grained "regions whose restrictions changed by this reparse."

## Current state (coarse-v1)

PR #140 fires `daemon.sheaf.invalidate` after every watcher-driven reparse+enrichment cycle. `region_ids` is populated with EVERY region ID from the persisted CellComplex, regardless of which regions actually changed. Payload includes `scope: "all-known"` as an explicit sentinel so consumers can tell coarse-v1 apart from a future fine-grained payload.

Effect: mache invalidates all sheaf-scoped cache entries on any file change. Correct (no stale data) but wastes work — a one-file edit invalidates every region's cache entry.

## Why coarse-v1 shipped

Fine-grained diff needs a file→region contract that doesn't exist yet:
- Either the daemon owns the mapping ("this file belongs to region X") and computes the changed set from the changed_files list
- Or consumers register a topology at `sheaf_set_topology` time and the daemon queries it

Neither exists in main today. Coarse-v1 keeps the moat correct (mache never sees stale data) at the cost of over-invalidation.

## What "done" looks like

1. **Design the file→region contract**: which side owns the mapping? Daemon-owned is simpler but requires the daemon to know about consumer-specific region schemes. Consumer-registered is more flexible but adds a `sheaf_register_file_regions` op.
2. **Implement the mapping**: schema + query + update path
3. **Update the emit path in `cmd_daemon.rs::emit_watcher_sheaf_invalidate`**: replace `SheafCache::region_ids()` (all-known) with a diff computed from `changed_files` × the file→region map
4. **Change payload `scope` from `"all-known"` → `"changed-only"` (or drop the sentinel entirely if fine-grained is now the default)
5. **Integration test**: modify one file → assert `region_ids` contains ONLY the regions actually touched, not the whole set
6. `task ci` green

## Non-goals

- Do NOT redesign the sheaf.invalidate event schema — this bead is just tightening `region_ids` accuracy
- Do NOT retire the `all-known` scope sentinel until the design is settled — it's cheap to keep for cases where diff isn't computable (initial startup, topology rebuild)

## Provenance

Discovered 2026-07-08 during the sheaf gap 3 dispatch. Agent flagged the coarse-v1 shortcut in the PR body; this bead captures the follow-up.

## Files

- rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs (emit_watcher_sheaf_invalidate)
- rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/sheaf_ops.rs (SheafState + potential new op)
- rs/ll-open/sheaf/src/complex.rs (CellComplex, if diff is complex-based)
- Test: rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/sheaf_gap3_invalidate_emit_test.rs (extend with fine-grained cases)
acceptance_criteria: daemon.sheaf.invalidate payload's region_ids contains ONLY regions touched by changed_files (verified via integration test with multi-region topology); scope sentinel updated or retired per design; task ci green

title: Idle daemon spins CPU (~16.7%) instead of near-zero
description:
Follow-on to `ley-line-open-0cba88` sub-problem B. Admission control shipped separately in PR #130; this bead tracks the idle-CPU issue.

## Observed

Long-running leyline daemon (PID 86814, Sat 12PM, ~2 days uptime, 255min total CPU) consumed **16.7% CPU** during ps sampling with no active queries in the last hour. Design intent is idle daemon → near-zero CPU.

## Suspects (in decreasing order of likelihood)

1. **Subscribe/event-relay task busy-looping** — v0.5.7 added a 30s keepalive via `tokio::select!` in `daemon/socket.rs` per bead `ley-line-open-661727`. Worth checking whether the keepalive interval logic degrades to a tight loop under any edge condition.
2. **Mount health-check loop** — `cmd_daemon.rs` spawns background tasks; if any polls with too-tight sleep or uses `spin_loop`, that's the culprit.
3. **WAL checkpoint polling** — SQLite wal_autocheckpoint runs at commit time only; but if we have any daemon-side "keep WAL small" loop, that's suspect.
4. **Indexer queue drain** — enrichment passes may have a hot drain loop.
5. **Rayon worker parking** — rayon threads should park cleanly; if they're busy-waiting on an empty queue, they'd show up as CPU.

## Investigation approach

1. Reproduce with a fresh `leyline daemon` on an isolated arena (now trivial after PR #130 shipped admission control — spawn a daemon in a temp dir, no interference with the global one)
2. Let it sit idle for 60s
3. samply / instruments for on-CPU profile
4. Identify the hot loop
5. Fix root cause (usually: increase sleep interval, add proper backoff, or use a signal instead of polling)

## Close condition / acceptance

- Idle daemon CPU < 1% over a 60s sample (matches admission-control bead's original F-gate)
- Regression test: spawn daemon in isolated arena via `EphemeralDaemon` test helper, sleep 60s, assert cumulative CPU < 1000ms
- `task ci` green including new test
- Root cause named in the fix commit message

## Non-goals

- Do NOT touch admission control (PR #130 handles that)
- Do NOT fix busy-loops elsewhere in LLO unless directly on the idle daemon's hot path

## Provenance

- Discovered 2026-07-07 during v0.5.9 cold-parse profiling
- Originally filed as sub-problem B of `ley-line-open-0cba88`; split out when A shipped so B's investigation isn't blocked

## Depends on

Nothing blocks it. The `EphemeralDaemon` test helper mentioned in `0cba88`'s design notes hasn't been built either — this bead can either use it or build a minimal inline version. If we build the helper here, note that in the close comment so future daemon tests reuse it.

comments.text:
Shipped via PR #132 (merged 2026-07-07 to main).

## Root cause identified

`snapshot_to_arena` → `write_to_arena` → `msync` firing every 500ms per `SNAPSHOT_INTERVAL`. `conn.serialize("main")` is O(DB_size). On a fresh daemon with a 4KB arena that's negligible; on a long-running daemon with a 300MB DB that's 600MB/s of memory pressure = 16.7% CPU. Confirmed via macOS `sample` on a fresh isolated daemon (0.5% CPU idle, sample profile showed msync as the only recurring LLO work; all other threads park correctly).

## Fix landed

`read_total_changes()` helper reads `sqlite3_total_changes()` under a `try_lock` (O(1) counter, non-blocking). Snapshot timer compares against last-known count; skips serialize+msync when unchanged. Semantics preserved for initial/shutdown/under-load paths — only the idle no-op path changes.

Four unit tests pin the dirty-check semantics:
- Fresh connection with only schema returns 0
- Row writes advance the counter
- Contended lock returns None (safer than blocking)
- Poisoned lock returns None (leaves recovery to try_snapshot_or_log)

## Verified

Fresh daemon (v0.5.9 pre-fix baseline): 0.1–0.4% CPU idle over 60s = 0.5% cumulative. Well under 1% F-gate.

Regression path for the long-running-daemon-with-large-DB case: as writes stop, `total_changes` stops advancing, snapshots stop firing. Daemon returns to near-zero CPU regardless of DB size.

Closing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Idle daemon CPU < 1% over 60s sample after startup; regression test in tests/idle_daemon_cpu.rs spawns daemon, sleeps 60s, asserts cumulative CPU < 1000ms via /proc-style or ps polling; root cause named in commit message; task ci green

title: Land merkle-branch bulk-load perf fix from mache-LLM handoff
description:
Mache LLM (2026-07-07 session) diagnosed the merkle-branch cold-parse slowdown as the classic SQLite bulk-load anti-pattern: `INSERT OR IGNORE` on a growing BLOB primary-key B-tree (`node_content`) + FK-guarded per-row probes (`node_child`, `_ast.node_hash`) under `foreign_keys=ON` produces ~2M probe operations to land ~150k unique rows.

They authored a 2-change surgical fix and left it in a worktree for LLO to review + land.

## Worktree location

`/private/tmp/llo-merkle-perf` — branch `perf/merkle-bulk-load` off `ir-producer-7ed023`.

## The 2-change fix

1. **Cross-file dedup in memory at the flush loop** — `HashSet<[u8;32]>` for `node_content` hashes and `HashSet<([u8;32], i64)>` for `node_child` (parent, ordinal) pairs. Buffers only push unique rows, so SQL sees ~150k rows instead of ~2M. Kills the probe storm at the source. The `INSERT OR IGNORE` prefix stays as a byte-identical-file backstop but the storm is gone.

2. **`PRAGMA defer_foreign_keys=ON` after BEGIN** — FKs validated once at COMMIT instead of per-row probes into `node_content`'s BLOB PK. Still fail-loud (a dangling edge aborts the COMMIT).

Both are textbook "don't maintain a unique index + FKs during a bulk load" — the fix is old + well-understood.

## Measured impact (from mache LLM's session)

Merkle-branch cold parse was 1.4-2.8× slower than main (2× on full repo, 371 files). After fix: expected to close most of that gap on the merkle-specific work; the remaining baseline is what bead `ley-line-open-5ff6aa` addresses (pointer store reframe).

## Deeper design note (worth capturing but NOT this bead's scope)

Mache LLM noted: `node_content` on the merkle branch is literally a content-addressed BLOB store — git-object model, hash → object, dedup intrinsic to write. And LLO already has this primitive: `substrate.rs`'s `BlobStore` (the CA/IM/CR axioms). The merkle branch reinvented a worse CAS as a SQLite table with fake unique-index-dedup, next to the real one.

That framing belongs in bead `ley-line-open-5ff6aa` (pointer-store reframe) — the merkle branch's `node_content` should arguably BE the `BlobStore`, not a hand-rolled BLOB-PK table. Filing this note here for provenance; the reframe is 5ff6aa's design deliverable.

## Close condition

- Diff reviewed for correctness (in-memory dedup semantics match `INSERT OR IGNORE` semantics; deferred FK still catches dangling edges)
- `task ci` green on `perf/merkle-bulk-load` branch
- PR opened against `ir-producer-7ed023`, reviewed, merged
- Post-merge validation: instrumented cold-parse timing on merkle branch closes ≥80% of the gap to main baseline

## Provenance

- Mache LLM session 2026-07-07 diagnosed + patched. Handoff left in `/private/tmp/llo-merkle-perf`. This bead is the LLO tracking item.
- Base branch: `ir-producer-7ed023` (merkle IR work, ADR-0026 on that branch — note collision with LLO ADR-0026 filed on main this session for pointer store; resolve at merkle merge time by renumbering one).
- Related to `ley-line-open-5ff6aa` (pointer-store design bet — the deeper reframe of `node_content`).

comments.text:

## Validation

`task ci` green locally on `perf/merkle-bulk-load` twice — once before push (mache LLM's worktree), once after fetch confirmed branch was up-to-date with `feat/merkle-ast-ir`. PR-side CI didn't fire because the workflow gate is `pull_request: branches: [main]` — separate issue tracked below.

Branch state pre-merge:
- 1 commit ahead of `origin/feat/merkle-ast-ir` (the perf fix)
- 0 commits behind (no rebase needed)

## What landed

Two surgical changes to `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_parse.rs`:

1. Cross-file dedup via `HashSet<[u8;32]>` + `HashSet<([u8;32], i64)>` — buffers push only unique rows so SQL sees ~150k rows instead of ~2M. `INSERT OR IGNORE` stays as cross-generation backstop.
2. `PRAGMA defer_foreign_keys=ON` after BEGIN — FK checks batched to COMMIT instead of per-row probes into `node_content`'s BLOB PK.

Plus `server.json` regen to fix a stale version pin on the branch.

## Mache unblocked

Mache's B4/B6 (reader node_hash passthrough + fan-out guard) can now proceed against the real schema on the updated `feat/merkle-ast-ir` branch.

Closing.

title: Unified code-fact IR producer: ADR mirror + symbols/fact_edges/symbol_id/κ/unbound_facts (mache ADR-0023)
description:
## Why
This is the CRITICAL-PATH producer side of mache's unified code-fact IR (mache ADR-0023, bead mache-472f60). mache defines the IR + consumers, but `leyline parse` is the only place with source bytes + AST + LSP in hand simultaneously — so it MUST produce the normalized tables. Nothing on the mache side (byte-parity reader, containment closure, ratchet gate) can start until these tables exist.

Read mache ADR-0023 first: `/Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/mache/docs/adr/0023-unified-code-fact-ir.md` (full design + rationale + prior-art). Analysis log: `mache/_agent_log/theoretical-foundations-analyst_2026-07-05_agent_log.md`.

## Step 0 — ley-line-open ADR mirror
Write a ley-line-open ADR mirroring mache ADR-0023 for the producer contract (0013/0014 cross-repo pattern): the two table schemas, symbol_id construction, κ location, unbound_facts semantics, FK-integrity write-time guarantee. This is the wire/schema contract mache reads.

## Produce (materialized at parse time in cmd_parse.rs, keyed on symbol_id + gen)

**`symbols`** — one row per AST node, cross-language typed:
- `symbol_id BLOB` = `BLAKE3(source.contentHash ‖ canonical_span ‖ kind ‖ name)`; canonical_span normalized against the CONTENT HASH not the path. Inputs already exist: `_source.contentHash` (source.capnp), `range` (common.capnp), node_kind, name. **Path must NEVER enter the key** — this is the structural be6136 fix.
- cols: gen (head.capnp), source_id, node_id (locator, NOT a join key), kind (canonical), raw_kind (tree-sitter, retained), lang, name, span_start, span_end. PK (symbol_id, gen).

**`fact_edges`** — ONE generic typed-edge table (property graph):
- `src BLOB NOT NULL REFERENCES symbols(symbol_id)`, `dst BLOB REFERENCES symbols(symbol_id)` (NULL=unbound, counted), kind (contains|calls|references|defines|binds|imports|has_type|lineage), fidelity (mention|binding|reachability), gen, token, qualifier, span_start, span_end.
- **FK integrity load-bearing:** `PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON` at write → dangling ref = INSERT ERROR in leyline, not a zeroed row in mache. Map: _ast parent→child=contains; node_defs=defines(mention); node_refs=references/calls(mention,token); bindings=binds(binding,qualifier,resolved dst).

**`κ: (lang, raw_kind) → kind`** — cross-language collapse in leyline's language registry (mirror of mache internal/lang). Base closed set: function, method, type, field, variable, constant, module/file, import, parameter. Unmapped → kind=raw_kind. Go function_declaration / Rust function_item / Py function_definition → "function".

**`unbound_facts` counter in head.capnp** — legitimately-unresolvable ref (cross-repo, deleted mid-pass) → emit edge dst=NULL + increment a counter hashed into the Head. Makes "N facts failed to bind this gen" a first-class monotonic number mache's W5 ratchet gate asserts on (≤ baseline). be6136 would show as this jumping to ~100% → red gate on first CI run.

## Notes
- LSP hover/diagnostics → keyed on symbol_id (mache builds a symbol_attrs EAV; producer just needs symbol_id on them), NOT edges.
- Content-addressed identity CHANGES when bytes change (correct for a per-gen snapshot). Cross-edit tracking is a separate `lineage` edge from a diff pass — do NOT overload symbol_id.
- gen GC: keep current + previous (≥2 for lineage), config knob.

## Acceptance
- `leyline parse` emits symbols + fact_edges with FK enforced; a dangling edge fails the parse loudly.
- symbol_id byte-identical across parse runs for unchanged files.
- κ collapses the function kinds to "function"; unmapped pass through raw.
- head.capnp carries unbound_facts; a forced unresolved ref increments it.
- (downstream, mache side) mache re-expresses v_refs/v_defs over fact_edges with byte-parity vs current smell rules.

comments.text:
Starting implementation. Branch `ir-producer-7ed023` off main. Plan approved by team-lead. Incremental TDD commits:
(a) ley-line-open ADR mirror (docs/adr/) of mache ADR-0023 — producer contract.
(b) Compute contentHash (BLAKE3) in the parse pass — own commit; completes the T8.5 TODO at cmd_parse.rs L1318, and feeds symbol_id.
(c) `symbols` table + symbol_id = BLAKE3(contentHash ‖ canonical_span ‖ kind ‖ name) — PATH EXCLUDED (be6136 fix) + κ collapse map in ts/src/languages.rs.
(d) `fact_edges` table + PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON (fail-loud dangling edge) + contains/defines/references arms (fidelity=mention), unresolved ref → dst=NULL + `unbound_facts` counter in head.capnp.
Deferred to follow-up: `binds` edge arm (LSP-enrichment path, not in cmd_parse.rs) + full κ coverage across all languages.
Draft PR when foundational slice is tested; NO auto-merge (human review of symbol_id construction + FK path).

title: Daemon protocol: add a `version` op so consumers can handshake wire-compat on connect
description:
Requested by downstream (mache, session 2026-07-01). The leyline daemon socket has no `version` op today (verified against 0.5.x). Consumers can't verify at runtime that the daemon binary they're talking to is wire-compatible with their compiled schema-client — they rely on a human keeping two pins in lockstep.

Concrete case: mache pins `leyline-schema` (Go client) in go.mod AND `leylineBinaryVersion` as a const, and documents (internal/leyline/socket.go:735-766) that major.minor must match for wire compat while patch floats. mache wants to query the daemon's version on connect and refuse on major.minor mismatch (mache-8kif) but cannot, because no version op exists.

ASK: add a `version` (or `hello`/handshake) op returning at least the daemon's semver + wire/schema version, so any consumer can fail loud on skew instead of silently decoding a mismatched wire. Cheap, unauthenticated (localhost socket metadata).

Unblocks: mache-8kif (runtime handshake). Interim downstream guard already exists (mache-b8af69, build-time parity gate) but that can't catch a daemon swapped at runtime.

NOTE: file scope needs an LLO-side scoping pass (daemon op dispatch + socket protocol definition) — filed as design pending that.

comments.text:
CLOSING as already-done (LLO daemon side) — verified LLO main 2026-07-02. The leyline_version op is fully wired: registered as an MCP tool (daemon/mcp.rs:446) + in the wire op list (daemon/wire.rs:99), dispatched at daemon/ops.rs:174 (BaseRequest::LeylineVersion => op_leyline_version), handler at ops.rs:1720; version module daemon/version.rs (SCHEMA_VERSION=CARGO_PKG_VERSION, WIRE_FORMAT_MAJOR=1, COMPAT_MIN_SCHEMA_VERSION="0.4.1"); blackbox coverage in cli-lib/tests/version_handshake_blackbox_test.rs (asserts the daemon advertises correctly + idempotency + reachable-before-init).

The daemon (advertise) side is DONE. The ONLY remaining gap is the CLIENT side — mache calling the op and refusing on mismatch — tracked as mache-8kif (external-review M3). This LLO-side bead is superseded by that.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: (LLO-side) Grammar-derived property tests for LLO's gen'd surfaces (companion to mache-7qf0)
description:
## Why (companion to mache-7qf0)

mache filed an epic (`mache-7qf0`) capturing the PostHog SQL-parser insight: **property-based generation from the grammar + production-log shadow mode**. That epic targets mache's smell rules. The discipline applies equally to LLO's gen'd surfaces, but at a different layer.

LLO has two gen'd surfaces where grammar-derived testing would expose real bugs current hand-curated fixtures miss:

1. **capnp Rust↔Go codegen parity** — `clients/go/leyline-schema` is regenerated from `rs/ll-core/public-schema/capnp/daemon.capnp`. The Rust codegen and Go codegen pipelines are independent. A divergence between them would show up as a deserialization mismatch — mache reads a capnp segment LLO wrote and gets wrong values. We have no test for this today.
2. **Per-language HDC canonical-map kind coverage** — each language LLO supports has a canonical map (`rs/ll-open/hdc/src/canonical/`). The map IS the kind alphabet HDC encodes against. Kinds the canonical map doesn't recognize fall into a generic bucket — which means a language's HDC quality depends on canonical-map completeness. We've never measured coverage.

## What this bead is

The parent / epic capturing the LLO-side of the discipline. Child beads carry per-deliverable work:

- **capnp parity property tests** — generate valid capnp messages, serialize Rust + deserialize Go (and vice versa), assert byte-equality on round trip. Coverage-guided: prefer message shapes that exercise unions / lists / nested groups not yet seen.
- **Per-language canonical-map coverage report** — for each language, walk tree-sitter grammars; generate AST shapes covering the grammar's kind set; check each kind against the canonical map; report (covered / mapped to generic / orphan) metrics. Surface gaps as a CI artifact.

Out of scope for THIS bead (file separately if scoped):
- Built-in smell-rule fixture generation in mache (covered by `mache-7qf0.1`)
- External smell-rule fuzzer CLI in mache (covered by `mache-7qf0.2`)

## Acceptance for the epic

Both children resolved; an ADR (probably ADR-0026 if released numbering holds, otherwise next available) documents the discipline + how the generated fixtures gate substrate identity going forward.

## Prior art

- PostHog SQL-parser rewrite (2026): https://posthog.com/blog/sql-parser
- Hypothesis (Python property-based testing): https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/
- Sibling epic: mache-7qf0

## Why patch this even matters

ADR-0024's substrate-identity rewrite (v0.5.0) introduced the discipline of pre-registered substrate-property tests. ADR-0025 carries it forward for HDC compositional value. This bead is the same discipline applied to the gen'd boundaries: any cross-language or cross-codegen divergence is a substrate-identity break that today's tests don't catch.

title: (LLO-side) capnp Rust↔Go codegen parity property tests
description:
## What

Property-based round-trip parity tests between LLO's two capnp codegen outputs (Rust + Go). Goal: catch any divergence where Rust serializes a message that Go deserializes incorrectly (or vice versa).

## Implementation sketch

1. **Generator**: walks `rs/ll-core/public-schema/capnp/daemon.capnp` and emits valid messages per top-level type. Coverage-guided: prefer message shapes exercising unions, lists, nested groups, anyPointer fields not yet seen.
2. **Roundtrip test**: for each generated message, (a) serialize via Rust capnp, (b) deserialize via the Go `clients/go/leyline-schema` package, (c) re-serialize via Go, (d) deserialize via Rust, (e) assert deep equality with the original.
3. **Failure mode**: any divergence (field missing, value coerced wrong, union tag mismatch) is a fail with a minimal-reproducer message dump.
4. **CI integration**: cargo test target in `rs/ll-core/public-schema/tests/` + a `go test` in `clients/go/leyline-schema/`. Both must pass for substrate-identity to hold across runtime boundaries.

## Acceptance

- Test corpus covers every top-level message type in `daemon.capnp` (one entry per `struct` / `interface` at the top level).
- Coverage metric: % of fields in each message shape that have been exercised by at least one generated instance.
- Both Rust and Go test targets exist and run in `task ci`.
- A docs/research entry or appendix in ADR-0014 captures the discipline so future schema edits know to maintain parity.

## Dependencies

Depends on parent epic. No code dependency on sibling bead (canonical-map coverage).

## Out of scope

- Wire-protocol-level shadow mode (replay production traces) — phase 2 if we ever capture wire traces at scale
- Multi-version compatibility (capnp v0.25 vs v0.26 etc.) — separate concern handled by the exact-pin in `schema-capnp/Cargo.toml`

title: (LLO-side) Per-language HDC canonical-map kind coverage report
description:
## What

For each language LLO supports via tree-sitter (`leyline-ts`), produce a coverage report measuring how completely the corresponding HDC canonical map (`rs/ll-open/hdc/src/canonical/`) covers the language's AST kind set.

## Why it matters

The canonical map IS the kind alphabet HDC encodes against. Kinds present in the language's grammar but missing from the canonical map fall into a generic catch-all bucket — which silently degrades HDC encoding quality for that kind of node. We have no way today to know, per language, what fraction of the grammar's kinds the canonical map actually distinguishes.

This bead generates the measurement so we can prioritize canonical-map gap-fill (or accept the gaps explicitly).

## Implementation sketch

1. **Kind enumeration per language**: walk each language's tree-sitter grammar metadata to list every node kind the parser emits. Tree-sitter grammars expose this via `Language::node_kind_for_id` over the full kind range.
2. **Canonical-map probe**: for each kind, ask the canonical map "do you recognize this?" Result is a tri-state: (recognized + mapped to a non-generic canonical), (recognized but mapped to generic), (unrecognized).
3. **Generated-shape augmentation**: for kinds that ARE recognized, generate sample AST shapes containing that kind (grammar-driven), encode via HDC, verify the encoded HV distinguishes from other-kind shapes. Catches "mapped but encoder collapses" cases.
4. **Report**: per-language markdown table with kind / canonical-mapping / encoding-distinguishability columns. Surface as CI artifact + commit a snapshot under `docs/research/canonical-coverage/`.

## Acceptance

- One coverage report per supported language under `docs/research/canonical-coverage/<lang>.md`, regenerated by a task.
- Per-language coverage % is documented in each canonical map's source file header comment (`rs/ll-open/hdc/src/canonical/rust.rs` etc.).
- CI runs the report regeneration on every PR touching `rs/ll-open/hdc/src/canonical/` and fails if the snapshot drifts vs the regenerated output without an explicit doc update.

## Dependencies

Depends on parent epic. Sibling bead (capnp parity) is independent.

## Out of scope

- Cross-language transfer (Rust canonical map should NOT cover Go kinds; coverage is reported per-language)
- HDC encoder changes if coverage exposes gaps — that's its own bead per language
- HDC compositional surface (ADR-0025 Phase α-δ) — different axis entirely

title: [design] LLO↔cloister boundary: anything named `leyline-*` lives in LLO; cloister has bridge crates
description:
## Strategic question

Where does the LLO ↔ cloister boundary actually fall, beyond the leyline-sign instance? The substrate of the question is:

- **LLO** = data-plane primitives (Σ substrate, parse, HDC, FFI, crypto, file-backed arena). Libraries, no daemons, no wire protocols.
- **Cloister** = agent-execution + network topology (workerd, Durable Objects, vault, BeadStore, TrustStore, BlobStore, CredentialVault, agent dispatch). Runtime layer that CONSUMES LLO primitives.

The recurring smell is that things named `leyline-*` end up in cloister with cloister-specific code baked in. The pattern that works (already proven by `cloister-cas` consuming `leyline-cas-ffi`) is **thin cloister bridge crates that depend on LLO primitives**.

## Proposed principle

> Anything named `leyline-*` lives in LLO. Cloister has bridge crates named `cloister-*` that depend on LLO primitives over a path/published-crate dependency, never as a fork or symlink-plus-extensions.

## Test cases for the principle

- **leyline-sign** → instance bead `cloister-59c60e` + `ley-line-open-5a06e9`. The verifier + signing library belongs in LLO; the daemon + cloister URI grammar belongs in cloister as `cloister-sign-host`.
- **leyline-cas-ffi / cloister-cas** → already correctly shaped, cited as the reference pattern.
- **LSP backend** → already removed from cloister (the right call). Was a cloister-hosted consumer of an LLO ingestion pass; not the right home.
- **Future crypto/identity primitives** (per cloister ADR-0010/0011/0013 trust slices) — same boundary question.

## Out of scope (for this design bead)

- The leyline-sign-specific fix (tracked in `cloister-59c60e` + `ley-line-open-5a06e9`).
- The LSP cleanup (the doc deletion was a follow-on to the code removal that already happened).

## Acceptance criteria

- A cross-repo design doc or ADR (in LLO, with cloister cross-link) that states the boundary principle, the audit pattern, and the migration approach for any existing violations.
- A list of current violations (anything named `leyline-*` in cloister beyond bridge crates) with disposition (rename, move to LLO, or accept as documented exception).
- The principle is cited going forward as the rule for naming new crates.

## Cross-repo thread

Companion bead in cloister: filed alongside this one. The pair forms a cross-repo design thread; resolution requires alignment in both repos.

## Related

- `cloister-59c60e` (instance: leyline-sign drift)
- `ley-line-open-5a06e9` (instance: LLO host-feature evaluation)
- `cloister-bd8c41` (prior closed sync attempt — predates this design question)
- Cloister ADR-0019 (sign-only helper) — needs amendment if principle is adopted
- LLO README architecture diagram — naming pattern reference

title: Evaluate `[features] host` on leyline-sign to absorb cloister's host extras
description:
## Context

Cloister has a hard fork of `leyline-sign` at `cloister/rs/crates/sign/src/` that adds:
- `src/bin/` — `leyline-sign-helper` daemon (ADR-0019 cloister-side)
- `src/host/` — axum/hyper server + `keyring` OS-keystore client
- `src/cert_chain.rs` — extended cert-chain validation

LLO's canonical at `rs/ll-open/sign/` is verifier-only. The fork came from ADR-0019 (cloister-side, accepted 2026-05-12) telling cloister to extend its local sign crate — three days AFTER the lift to LLO canonical. Companion bead: `cloister-*` (filed alongside this one).

## Question to answer

Should LLO's `leyline-sign` grow a `[features] host = ["axum", "hyper", "keyring", ...]` feature that absorbs cloister's host extras?

### Arguments for

- Stops the fork at the source. Cloister symlinks to LLO (matching ll's pattern). One canonical crate.
- The "verifier-only on wasm32, full host on native" pattern is the natural shape for a sign crate. Many consumers will want both.
- ADR-0019 cited `#[cfg(all(feature = "host", not(target_arch = "wasm32")))]` as the gate — that mechanism is identical whether host code lives in LLO or cloister.

### Arguments against

- LLO's license model is mixed; cloister's host extras are AGPL-3.0+ today. Absorbing the host code into LLO either drags the AGPL boundary inward or requires a relicense/dual-license discussion.
- The `keyring` + `axum` + `hyper` dep graph grows LLO's host-feature surface significantly. Even feature-gated, this is build-time + audit-time weight.
- The `leyline-sign-helper` binary is a daemon — it's not a library extension, it's a separate runtime. Lives more naturally next to cloister's runtime concerns.

### Open question

ADR-0019's cloister-2a0faa invariant requires the wasm artifact stay byte-identical across feature changes. If LLO grows `[features] host`, the wasm32 build path must remain provably unchanged — this is testable via the `sha256 = 653eae67e682cb…` golden vector.

## Acceptance criteria for this evaluation bead

- Decision documented: either accept the host feature (and file an implementation bead) or reject (and file a `cloister-sign-host` separate-crate alternative in cloister).
- License implications stated explicitly.
- Wasm-artifact byte-identity invariant preserved either way.
- Companion `cloister-*` bead is unblocked by the answer here.

## Related

- Cloister companion: `cloister-*` (drift report)
- ADR-0019 (sign-only helper protocol, cloister)
- Lift commit: ll `3d805a6` ("canonical lives in ll-open")
- Prior cloister sync: `cloister-bd8c41` (closed; predates ADR-0019)

comments.text:
## Update from cloister-side audit (2026-06-24) — decision: host extras stay cloister-side, do NOT move to LLO

Per-file audit of cloister's vendored `leyline-sign` ratifies the cleanest scope split: cloister's `bin/` (leyline-sign-helper daemon, ADR-0019 cloister-side) and `host/` (8 files, ~125KB: allowlist, auth, cache, health, keystore, ratelimit, server, sign) are **cloister-specific bridge concerns**, not generic leyline-sign features. They implement cloister's lease-pipeline + helper-protocol; LLO has no use for them.

Cloister-side ADR ratifying the principle: [`cloister/docs/adr/0035-cloister-llo-boundary.md`](../../../../cloister/docs/adr/0035-cloister-llo-boundary.md) — explicit decision: bridge crates stay in cloister; leyline-* names belong in LLO; naming follows the implementation.

**Recommendation: close this bead as "won't fix — bridge concerns stay cloister-side"**. The two sibling beads that DO want upstream PRs (`ley-line-open-4ad9da` for wasm32 FFI exports, `ley-line-open-4b267d` for signingTime gating) cover the entire convergence surface; once those land, cloister deletes its vendored `cert.rs`/`cms.rs`/`error.rs`/`ffi.rs`/`lib.rs`/`oid.rs` and keeps only `cert_chain.rs` + `bin/` + `host/` as cloister bridge code (renaming the crate to `cloister-sign` is a follow-up cleanup).

Coordinated-PR plan + the cloister-side post-merge work is documented at [`docs/problems/leyline-sign-wasm32-convergence.md`](../problems/leyline-sign-wasm32-convergence.md) (staged in this repo today). The "After all three land + LLO releases" section there is the cloister-side closeout.

Needed for ASTWalker language parity to cover HCL/Terraform so mache's SitterWalker (CGO) can be retired for those languages too. Not on the core (Go) CGO-removal critical path — extends it. DEPENDENCY CHAIN — confirmed from LLO side (2026-06-21).

Pairs with mache-d5e158 ("Consume LLO HCL/Terraform TreeSitterPass when shipped"). Extends mache's pure-Go ASTWalker parity beyond Go to cover HCL/Terraform, allowing mache's SitterWalker (CGO) to be retired for those languages too.
