title: Local integration recipe: compose.dev.yaml pairing llo-daemon + mache for end-to-end testing
description:
Filed 2026-05-12 after PR #12's image:smoke validated the daemon runs end-to-end. P2 task. Surfaces the gap: cloister's `cluster.compose.yaml` orchestrates cloister-router + notme + mache + rosary but LLO isn't in it — so there's no single command that brings up the canonical mache-talks-to-LLO integration.

## Why this is the right shape

Today's validation paths:
- `task image:smoke` — builds image, starts daemon, hits MCP `tools/list`. Validates the daemon binary boots. Doesn't validate the daemon-↔-mache integration.
- mache's own tests — validate mache's UDS client logic against mocked daemon responses.
- Cross-runtime fixture gate (b5a77b) — validates schema-↔-handler agreement at protocol-shape level, not runtime integration.

What's missing: **one command that brings up a real LLO daemon, a real mache container, points mache at LLO's UDS, parses a small fixture repo, and exercises every base op.** That's what the user wishes for ("I almost want a docker container that spins up mache + LLO etc so we can run it that way").

## Scope

Ship a `compose.dev.yaml` (or `Taskfile.yml` target — `task cluster:dev`) at the LLO repo root that:

1. **Brings up an LLO daemon container** (uses the existing `ley-line-open:0.2.1` image) with a tmpfs volume for arena + ctrl files, MCP HTTP on `:8384` (host) and UDS socket exposed via a named volume.
2. **Brings up a mache container** (uses `mache:0.8.x`) with the LLO UDS volume mounted, pointed at LLO via env (`LLO_UDS_PATH=/run/llo/default.sock` or similar).
3. **Parses a fixture repo** — small enough to be embedded in the repo (say `tests/fixtures/sample-repo/` with a few Go/Rust files) — via a one-shot `leyline parse` invocation inside the LLO container or via the `reparse` op against MCP.
4. **Smoke-tests the integration**: a `task cluster:smoke` target that brings up the cluster, runs a query through mache (which goes mache → UDS → LLO), and asserts the response is well-formed.

## What this is NOT

- Not a replacement for `task image:smoke` (that tests LLO standalone).
- Not the same as cloister's `cluster.compose.yaml` (which is cloister-shaped — workerd tenants + cloister-router). This is LLO-shaped — two services, no cloister required.
- Not production deployment scaffolding. Dev/test only. Production users would compose differently.

## Coordination

- mache version: pin to whichever mache release consumes the current LLO daemon protocol. After PR #12 (capnp-json wire), mache needs the `clients/go/leyline-schema/v0.3.0` decoder updates (Int64-as-string). So `compose.dev.yaml` pins `mache:0.8.x+` once that lands.
- Could optionally add cloister-router as a third service (LLO ↔ mache via cloister) — but that's gold-plating; two-service is enough for the integration story.

## Files

- `compose.dev.yaml` (new — root of LLO repo)
- `Taskfile.yml` (new `cluster:dev` and `cluster:smoke` targets)
- `tests/fixtures/sample-repo/` (new — small embedded repo for the parse target)
- `README.md` (Getting Started section — single-command integration)

## Acceptance

- `task cluster:dev` brings up llo-daemon + mache, both healthy
- `task cluster:smoke` parses sample-repo, queries via mache, asserts shape
- Documented in README's Getting Started section as the canonical "see it work" command
- mache + LLO version compatibility documented (compose pins ensure the pair is known-good)

## Related

- Bead `ley-line-open-b0ea2e` (PR #12) — needs to land + Go bindings v0.3.0 tagged before mache can consume the new wire
- Bead `ley-line-open-40df83` — dual-codec wire; eventually `compose.dev.yaml` exercises the binary path too
- Cloister's `cluster.compose.yaml` — pattern reference for orchestration shape (LLO version is simpler — no workerd, no service bindings)

comments.text:
**Closing — re-filed as `mache-cafce9` in the mache repo.**

Architectural reconsideration: LLO is a substrate; the compose recipe for "how to run mache against LLO" belongs on the consumer side. LLO's own validation already lives at `task image:smoke` (single-service, substrate-local — the right shape).

The original scaffold work (PR #14, now closed) is referenced from `mache-cafce9` as a starting point — compose.dev.yaml + Taskfile cluster targets + sample-repo fixture all sketched out. Adapt to mache's conventions.

What survives on the LLO side from PR #14:
- `tests/fixtures/sample-repo/` — useful tree-sitter parse input regardless of orchestration. Re-filing as a small focused follow-up PR.

See:
- PR #14 close comment for the architectural rationale
- `mache-cafce9` for the actual implementation work
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: Dual-codec daemon UDS wire: binary capnp + JSON via magic-byte dispatch
description:
Filed 2026-05-12 after b0ea2e (capnp-json wire codec) lands. P2 feature. Natural step 4 in the daemon-typed-wire decade.

## Why this is the right shape

After b0ea2e, `daemon.capnp` is the typed contract and `wire.rs` is gone — every handler builds a `capnp::message::Builder` and calls `capnp_json::to_json(reader)` to emit. The encoding is now a one-line swap per handler:

```rust
// JSON (post-b0ea2e):  capnp_json::to_json(reader)
// Binary:              capnp::serialize::write_message_to_words(reader)
```

The hard part (typed Builders, schema-as-SSOT, fixture gate) is done. Dual-codec is a pure carrier-layer change.

## What we'd gain with binary on the wire

- True ZC on receive — mache could borrow segment bytes directly, same way it consumes substrate segment files today
- No Int64-as-string awkwardness
- No "generation/last_reparse_at_ms emit `"0"` forever because UInt64 default is 0" issue
- No `enrichment` double-encoding (open-ended JSON inside Text)
- Faster — skip the parse step
- Homogeneous encoding — substrate and wire use the same canonical capnp bytes

## What we'd lose (preserved via dual-codec)

- Shell debuggability (`nc -U default.sock <<< '{"op":"status"}'`) — kept via the JSON path
- MCP HTTP transport stays JSON (external MCP spec) — naturally routes to JSON codec
- mache JSON path keeps working — opt-in to binary incrementally

## Implementation sketch

**Magic-byte dispatch** in `socket.rs`:
- capnp segment format starts with a 32-bit segment count (typically `0x00 0x00 0x00 0x00` or `0x01 0x00 0x00 0x00`) — never `{`
- JSON line-delimited starts with `{` (or whitespace+`{` for pretty input)
- Server peeks first byte, routes to JSON or capnp handler
- ~50 lines in `socket.rs`

**Handler finalizer switch**: each handler today calls `capnp_json::to_json(reader)`. Refactor to return the typed `Reader` instead of a serialized String; the dispatcher picks the finalizer:

```rust
match codec {
    Codec::Json => capnp_json::to_json(reader)?,
    Codec::Binary => {
        let mut out = Vec::new();
        capnp::serialize::write_message(&mut out, &builder)?;
        // length-prefix or stream-framed
        out
    }
}
```

~3 lines per handler; the Builder construction is shared.

**Framing nuance**: JSON is line-delimited (`\n` terminator). Binary capnp needs explicit length prefix OR capnp's stream-framed format (segment-table prefix). Pick one and document.

**Fixture gate extension**:
- Keep `daemon-protocol.json` with JSON `response` examples per op
- Add `tests/fixtures/daemon-protocol/<op>.bin` files (T8.10 substrate pattern, already known)
- Both Rust + Go gates decode both formats; assertion is "produces same typed struct"

## Acceptance

- `task ci` green with both codecs exercised
- `echo '{"op":"status"}' | nc -U <ctrl.uds>` still produces parseable JSON (back-compat)
- A new `--codec=binary` daemon flag (or per-connection negotiation) routes binary capnp on UDS
- mache client demonstrates binary path with measurable round-trip-time improvement
- README + ADR-0014 status section updated to reflect both codecs supported; binary is preferred for typed consumers, JSON for shell/debug/MCP-HTTP

## Forcing functions to watch

- mache files a bead asking for typed end-to-end (Int64 string-parsing friction, decode latency on hot loops)
- Profiling shows UDS encode/decode is non-trivial fraction of a daemon round trip (currently it isn't — UDS is control-plane, not hot path)
- A new typed consumer arrives (cloister `udsForward` triggers this naturally per cloister ADR-0005 §"Internal wire = leyline-net")
- Anyone reports the "generation: '0' forever" or Int64-as-string as actually painful

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- Changing the MCP HTTP transport (it's JSON-RPC by external spec — can't change)
- Dropping JSON support (preserves shell debugging)
- Schema changes — `daemon.capnp` is the unchanged SSOT; only the carrier changes

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/socket.rs` (magic-byte dispatch)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs` (handler finalizer split)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/fixtures/daemon-protocol/` (binary fixture files)
- `clients/go/leyline-schema/daemon/daemon_protocol_test.go` (binary decode path)
- `docs/adr/0014-capnp-as-protocol.md` (status section update)
- `README.md` (daemon protocol section: document both codecs)

## Related

- Bead: `ley-line-open-b0ea2e` (the post-3-step migration this builds on)
- Bead: `ley-line-open-b07a79` (the `op_query` structural skip — dual-codec doesn't fix it; that's separate)
- Decade: `daemon-typed-wire` (step 4)
- Thread: `daemon-json-type-lockdown` (this bead is the natural sequel — call it the wire-carrier-evolution thread once filed)

title: wire.rs codegen: emit typed serde structs from daemon.capnp at build time (A.1a path from `a373ba`)
description:
Filed 2026-05-12 after A-3 (`b69606`) landed. The original tracking bead `a373ba` had two implementation paths:

- **A.1a (preferred):** `build.rs` codegen — `rs/ll-core/public-schema/build.rs` emits serde-Serialize/Deserialize structs from `daemon.capnp` alongside the Cap'n Proto Rust bindings.
- **A.1b (fallback):** hand-written serde mirror in `daemon/wire.rs` + cross-runtime fixture gate.

A-3 shipped A.1b. This bead is the follow-up that takes us from A.1b to A.1a, closing the "wire.rs is hand-written; schema↔wire parity is enforced at test/CI time, not compile time" gap that PR #10's Copilot review surfaced.

## Why this matters

- **Compile-time enforcement.** Adding a field to `daemon.capnp` would either auto-flow into the serde structs (schema-driven codegen) or fail the build (handler doesn't match the new shape). Today the same change requires a manual `wire.rs` edit; if missed, the fixture gate catches it at test/CI time but `cargo check` is happy.
- **Eliminates one source of doc drift.** The current ADR-0014 interim-status paragraph + Go-module README + top-level README all carry caveats about wire.rs being hand-written. Codegen removes the caveats.
- **Scales as the typed surface grows.** 17 of 23 base ops are typed today. Each new op is a wire.rs PR. With codegen, each new op is a daemon.capnp PR + auto-generated wire.rs.

## What needs investigating

1. **Toolchain.** Options surveyed in `a373ba`:
   - `capnp-conv` / `capnp-derive` — third-party crates that derive serde for capnp-generated types. Maturity unknown; license fit unknown; need to read the source before pinning.
   - Custom build.rs that parses `daemon.capnp` via the `capnp` Rust API and emits serde structs directly. Maximum control, maximum maintenance burden.
   - Two-stage: capnpc-rust emits the Cap'n Proto Rust bindings; a thin wrapper layer derives serde via `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]` on a manually-attribute-mapped facade. Splits the difference.

2. **JSON-as-carrier customizations.** wire.rs today has hand-applied attributes the codegen needs to handle:
   - `#[serde(rename = "snake_case")]` for camelCase→snake_case (capnp uses camelCase; JSON wire uses snake_case).
   - `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]` on `Node.record`, `StatusResponse.lastReparseAtMs`, etc.
   - `serde_json::Value` for open-ended payloads (`StatusResponse.enrichment`, `EnrichResponse.passes`).
   - The `BaseRequest` tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "op", rename_all = "snake_case")]`) — this is the request-side discriminator that has no capnp equivalent.

   Decision: which of these are codegen'd from schema annotations and which stay as a thin hand-written adapter layer?

3. **Carve-outs from typed surface.** Per `b07a79` and the LSP/vec_search untyped ops: codegen needs to either skip these or let hand-written mirrors coexist. wire.rs today is hand-written so coexistence is trivial; under codegen, the boundary needs to be explicit.

4. **The `Cap'n Proto schema is Rust-side`** boundary. Cloister, mache, and downstream Go consumers don't ingest Rust source — they consume `daemon.capnp` directly. So whatever codegen path we pick stays an LLO-internal compile-time concern.

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- Cross-runtime codegen unification (TS, Swift). The schema is already cross-runtime; the question here is only the *Rust serde mirror*.
- Code-level changes to handlers in `ops.rs`. Handlers consume the typed structs; if codegen produces structurally identical types, handler code is untouched.
- Solving `op_query`'s structural skip (`b07a79`). Codegen + the structural skip are independent.

## Acceptance

- `daemon.capnp` is the single source of truth for the typed surface; `wire.rs` either disappears or shrinks to a thin adapter that only encodes the JSON-carrier conventions (rename rules, skip-serializing).
- `task ci` green; the cross-runtime fixture gate stays as a defense-in-depth check but is no longer load-bearing.
- A handler trying to construct a typed struct with a field that doesn't exist in `daemon.capnp` is a compile error.
- README + ADR-0014 + Go module README can drop the "hand-written / fixture gate is load-bearing" caveats.

## Files

- `rs/ll-core/public-schema/build.rs` (codegen entry point)
- `rs/ll-core/public-schema/Cargo.toml` (build-deps: capnp-conv or chosen toolchain)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/wire.rs` (shrinks or disappears)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/Cargo.toml` (consume codegen output)
- `docs/adr/0014-capnp-as-protocol.md` (drop the interim-status caveat)
- `README.md` + `clients/go/leyline-schema/README.md` (drop hand-written caveat)

Decade: `daemon-typed-wire`. Thread: `daemon-json-type-lockdown` (the codegen closeout). Related: `a373ba` (parent — closed when A.1b shipped; this is the deferred A.1a path).

title: op_query structural skip — design call: reconcile rows shape vs schema's positional List(QueryRow)
description:
Filed 2026-05-12 after A-3 (`b69606`) landed. Follow-up on the one structural skip in the cross-runtime fixture gate. P2 design; "we'll fix this later" candidates harden if left undated.

## The mismatch

`daemon.capnp` declares:
```
struct QueryRow {
  values @0 :List(Text);
}
struct QueryResponse {
  ok @0 :Bool;
  columns @1 :List(Text);
  rows @2 :List(QueryRow);
}
```

Handler in `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs` emits:
```json
{"ok":true,"columns":["col_a","col_b"],"rows":[{"col_a":"v1","col_b":"v2"}]}
```

i.e. column-keyed maps rather than positional `values: [v1, v2]`. The fixture `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/fixtures/daemon-protocol.json` carries this as the one `go_drift_skip` entry (with the reason quoted in-line).

## Why it's structural, not additive

ADR-0014 §2 forbids removing/renaming ordinals. Three real options:

1. **Change handler to match schema** — emit `rows: [{values: [v1,v2,...]}, ...]`. Cleanest from a schema-discipline view, but breaks every existing debug consumer that does `row[colName]` — including the typed-Go fixture decoder and probably mache's own SQL-projection consumers. Wire-shape break.
2. **Add a parallel field at next ordinal** — e.g. `rowsMap @3 :List(Text)` carrying the JSON-encoded column-keyed shape. Additive, doesn't break consumers. Costs: schema now carries both shapes; "which one is canonical" becomes a documentation problem; consumer code has to pick a side.
3. **Document the carve-out** — keep handler + schema as-is, accept `query` lives outside the typed-mirror contract. This is what we're doing today, expanded on in the post-A-3 docs (PR #10). Lowest-effort but the gap calcifies.

## What I'd recommend (but it's a design call)

Option 1 if and only if we can do a coordinated debug-consumer flip (cloister's MCP query proxy + mache's SQL projection). Otherwise Option 3 stays the right answer for now, gated on the forcing function: the next consumer that wants typed query rows. File the bead, let it sit, reopen when triggered.

## Scope

- Decision artifact: short ADR addendum or a dated note in `docs/TABLE_CONTRACT.md`'s daemon-protocol section.
- If Option 1 picked: handler rewrite + fixture update + Go decoder switch + mache notify + cloister notify (if affected).
- If Option 2: schema bump + handler emits both + fixture covers both + Go decoder picks one + deprecation note for the column-keyed shape.
- If Option 3: nothing in code; doc-only commit clarifying the carve-out is durable.

## Files

- `rs/ll-core/public-schema/capnp/daemon.capnp` (QueryResponse / QueryRow ordinals)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs` (`op_query` handler)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/wire.rs` (no QueryResponse struct today — Option 1/2 would add one)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/fixtures/daemon-protocol.json` (the `query.go_drift_skip` reason)
- `clients/go/leyline-schema/daemon/daemon_protocol_test.go` (the Skip path)

## Forcing functions to watch for

- Cloister or another consumer files a "we need typed query rows" bead.
- A real bug surfaces where the column-keyed shape causes a decode error in production (e.g. column name with a slash, JSON-unsafe character).
- We start using `query` for non-debug paths (today it's nominally a debug escape hatch).

Decade: `daemon-typed-wire`. Thread: `daemon-json-type-lockdown` (closes out one of the two known carve-outs from A-3).

title: Auto-start leyline daemon on login (LaunchAgent / systemd user unit)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11. P2 chore. Real friction surfaced today.

## Problem

After `task install`, the leyline binary is on PATH but the daemon isn't running. Every workflow that needs the daemon (mache mounts, cloister MCP routing, anything via UDS) requires the user to manually:

```
leyline daemon --mcp-port 8384 --mcp-bind 0.0.0.0 &
```

Forget it = silent failures downstream. mache hangs on UDS connect. cloister returns empty tool results.

User observation today: "Why do I have to start it" — fair.

## Work

### macOS — LaunchAgent

Create `~/Library/LaunchAgents/sh.agentic.leyline.daemon.plist` with:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>Label</key><string>sh.agentic.leyline.daemon</string>
  <key>ProgramArguments</key>
  <array>
    <string>/Users/USER/.local/bin/leyline</string>
    <string>daemon</string>
    <string>--mcp-port</string><string>8384</string>
    <string>--mcp-bind</string><string>0.0.0.0</string>
    <string>--mcp-allow-public</string>  <!-- gated by future b7dd03; until then bare 0.0.0.0 -->
  </array>
  <key>RunAtLoad</key><true/>
  <key>KeepAlive</key><true/>
  <key>StandardOutPath</key><string>/tmp/leyline-daemon.log</string>
  <key>StandardErrorPath</key><string>/tmp/leyline-daemon.log</string>
</dict>
</plist>
```

Then: `launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/sh.agentic.leyline.daemon.plist`

Wire as a Taskfile target:

```yaml
install:agent:
  desc: "Install macOS LaunchAgent that auto-starts the daemon on login (idempotent)"
  cmds:
    - cp dist/launchagent/sh.agentic.leyline.daemon.plist ~/Library/LaunchAgents/
    - sed -i '' "s|USER|$(whoami)|g" ~/Library/LaunchAgents/sh.agentic.leyline.daemon.plist
    - launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/sh.agentic.leyline.daemon.plist 2>/dev/null || true
    - launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/sh.agentic.leyline.daemon.plist
    - echo "leyline daemon will auto-start on login; check /tmp/leyline-daemon.log"
```

Ship the plist template in `dist/launchagent/` (gitignored allowlist).

### Linux — systemd user unit

Parallel artifact at `dist/systemd/leyline-daemon.service`:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Leyline daemon

[Service]
ExecStart=%h/.local/bin/leyline daemon --mcp-port 8384 --mcp-bind 0.0.0.0
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

Taskfile target similar shape.

### task install hook

`task install` triggers `task install:agent` as a `deps:` step. Could be opt-in via `task install:full` if some users prefer manual control.

## Acceptance

- After `task install` on macOS, daemon auto-starts immediately and on every login
- `lsof -nP -iTCP:8384` shows the daemon listening
- `nc -U ~/.mache/default.sock <<< '{"op":"status"}'` returns a response without manual daemon launch
- `task uninstall:agent` (or similar) cleanly tears it down

## Why P2 not P1

P2 because the daily friction is real but the workaround (manual start) is trivial. Worth doing soon, not blocking anything.

title: Add get_schema daemon op (topology export for diagram/architecture)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11. T2 of decade `daemon-typed-wire`, thread `missing-ops`.

## Context

mache's `serve_diagram.go` type-asserts a `schemaProvider` interface to retrieve the schema topology (the cross-tier dependency map LLO ships with). `SQLiteGraph` implements it; `udsGraph` doesn't, so the diagram-by-name path silently degrades in `--control` mount.

Today the schema topology lives in mache itself (built from `Topology` at startup). But LLO is the owner of the source-of-truth dependency graph at parse time, so the daemon should be the one exposing it.

## Work

Add `get_schema` op that returns LLO's parsed schema topology — the cross-tier dependency map (ll-core → ll-open relationships, plus any extension layer DDLs registered via `DaemonExt`).

### Schema (`daemon.capnp`)

```capnp
struct GetSchemaRequest {}

struct SchemaTier {
  name   @0 :Text;        # "ll-core", "ll-open", extension-defined names
  crates @1 :List(Text);  # crate basenames owned by this tier
}

struct GetSchemaResponse {
  ok    @0 :Bool;
  tiers @1 :List(SchemaTier);
}
```

### Handler

`op_get_schema(ctx)` reads from a static topology source (likely `rs/ll-core/schema/` or a config blob). No DB query needed; schema is parse-time-known.

### Acceptance

- `get_schema` in `base_op_names`
- Returns the same shape mache's local `schemaProvider` returns today
- A-1 drift gate has `get_schema.json` fixture entry
- Integration test
- Regen Go bindings; task ci green

## Downstream

Mache `udsGraph.Schema()` implementation in a separate mache-side bead (gated on this LLO bead + tag).

## Why MEDIUM priority (not P1 like find_callees)

Diagram degrades silently — users don't see errors, just less informative output. Useful but not blocking the `--control` mount itself.

comments.text: Closing — shipped. `"get_schema"` is in `BASE_OP_NAMES` (`wire.rs:84`) with `op_get_schema` handler in `ops.rs`. Live as of v0.4.8.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: Add bulk-export ops: get_refs_map + get_defs_map (full token→[node_id] dumps)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11. T2 of decade `daemon-typed-wire`, thread `missing-ops`. Parallel to `a47d7d` (find_callees) — different files mostly, no dep between them.

## Context

Mache's `SQLiteGraph` exposes `DefsMap()` and `RefsMap()` — bulk snapshots of the entire `token → [node_id]` index. Multiple serve handlers consume these for graph-wide analysis:

- `serve_diagram.go` — Louvain community detection over the full refs graph
- `serve_handlers.go` — `find_definition` (defs map), `get_communities` (refs map)
- `serve_impact.go` — BFS seed enumeration from defs
- `serve_architecture.go` — both maps for module-level dependency analysis

`udsGraph` doesn't implement these methods because LLO has no daemon op that returns the full map in one call. The only existing token-lookup ops are `find_callers` and `find_defs`, which take ONE token and return its node refs. Iterating those across all tokens would be N daemon round-trips and isn't tractable.

## What's blocked today

In `--control` (read-only) mount, mache silently degrades:
- `get_diagram` returns no community grouping
- `get_communities` returns nothing
- `get_architecture` fails
- `get_impact` runs but with no seed candidates from defs

These regressions don't error out — type assertions for `refsMapProvider` / `defsMapProvider` just return `nil`, callers degrade. So this looks like "the daemon works fine" until you try one of these features.

## Why bulk-export, not raw SQL via `query` op

`op_query` exists and takes arbitrary SQL — mache COULD call `SELECT token, node_id FROM node_refs` to extract the map. But:

1. Per `docs/TABLE_CONTRACT.md`: *"Post-T8 (2026-05-08)... the cross-process contract moved to canonical-encoded capnp segment files at `${db}.{bindings,...}.capnp`. SQL column names are NOT the contract."*
2. Using raw SQL re-couples mache to LLO's internal table schema — exactly what T8.10 / ADR-0014 forbids.
3. Typed bulk-export ops are the right abstraction. Same modal-evidence frame as `find_callers` (Derived-from-the-living-db), just bulk.

## Work

### Schema (`daemon.capnp`)

Add two messages:

```capnp
struct TokenMapEntry {
  token   @0 :Text;
  nodeIds @1 :List(Text);
}

struct GetRefsMapRequest {}

struct GetRefsMapResponse {
  ok      @0 :Bool;
  entries @1 :List(TokenMapEntry);
}

struct GetDefsMapRequest {}

struct GetDefsMapResponse {
  ok      @0 :Bool;
  entries @1 :List(TokenMapEntry);
}
```

`TokenMapEntry` is reusable between refs and defs since their shape is identical (one token → many node_ids).

### Daemon handlers (`ops.rs`)

```rust
"get_refs_map" => Some(op_get_token_map(ctx, "node_refs", "refs")),
"get_defs_map" => Some(op_get_token_map(ctx, "node_defs", "defs")),
```

`op_get_token_map(ctx, table, json_key)` queries `SELECT token, node_id FROM <table>` once, groups in-memory by token, emits the typed response. Read-only (not in STATE_CHANGING_OPS).

### Tests + fixtures

- Integration test that parses a fixture, populates `node_refs` / `node_defs`, calls each new op, asserts the returned map shape
- Add `get_refs_map.json` / `get_defs_map.json` entries to `tests/fixtures/daemon-protocol.json` (the A-1 drift gate fixtures)

### Regen Go bindings

- `clients/go/leyline-schema/regen.sh` picks up the new schema types automatically
- The `leyline-schema-go` CI workflow's `git diff --exit-code` gate enforces regen-on-commit

## Acceptance

- `get_refs_map` and `get_defs_map` daemon ops in `base_op_names`
- `daemon.capnp` updated additively (per ADR-0014 §2 schema evolution rules)
- Go bindings regenerate cleanly
- A-1 drift gate (bead `b5a77b`) extends to cover the two new ops (with appropriate `go_drift_skip` set to null since the schema is being designed fresh — no drift possible)
- Integration test green
- `task ci` and `task image:smoke` green

## Downstream

Cross-repo bead in mache repo to implement `udsGraph.DefsMap()` / `RefsMap()` against the new ops. Filed AFTER this lands + LLO tagged. Same gating pattern as `a47d7d ↔ a5e4ea`.

## Why this is bigger than `find_callees`

`find_callees` is a near-trivial mirror of `find_callers` (same code path, different table). Bulk export is structurally different: returns a potentially large response (every token in the db). Want to gate the response size with a `limit` param or similar; will decide at implementation time. The audit notes this is "High effort — either bulk export or expose raw SQL, both require API stability decisions." Picking bulk-export side here.

comments.text: Closing — shipped. `"get_refs_map"` + `"get_defs_map"` are in `BASE_OP_NAMES` (`wire.rs:82-83`). Live as of v0.4.8.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: A-3: wire.rs typed-struct lockdown + 20-op handler swap
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T1.3 of decade `daemon-typed-wire`, thread `daemon-json-type-lockdown`. Depends on `b631c8` (schema reconciliation must land first so wire.rs has a stable target).

## Context

After A-1 (drift gate) and A-2 (schema reconciled), the daemon protocol contract is finally accurate. This bead makes the wire generation MECHANICAL via typed Rust structs, so future drift can only happen through deliberate schema bumps + corresponding wire.rs updates — all gated by the cross-runtime fixture test.

## Work

### A-3.1: wire.rs scaffolding
- Create `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/wire.rs`
- For each message in `daemon.capnp` (the reconciled version from A-2), define a Rust struct with `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]`
- Use `#[serde(rename = "...")]` to control JSON shape where Rust naming conventions differ from the JSON wire (e.g. capnp `currentRoot` → Rust struct field `current_root` → JSON field `current_root`)
- Tagged request enum: `enum Request { Status(StatusRequest), ListChildren(ListChildrenRequest), ... }` with `#[serde(tag = "op", rename_all = "snake_case")]` (or whichever serde shape matches the existing `{"op": "..."}` dispatch shape)

### A-3.2: ops.rs handler swap
For each op in `base_op_names`:
- Replace `json!({...})` builder with `let resp = ResponseStruct { ok: true, ... }; serde_json::to_string(&resp)?`
- Replace `req.get("foo").and_then(|v| v.as_str())` extraction with a typed request struct destructure (or use the tagged enum from A-3.1 if `socket.rs` does the parse)
- Same wire bytes; no behavior change
- Iterate per-op; the A-1 gate validates each conversion

### A-3.3: socket.rs typed dispatch
- Replace the `req.get("op").as_str()` extraction in `socket.rs` with `serde_json::from_str::<Request>(&line)` to deserialize the tagged enum
- The dispatch chain (event ops → base ops → extension async/sync → unknown) stays — just routes typed values instead of `&serde_json::Value`
- Error responses go through `ErrorResponse` typed struct

### A-3.4: Cross-runtime parity tests
- Each op gets a unit test in `tests/integration.rs`: build the typed `<Op>Response` struct → serialize via `serde_json::to_string` → compare byte-for-byte against the equivalent fixture from A-1
- If the comparison fails, the wire shape drifted — fix the struct, NOT the test
- The Go side (A-1's fixture decode test) continues to validate cross-runtime

### A-3.5: Drift-prevention enforcement
- The A-1 gate now catches three classes of drift:
  - Rust handler emits a field not in `daemon.capnp` → Go decode succeeds (extra fields tolerated) but the typed Rust struct refuses to serialize an unknown field — caught at compile time
  - `daemon.capnp` declares a field not emitted by a handler → Go decode may fail on the missing field if required (depending on JSON tag) — caught at test time
  - Schema and handler disagree on field SHAPE → Go decode raises `UnmarshalTypeError` — caught at test time
- Document the gate's coverage in `TABLE_CONTRACT.md`

## Acceptance

- No `json!({...})` calls remain in `ops.rs` for any op in `base_op_names` (audit via `cargo clippy -W ...` or a grep test)
- Every op handler emits its response via a typed `<Op>Response` struct
- All A-1 fixtures decode cleanly on the Go side
- `task ci` green
- `task image:smoke` green
- `nc -U` debuggability preserved (manual probe still returns parseable JSON)

## What's NOT in scope

- Migrating the wire encoding off JSON — explicitly deferred (bead `a4e860`)
- Adding new ops — Bead B (`a47d7d`) handles `find_callees` independently
- mache-side typed binding adoption — `mache-a5ad09`, separate timeline
- LSP/vec ops — included if they have schema entries from A-2; otherwise scope to base_op_names that DO have schema entries and file follow-up for any that don't

## Risk / rollback

- Wire encoding does NOT change. Byte-for-byte the existing JSON output is preserved (the typed structs serialize identically to current `json!({...})`).
- Rollback: revert the PR; existing tests cover the JSON wire shape.
- mache: no required change. Existing untyped consumers keep working.

comments.text:
A-3 complete (combined A-3.1 / A-3.2 / A-3.3 in one branch with A-2 / b631c8).

What landed:
- `daemon/wire.rs` — typed serde structs mirroring `daemon.capnp` (~20 message types) + `BaseRequest` tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "op", rename_all = "snake_case")]`) covering all 23 base ops (+`vec_search` under `#[cfg(feature="vec")]`).
- `daemon/ops.rs` — `handle_base_op(ctx, wire_line: &str) -> Option<String>` now decodes via `BaseRequest` and dispatches through an exhaustive `dispatch_typed` match. Two-stage decode keeps the unknown-op → None / known-op-bad-args → Some(error) contract that `socket.rs`'s chain-of-responsibility dispatch depends on. `is_known_base_op` is the production-side allow-list (test-side `base_op_names` mirrors it, drift-guarded by the existing sweep test).
- All 20+ `op_*` handlers swapped from `json!({...})` macros to typed struct construction (`StatusResponse`, `FlushResponse`, `LoadResponse`, `SnapshotResponse`, `ReparseResponse`, `EnrichResponse`, `ListChildrenResponse`, `ReadContentResponse`, `FindCallers/Defs/CalleesResponse`, `GetNodeResponse`, `GetRefs/DefsMapResponse`, `GetSchemaResponse`, `GetDbPathResponse`, `Lsp{Hover,Defs,Refs,Symbols,Diagnostics}` via shared helpers, `ErrorResponse`). Handler signatures now take typed args (e.g. `op_lsp_symbols(ctx, args: &LspFile)`), not `&Value` blobs.
- `daemon/socket.rs` — dispatch chain re-fuses `(op, req)` into the canonical wire line and routes through the typed `handle_base_op`. Event-emit semantics for state-changing ops preserved.
- `daemon/mcp.rs` — already on the wire-string signature from A-2; no change needed.
- Dead helper `parse_position` deleted. `parse_file_arg` / `required_str_field` moved to `#[cfg(test)]` (their unit tests still pin the error-message contract; production now rejects via serde).
- `handle_base_op_legacy(&ctx, op, &json!({...}))` `#[cfg(test)]` wrapper added so the existing 13 test sites that constructed args-as-Value pairs keep working unchanged.

Verification:
- `task ci` green — 0 failures across workspace (cli-lib lib 161 / integration 42 + every other crate). clippy `-D warnings` clean, fmt clean, tier-isolation clean.
- Go fixture gate: `GOWORK=off go test -count=1 ./daemon` — 14/14 ops decode into typed Go bindings (1 skip: `query`, structural drift tracked as follow-up per A-2 fixture doc).

Ready to commit + push as one PR covering A-2 + A-3 together.

title: Add find_callees daemon op (mirror find_callers, forward refs)
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T2 of decade `daemon-typed-wire`, thread `missing-ops`.

## Context

mache's `udsGraph.GetCallees` at `mache/cmd/uds_graph.go:172-174` currently returns `nil, nil` because there's no daemon-side `find_callees` op. LLO's `base_op_names` lists `find_callers` and `find_defs` but not `find_callees`.

`find_callers` at `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs:613-639` delegates to `op_find_token(ctx, req, "node_refs", "callers")`. The `node_refs` table is indexed by the *referenced* node. For `find_callees` (forward direction — given a node, what does it reference?) we need either a forward query against `node_refs` (if the schema supports it) or a separate `node_callees` table populated during the parser pass.

This bead is INDEPENDENT of Bead `a373ba` (typed wire lockdown). Both can land in parallel — `a373ba`'s typed-wire codegen will automatically pick up Bead B's new request/response messages once both are merged. Merge order doesn't matter.

## Work

### B.1: schema audit
- Read existing `node_refs` schema (`rs/ll-core/schema/`)
- Test whether forward queries work by filtering on a different column
- If forward queries work natively: no schema change needed
- Otherwise: add `node_callees` table + parser-pass population alongside `node_refs` (likely a one-line addition to the parse pass that emits both rows for every reference)

### B.2: capnp schema
- Add `FindCalleesRequest` / `FindCalleesResponse` to `rs/ll-core/public-schema/capnp/daemon.capnp` (mirror `FindCallersRequest` at lines 134-141)
- Regen `clients/go/leyline-schema/` Go bindings via `regen.sh`
- The `leyline-schema-go` CI workflow's regen-diff gate enforces no drift

### B.3: daemon op
- New `op_find_callees(ctx, req)` in `ops.rs` — calls `op_find_token(ctx, req, "<table>", "callees")` where `<table>` is whatever B.1 decided
- Add to `base_op_names` list at the top of `ops.rs`
- NOT a state-changing op — read-only — do NOT add to `STATE_CHANGING_OPS`

### B.4: integration test
- Parse a fixture with known A→B→C call chain
- Assert `find_callees(A)` returns B
- Assert `find_callees(B)` returns C
- Mirror via both wire encodings (today: JSON only; after `a373ba` lands: typed-JSON via cross-runtime gate)

## Acceptance

- `find_callees` accessible over UDS and MCP HTTP (both share dispatch via `handle_base_op`)
- Schema regen-diff CI gate passes
- Cross-runtime fixture decode green
- `task ci` green

## Downstream

- **mache repo, separate bead**: `udsGraph.GetCallees` becomes a one-liner against this op. **Gated on this bead landing + LLO tag pushed** (same cross-repo coordination pattern as `ley-line-open-41867b ↔ mache-3bf414`).

## Why this is T2, parallel to T1

Bead A (`a373ba`) is about HOW JSON is generated. Bead B is about WHAT ops exist. They touch different concerns. Files barely overlap (`ops.rs` is shared but the edits are additive — A swaps `json!()` builders, B adds a new handler). Merging in either order works.

title: Refactor: replace 11-arg run_daemon with DaemonConfig struct
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T1.1 of decade `daemon-shape-debt`, thread `daemon-config-refactor`.

## Problem

`rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs::run_daemon` takes 11 parameters:

```rust
pub async fn run_daemon(
    arena: &Path,
    arena_size_mib: u64,
    control: Option<&Path>,
    mount: Option<&Path>,
    backend: &str,
    nfs_port: u16,
    language: Option<&str>,
    timeout: Option<&str>,
    ext: Arc<dyn DaemonExt>,
    source: Option<&Path>,
    mcp_port: Option<u16>,
    mcp_bind: Option<std::net::IpAddr>,  // I just added this in v0.2.1, now 12
) -> Result<()>
```

It carries `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]`. Every new feature adds another parameter. The v0.2.1 `--mcp-bind` work bumped it from 11 to 12 and required threading through 4 sites. This is a code smell that compounds.

`cmd_daemon` (the open-edition wrapper) takes the same shape. `main.rs` constructs the call. Tests construct it differently. Each addition is a 4-file change.

## Work

1. Define `DaemonConfig` struct in `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs`:
   ```rust
   pub struct DaemonConfig<'a> {
       pub arena: &'a Path,
       pub arena_size_mib: u64,
       pub control: Option<&'a Path>,
       pub mount: Option<&'a Path>,
       pub backend: &'a str,
       pub nfs_port: u16,
       pub language: Option<&'a str>,
       pub timeout: Option<&'a str>,
       pub source: Option<&'a Path>,
       pub mcp_port: Option<u16>,
       pub mcp_bind: Option<std::net::IpAddr>,
       pub ext: Arc<dyn DaemonExt>,
   }
   ```
2. Or — given the lifetime juggling — use owned types (`PathBuf`, `String`) with `Default` impl + builder
3. Refactor `run_daemon(config: DaemonConfig)` and `cmd_daemon` (the open-edition wrapper)
4. Update `main.rs` Cmd::Daemon arm to construct DaemonConfig from clap args
5. Update tests in integration.rs that call run_daemon
6. Update `ley-line` (private edition) — heads-up to the user, since this is a breaking API for ll-private's daemon entry point. May need a coordinating PR over there.

This is a mechanical refactor — NO behavior change. PR description should explicitly say "no functional change; pure shape refactor."

## Acceptance

- `run_daemon(config: DaemonConfig)` and `cmd_daemon(config: DaemonConfig)` — single arg
- `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` removed
- `main.rs` and integration.rs updated
- `task ci` green; `task image:smoke` green (behavior unchanged)
- Cross-repo: `ley-line` (private) updated in a coordinating PR (filed as a follow-up bead in ll-private if the user wants me to)

## Why this is T1.1

Lowest-risk, highest-leverage. Subsequent refactors (T1.2 feature-gate registry, T1.3 lifecycle module decomposition) become easier once the function signature isn't a wall of args.

title: Implement chosen daemon transport convergence (per b9b6e3 ADR)
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T2.3 of decade `daemon-trust-and-transport`, thread `daemon-transport-convergence`. Depends on `b9b6e3` (the convergence ADR).

## Work

Concrete shape depends on the ADR outcome. This bead is the implementation slot — the ADR will overwrite the description with specifics when it lands.

Likely shape (assuming Option B — peers sharing a registry — wins, since it's the lowest-disruption answer):

1. Define `OpRegistry` (or `DispatchTable`) — a single map from op-name to handler, with shared arg/result types
2. Refactor `daemon/socket.rs` to call into the registry instead of inlining handlers
3. Refactor `daemon/mcp.rs::handle_post` to do the same
4. Both transports become pure I/O (parse-bytes → registry call → encode-result)
5. New ops added to the registry, not to either transport — both surfaces pick it up automatically
6. Cross-runtime test: the same op called via UDS and via MCP HTTP returns equivalent results (modulo encoding)

If the ADR picks Option A or C, this bead's plan changes substantially — re-scope at that time.

## Acceptance

- Concrete depends on ADR. Generally:
  - One source of truth for op contracts
  - All previously-divergent ops (from `b93871`) are either reconciled or explicitly justified as transport-specific
  - Both transports' dispatch is exercised by tests
  - `task ci` green; `task image:smoke` still passes
  - CHANGELOG entry

## Why this is T2.3

Implementation that closes the thread. After this lands, daemon transport surface is no longer drifting silently.

title: ADR: pick daemon transport convergence strategy
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T2.2 of decade `daemon-trust-and-transport`, thread `daemon-transport-convergence`. Depends on `b93871` (the audit — its output is the input to this decision).

## Decision needed

Given the audit results from `b93871`, pick one:

### Option A: Fold UDS into MCP — single transport
- MCP HTTP becomes the canonical surface; UDS becomes thin shim that does HTTP-over-UDS (or removed entirely if UDS-specific consumers have all migrated)
- Pro: one dispatch table, one place to add ops, one place to add auth
- Con: HTTP overhead on every UDS call (small, but present); breaks any consumer hardcoded to the binary UDS protocol
- Compatible with: cloister, any MCP-compatible client. Hostile to: legacy local tools.

### Option B: Keep both as peers, share op registry
- Define op contracts in one place (a trait, a registry, or capnp schema)
- Both transports call into the registry; transports are pure I/O
- Pro: keeps UDS's wire efficiency; consumers of either transport see identical ops
- Con: transport-specific quirks (auth, framing, error reporting) live in two places

### Option C: Specialize each — UDS for admin, MCP for tool-call
- Explicitly diverge: UDS exposes ops cloister/operators need (status, snapshot, restart-pass); MCP exposes only the tool-call surface AI clients want (parse, query, enrich)
- Pro: each surface is justified by its consumer; no pretense of "same thing"
- Con: doubles the contract maintenance; auth model has to handle both

## Inputs to weigh
- Audit table from `b93871`
- Cloister's actual consumption pattern (does it use UDS at all today, or only MCP HTTP?)
- Any other consumers of the UDS transport (mache? leyline-private? ad-hoc scripts?)
- Auth model from `b8395d` — does the chosen auth scheme apply to both transports cleanly?

## Acceptance

- `docs/adr/00XX-daemon-transport-convergence.md` written
- Decision recorded with rationale
- Falsifiable claim ("if we picked wrong, X will happen by Y")
- Implementation pointer to T2.3 bead

## Why this is T2.2

The audit makes the inventory legible; this turns it into a decision. Implementation (T2.3) follows.

title: Audit: UDS socket vs MCP HTTP dispatch — confirm or list divergences
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T2.1 of decade `daemon-trust-and-transport`, thread `daemon-transport-convergence`.

## Context

`cmd_daemon.rs`'s docstring claims UDS socket and MCP HTTP "share the same dispatch table" — different transports, identical ops surface. This bead tests that claim.

`daemon/socket.rs` (UDS) and `daemon/mcp.rs` (HTTP) both call into the same op registry, AFAICT. But:
- The MCP transport speaks JSON-RPC (`tools/list`, `tools/call`)
- The UDS transport speaks... a length-prefixed binary protocol? raw JSON? capnp? (haven't read it carefully)
- If they truly share dispatch, they should expose the same set of operations with the same arg/result types

If they're not identical, that's either:
- A latent bug (an op that works on UDS but not MCP, or vice versa)
- An intentional divergence (e.g. UDS exposes admin ops MCP doesn't)
- An unintentional divergence (MCP added without porting all UDS ops, or vice versa)

We can't make a transport-convergence decision (T2.2) without knowing the current truth.

## Work

Read-only audit. Output: a markdown table or doc with rows for each operation, columns for "exposed via UDS / exposed via MCP / arg type identical / result type identical / notes." Posted as a comment on this bead, OR added to `docs/` as `daemon-transport-audit-2026-05-10.md`.

Specifically:
1. Enumerate ops in `daemon/socket.rs`'s dispatch
2. Enumerate ops in `daemon/mcp.rs`'s tools/list
3. Diff
4. For any divergence: classify as (a) intentional, (b) bug, (c) future work / undecided
5. Note any ops that have different SHAPES across transports (e.g. UDS takes raw bytes, MCP takes JSON — but they're nominally the same op)

## Acceptance

- A bead comment (or `docs/` file) lists the audit table
- Any (b) bug-class divergences are filed as separate beads (high priority — silent transport drift is a correctness issue)
- A handoff note for T2.2 (the convergence decision bead) summarizing the inventory

## Why this is T2.1

You can't pick a convergence strategy without the inventory. This is the data-gathering pass.

title: Deprecate --mcp-allow-public after wire auth lands
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T1.4 of decade `daemon-trust-and-transport`, thread `mcp-auth-hardening`. Depends on `b885d1` (the implement-auth bead — once real auth is enforced, the deliberate-intent flag is redundant).

## Work

Once `b885d1` ships real wire auth, `--mcp-allow-public` (from `b7dd03`) becomes belt-and-suspenders. Two paths:

### Option A: Remove the flag entirely
- Delete `--mcp-allow-public` from clap, cmd_daemon, mcp.rs
- Bump LLO MAJOR (or document as a breaking CLI change in CHANGELOG)
- Pro: clean
- Con: anyone scripting `leyline daemon --mcp-allow-public` breaks

### Option B: Keep as a no-op + deprecation warning
- Flag still parses but just emits "DEPRECATED: --mcp-allow-public has no effect; auth is now enforced via LEYLINE_MCP_TOKEN. Remove this flag."
- Schedule removal for a later release (3 versions out)
- Pro: gentle
- Con: maintenance burden; deprecated surface lives forever in practice

Recommend **Option B** for one minor release cycle, then **Option A** in the next major. Standard deprecation cadence.

## Acceptance

- `--mcp-allow-public` either removed or made a deprecation no-op (per chosen option)
- CHANGELOG entry noting the change
- Test that the deprecation warning fires (if Option B)
- `image.Dockerfile` CMD no longer passes `--mcp-allow-public` (since it's redundant); test with `task image:smoke`

## Why this is T1.4

Closes the loop: T1.1 added the flag, T1.2 wrote the auth ADR, T1.3 implemented the auth, T1.4 retires the flag. Thread is done when this bead closes.

title: Bump GHA action SHA pins to Node.js 24-supporting versions
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Fast-follow to PR #3 (`ae0cf9f`).

GitHub flagged a deprecation warning during the PR #3 CI run on `Check + Clippy + Test (task ci)`:

> Node.js 20 actions are deprecated. The following actions are running on Node.js 20 and may not work as expected: `actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5`, `arduino/setup-task@b91d5d2c96a56797b48ac1e0e89220bf64044611`. Actions will be forced to run with Node.js 24 by default starting June 2nd, 2026. Node.js 20 will be removed from the runner on September 16th, 2026.
> https://github.blog/changelog/2025-09-19-deprecation-of-node-20-on-github-actions-runners/

## Action inventory (across both workflows)

```
.github/workflows/ci.yml:21               actions/checkout@34e114876b0b... (v4.3.1, Node 20) ⚠️
.github/workflows/ci.yml:23               dtolnay/rust-toolchain@3c5f7ea2...
.github/workflows/ci.yml:35               arduino/setup-task@b91d5d2c... (v2.0.0, Node 20) ⚠️
.github/workflows/leyline-schema-go.yml:36  actions/checkout@34e114876b0b... (v4.3.1, Node 20) ⚠️
.github/workflows/leyline-schema-go.yml:39  actions/setup-go@0aaccfd1...
```

## Work

For each `uses:` line, find the latest tag whose action.yml declares `runs.using: node24` (or `composite` / `docker`, which are unaffected). Repin the SHA to that tag's commit SHA. Keep a `# tag = vX.Y.Z` comment next to each pin so future readers know what was resolved.

Practical: `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/releases/latest` to find the latest tag, then `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/contents/action.yml?ref=<tag>` to confirm `runs.using`. Or check release notes for "Node.js 24" mentions.

Specifically known to need a bump (per the deprecation warning):
- `actions/checkout` — needs ≥ v4.4.0 / v5 (Node 24)
- `arduino/setup-task` — check for newer release; if upstream hasn't shipped Node 24 yet, file an upstream issue and add a TODO comment

Probably also worth re-checking:
- `dtolnay/rust-toolchain` — appears to be a composite/script action, may not be Node-based at all
- `actions/setup-go` — confirm latest is Node 24

## Acceptance

- All `uses:` SHAs in both workflows point to Node 24-supporting tags (or are composite/docker)
- A re-run of either workflow shows no Node 20 deprecation warning in the job log
- Each pin has a `# tag = vX.Y.Z` comment for human readability

## Why this is a separate bead

Mechanical maintenance, not gated on any release. PR #3 was already long; splitting leaves a clean audit trail: `[ley-line-open-XXXXXX] ci: bump action pins to Node 24` is one focused commit.

comments.text:
Closing — landed in PR #5 (`21f8f60`). 2 of 3 actions bumped to Node 24:

- `actions/checkout` → v6.0.2 (`de0fac2e…`)
- `actions/setup-go` → v6.4.0 (`4a360112…`)
- `arduino/setup-task` → unchanged at v2.0.0; upstream [#1359](https://github.com/arduino/setup-task/issues/1359) tracks Node 24 (not yet shipped). TODO + link inlined in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` so future-us spots it. Node 20 has 4 months runway (force-default 2026-06-02, removed 2026-09-16).
- `dtolnay/rust-toolchain` is a composite action; Node deprecation N/A.

Re-opening if/when arduino ships Node 24 is mechanical (one SHA bump in ci.yml).

(PR #4 was the original; closed by misclick instead of merged. Same commit re-opened as #5 from local reflog.)

title: Adopt cloister's wasm32 FFI exports (lsign_alloc / lsign_free)
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-10 from cloister-bd8c41 audit. cloister's `rs/crates/sign/src/ffi.rs` adds wasm32 linear-memory exports that llo's `rs/ll-open/sign/src/ffi.rs` doesn't have.

## What's there in cloister

```rust
#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
pub extern "C" fn lsign_alloc(size: usize) -> *mut u8 { ... }

#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
pub unsafe extern "C" fn lsign_free(ptr: *mut u8, size: usize) { ... }
```

Required for any wasm32 consumer (workerd, browsers, WASI hosts) to pass byte buffers across the FFI boundary. Without these, wasm32 linear memory is opaque to JS — callers can't allocate input buffers, can't free them, can't read output bytes.

## Why this belongs upstream

`leyline-sign` already advertises itself as targeting wasm32 (Cargo.toml has wasm32-unknown-unknown bindings). The native cdylib FFI works without these (the host runtime owns memory); only wasm32 needs them. ll-open's omission is an oversight rather than a design choice — any wasm32 consumer hits the same need.

## Acceptance

- `lsign_alloc` + `lsign_free` exported from `rs/ll-open/sign/src/ffi.rs`
- Match cloister's safety doc-comments (paired alloc/free, double-free is UB)
- No new dependencies (uses `Vec::with_capacity` + `mem::forget` / `Vec::from_raw_parts` — std-only)
- cloister can drop the local fork once this lands

## Refs
- cloister-bd5241 — wasm32 ABI design
- cloister-bd8c41 — audit identifying the upstream gap
- ll-open-4a9e5a — sibling adoption bead for cert_chain

comments.text:
## Update from cloister-side audit (2026-06-24)

`cloister-59c60e` ran a per-file audit of the vendored `leyline-sign` and confirmed: nothing has silently drifted; the `lsign_alloc` / `lsign_free` additions are still the same shape, fully documented inline, and ready to upstream. The cloister-side ADR ratifying the convergence principle landed today as [`cloister/docs/adr/0035-cloister-llo-boundary.md`](../../../../cloister/docs/adr/0035-cloister-llo-boundary.md) — "bridge crates in cloister; leyline-* names in LLO."

Full convergence checklist + the canonical patch content (lifted from cloister's vendored copy) is staged at [`docs/problems/leyline-sign-wasm32-convergence.md`](../problems/leyline-sign-wasm32-convergence.md) in this repo — pulls together this bead + `ley-line-open-4b267d` (signingTime) + the lib.rs module doc as a single coordinated PR series, with the cloister-side post-merge work scoped.

**Gating recommendation (re-confirmed):** these exports are safe to land unconditionally — native builds get them too, harmless (just two extra cdylib symbols). No Cargo feature needed. The wasm32-portability concern is exclusively about signingTime (the sibling bead).

**Test coverage exists on the cloister side:** `cloister/src/wire/signet-verify.ts` (cloister-bd5241) is the TS-side consumer that exercises the alloc/free roundtrip — useful reference when writing the LLO-side integration test.

title: Adopt cloister's cert_chain.rs verifier for Interlace ephemeral certs
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-10 from cloister-bd8c41 audit. cloister forked `leyline-sign` into `rs/crates/sign/` and added a cert-chain verifier that's not present upstream in ll-open 0.2.0.

## What's there in cloister

`rs/crates/sign/src/cert_chain.rs` (~440 lines, AGPL-3-or-later):
- `verify_cert_chain(cert_der, master_pubkey) -> Result<CertClaims, ChainError>` — verifies an ephemeral Signet cert is signed by a pinned master, extracts validity window + ephemeral SPKI + Interlace claims
- Interlace OID extension parsing at `1.3.6.1.4.1.99999.1.{4=epoch, 5=peer_fp, 6=scope}`
- Rejects critical unknown X.509 extensions per RFC 5280 §4.2 (cloister-c71977)
- `claims_to_json()` — compact JSON encoder for the FFI surface (no serde_json dep)
- `pub mod tests_helpers` — `mint_test_cert`, `mint_test_cert_with_extra_ext`; used by `examples/gen-fixture.rs` to produce deterministic test fixtures

24 native Rust tests, all passing under cloister's CI. Files-touched in cloister:
- `rs/crates/sign/src/cert_chain.rs` (new)
- `rs/crates/sign/src/lib.rs` (pub mod cert_chain)
- `rs/crates/sign/src/ffi.rs` (+ `leyline_verify_cert_chain` export)
- `rs/crates/sign/examples/gen-fixture.rs` (deterministic test fixture minter)
- `Cargo.toml` — depends only on what ll-open's leyline-sign already depends on (x509-cert, ed25519-dalek, der, const-oid, thiserror) + adds base64 as dev-dep for fixture binary

## Why this belongs upstream

cloister's copy is forked from llo's `rs/ll-open/sign/`. Both copies are AGPL-3; cloister kept its NOTICE pointing at llo as the public upstream. The cert-chain verifier is generic-purpose — it's not cloister-specific. Any Signet-aware client (notme, signet's Go side, future Rust consumers) benefits.

Adopting upstream means:
- One canonical implementation, one audit surface
- cloister can re-depend on ll-open's crate via path/git dep instead of carrying a local fork
- Future bug fixes / hardening flow through a single place

## Acceptance

- `rs/ll-open/sign/src/cert_chain.rs` lands with the same 24 tests
- `rs/ll-open/sign/src/ffi.rs` gains the `leyline_verify_cert_chain` export
- `rs/ll-open/sign/examples/gen-fixture.rs` lands (deterministic seed; outputs stable bytes)
- cloister's NOTICE updated to remove "cert_chain.rs as cloister-only delta"

## Refs

title: T1.6: effective-rank invariant test (mode-collapse regression pin)
description:
Bead 1.6 of T1. Mode-collapse pin against the failure mode the math-friend flagged on the L-shape baseline.

## Why

The L-shape baseline shows 60% compression with "preserved relationships," but the manifesto JSON reports `lshape_selectivity = -0.0017` and the per-relationship cosine table shows DISSIMILAR going 0.19 → 0.96 — that's mode collapse, not preservation. Any encoder can score "high cosine" if it collapses everything to the same point. We need to pin against that failure mode.

## Test

- Encode N=500 diverse STS-B sentences via V0 encoder
- Stack into matrix `M ∈ ℝ^{500 × 10000}`
- Compute effective rank: `effective_rank(M) = exp(H(σ_normalized))` where H is the Shannon entropy of the singular value distribution
- Assert `effective_rank ≥ 2048` (≥ 20% of D)

This is independent of τ_dense and τ_sym — it pins that the encoded space is *not collapsed*. A degenerate encoder that maps everything to the same HV would have effective rank ≈ 1 and pass cosine-based tests trivially while teaching nothing.

## Deliverables

- `rs/ll-open/hdc-translate/tests/effective_rank.rs` — single test, ~30 LoC

## Acceptance

`cargo test --test effective_rank` green. Asserts `effective_rank ≥ 2048` on the actual STS-B fixture. If V0 fails this test, the encoder is degenerate and the experiment is invalid before τ_dense/τ_sym are even meaningful.

title: T3: V1 — bigram binding extension (conditional on T2 pass)
description:
Thread under decade ley-line-open-a660ab. **Hard gated on T2 passing both τ_dense and τ_sym.** If V0 falsifies, T3 does not run.

V0 explicitly forfeits sentence-level word order (Dog bites man ≡ Man bites dog at sentence level). V1 reclaims local order by layering shift-invariant bigram binding at the sentence level:

```
sentence level:   HV(sentence) = Σ_i ( HV(word_i) ⊗ HV(word_{i+1}) )
```

## Beads

- T3.1: add bigram binding layer at sentence level (extend encoder.rs)
- T3.2: re-run STS-B with bigram extension; verify τ_dense still passes (risk: bigram halves overlap, may dip below threshold)
- T3.3: add new symbolic claim — word-bigram-partner recovery; commit threshold ex-ante before measurement
- T3.4: decide V2 (parser-aware role-filler binding) or stop

## Why P2

Lower priority than V0 because:
- V0 must pass first (gating)
- V1's incremental value is order-sensitivity, useful but not the load-bearing claim
- The decade's headline result is V0's existence; V1 is refinement

title: T8.10: cross-runtime fixture suite (F8.6.4 from ADR-0014)
description:
Bead 8.10 of T8 under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac. Channel: final.

**Soft dependency:** T8.7 (qualifier in BindingRecord). Fixtures must lock against the *final* schema shape — adding qualifier after fixtures land would force regen.

**Why.** ADR-0014 falsifiable claim F8.6.4: "Encode-in-Rust, decode-in-Go MUST round-trip canonical bytes equal." Today this is unverified. The capnp ecosystem doesn't ship cross-runtime fixtures (the RTFM dossier flagged this — capnp's own runtimes test internally). We'd be ahead of community practice; appropriate for a substrate.

**Concrete deliverables:**

`rs/ll-core/schema-capnp/tests/fixtures/` — new dir with one `.bin` per schema, each accompanied by a `.expected.json`:

```
tests/fixtures/
├── binding-record-minimal.bin       (canonical-encoded BindingRecord, all defaults)
├── binding-record-minimal.expected.json
├── binding-record-realistic.bin     (BindingRecord with all fields populated, including qualifier post-T8.7)
├── binding-record-realistic.expected.json
├── ast-node-minimal.bin
├── ast-node-minimal.expected.json
├── source-file-minimal.bin
├── source-file-minimal.expected.json
└── head-minimal.bin
└── head-minimal.expected.json
```

`.bin` files: bit-exact canonical-encoded capnp messages. Generated once, committed as gold.
`.expected.json`: human-readable decoded form for assertions.

**LLO Rust test** (`rs/ll-core/schema-capnp/tests/cross_runtime_fixtures.rs`):
- Read each `.bin`, decode via Rust capnp runtime, assert fields match `.expected.json`.
- Builder-side: build typed message → `set_root_canonical` → write → assert byte-equal to `.bin`. Fails if Rust's canonical encoder ever drifts.

**Mache Go test** (paired bead in mache repo, gated on this one):
- Read each `.bin`, decode via Go capnp runtime, assert fields match `.expected.json`.
- Same builder-side check.

**Falsifiability:** any drift between Rust and Go canonical encoding fails CI on at least one side. Catches:
- A capnpc/capnp version bump that changes encoding mechanics
- A capnpc-go bug that decodes a field at wrong ordinal
- Any future producer regression that breaks canonical-bytes invariant

**Acceptance:**
- `tests/fixtures/*.bin` committed
- `tests/cross_runtime_fixtures.rs` passes via `cargo test -p leyline-schema-capnp`
- Mache CI (paired bead) consumes same fixtures and passes
- Drift in either runtime fails CI loudly

**ADR-0014 notes:** this is the first place we ship something the capnp community itself doesn't have. Document the fixture-gen procedure as part of the test (a `regen` cargo feature so devs don't accidentally re-generate on every build).

title: T8.9: retire SQL _lsp_refs writers from LLO (gated on T8.8)
description:
Bead 8.9 of T8 under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac. Channel: final.

**Why.** The be6136-class bug is structurally preventable only when the SQL `_lsp_refs` write surface is **removed**, not just dual-emitted. Today: producer dual-writes (capnp at `${db}.bindings.capnp` + SQL row at `_lsp_refs`); consumers can pick. That's a transitional state — it's *prevention via opt-in*, not *structural preclusion*. A new mache contributor (or any cross-runtime consumer) writing a query against `_lsp_refs.referrer_node_id` SQL columns tomorrow inherits the original failure mode.

**Hard dependency:** T8.8 (mache: capnp-default, SQL-fallback removed). Cannot retire LLO SQL writers while any consumer reads them as fallback. T8.8 must close first; only then is this safe.

**Files affected:**
- `rs/ll-open/lsp/src/project.rs::project_references` — remove SQL `INSERT INTO _lsp_refs` arm; keep capnp BindingRecord write.
- `rs/ll-open/lsp/src/project.rs::create_lsp_schema` — remove `_lsp_refs` table DDL OR leave it for legacy `.db` files (schema is additive — leaving the empty table is safe; deciding on retention is part of the bead).
- `rs/ll-open/lsp/src/project.rs::lookup_referrer_node_id` — was sister support for SQL writes; it now feeds capnp's `refSiteNodeId`. Keep, repurpose, or split clearly.
- Tests: `project_references_dual_writes_capnp_binding_log` etc. — assertions against `_lsp_refs` need to switch to capnp-only assertions.
- `rs/ll-core/schema/src/lib.rs` — if `_lsp_refs` DDL lives here, decide retention.

**Scope checklist:**
- [ ] T8.8 closed (mache no longer fallback-reads SQL `_lsp_refs`)
- [ ] No other consumer (cloister, future workerd, control-room) reads `_lsp_refs`
- [ ] Producer emits BindingRecord at every site that previously wrote `_lsp_refs`
- [ ] Existing migrations / `migrate_lsp_schema` paths keep legacy DBs readable but new writes are capnp-only

**Acceptance:**
- `project_references` no longer touches `_lsp_refs`
- All cli-lib tests green; integration tests verify capnp consumers see what SQL consumers used to
- task ci green
- Σ root (Head.rootHash) advances ONLY when capnp content changes — there's no SQL-side "dual-write that affects the chain via segment-bytes" anymore. Pin a regression test.

title: T8.6: ADR-0014 — capnp as producer/consumer protocol, schema evolution rules
description:
Bead 8.6 of T8 under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac. Channel: analysis.

**Deliverable:** `docs/adr/0014-capnp-as-protocol.md`.

**Sections to cover:**

1. **Context.** SQL-as-protocol (be6136 root cause). LLO/mache schema coupling. The L2/L3 synthesis (capnp = data plane, SQL = projection).

2. **Decision.** All cross-process / cross-repo / cross-runtime data shapes go through capnp schemas in `rs/ll-core/schema-capnp/schemas/*.capnp`. SQL tables are *local* projections; their column names are not part of the contract.

3. **Schema evolution rules.**
   - New fields: append at next `@N` ordinal, default-valued. Forward-compatible.
   - Renames: never. Add new field, deprecate old via comment.
   - Type changes: never. Add new field with new type.
   - Removed fields: leave hole; ordinal stays reserved.
   - Versioning: `@0xCAPABILITY_ID` per file; bumped only for incompatible major redesigns.

4. **Schema review process.** Schema changes land in `schema-capnp` first; consumers migrate after. Producer dual-writes during transition window. Each schema change documented in a comment block citing the bead that introduced it.

5. **Tooling.** `task schema:check` runs `capnp eval` to catch ordinal collisions / forward-incompat changes pre-commit.

6. **What stays SQL.** Local query indexes (mache canonical views, LLO `_ast`). These are projections, not contracts.

7. **What stays Cap'n Proto RPC.** Cross-host RPC (capability-mediated; covered separately by T8 or future thread). T8 itself is just data shapes; RPC adds capabilities + promise pipelining on top.

**Acceptance:**
- ADR-0014 merged
- `docs/adr/0014-capnp-as-protocol.md` linked from `docs/decades/2026-merkle-cas-substrate.md` §1.5 (arena↔Σ mapping)
- Schema-review checklist added to `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` (or equivalent)

Currently dangling — Copilot reviewer flagged it on the mache vendored copy (https://github.com/agentic-research/mache/pull/353).

mache vendors the schema verbatim (struct bodies + ordinals byte-identical), so the dangling pointer is only fixable upstream here. Mache picks up the fix on next schema sync.

comments.text:
Shipped at 1c476fd. Branch in sync with origin/main.

**Three load-bearing commitments grounded in published precedent:**

1. **Reading A — canonical encoding at the producer; Σ root over canonical bytes.** The math-friend's analysis was rigorous but rested on a factually wrong premise (capnp's encoding spec does specify a canonical form whose truncation rule is engineered for byte-stability across additive schema changes). Reversed the recommendation. Migrated 4 producer call sites to `set_root_canonical`; `hash_segment_files` canonicalizes-on-read defensively. New regression test `segment_hash_is_canonical_byte_stable` pins the invariant. Backed by IPLD/DAG-CBOR + ATproto/DRISL precedent.

2. **Append-only-additive evolution; the schema is the version manifest.** Workerd (114 ordinals / 5 years) and Sandstorm both evolve this way; neither bakes a `schemaVersion` field into the wire. ADR-0014 follows.

3. **Pin the toolchain triplet + ship cross-runtime fixtures.** Mache PR-1's Copilot review caught `capnpc-go @latest`; we tighten the same on the LLO side. F8.6.4 (cross-runtime fixture suite) is the strongest CI invariant once shipped.

title: T8.5: Σ integration — capnp segments hashed → drive Σ root advance
description:
Bead 8.5 of T8 under decade ley-line-open-9d30ac. Channel: final.

**Where T8 meets Σ.** T8.2/T8.3 emit capnp event-log files. T8.5 makes those files content-addressed segments that drive Σ root advance (T2.1's `Controller.current_root`).

**Concrete deliverables:**
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs` (snapshot path, ~line 540): after each successful parse + projection, hash the just-emitted capnp event-log segment(s) — BLAKE3 over the file bytes. Pass that hash through Σ's CAS root advance.
- New `EventSegment` type in leyline-core: `{ hash: [u8; 32], parent_hash: [u8; 32], gen: u64 }` — one CAS-advanced root per segment.
- `Controller::set_current_root(hash)` (T2.1 already exists) called at segment-finalize.

**Σ Merkle structure:** the Σ root commits to a Merkle tree of capnp segments — each segment's hash is a leaf; root advance = new tree root. Replay from a root reproduces the full event history.

**Out of scope:**
- ll/CSS distribution of segments (separate bead, lives under T3 GC / T4 diff threads)
- Replay/projection rebuild from segments (separate bead under T8.6 schema-evolution)

**Acceptance:**
- After parse+enrich, `Controller::current_root` is non-zero and equals BLAKE3(capnp event-log segment)
- Two parses → two distinct roots; `parent_hash` field of segment 2 == root of segment 1
- Test: write parse → snapshot → read root → independently hash the segment file → assert equal

Depends on T2.1 (current_root field, already shipped at 280f28b).

**Hashing:** `hash_segment_files` in `cmd_parse.rs`. Canonical order `source.capnp || ast.capnp || bindings.capnp` (lexicographic by suffix), BLAKE3-32. Σ §3.4's "BLAKE3 locked" claim now has concrete enforcement.

title: 5f7100-7b: LSP server pool — one long-lived client per language (60f75d follow-up)
description:
Follow-up to ley-line-open-60f75d. Per-file timeout landed at cbca333. 5f7100-7a moves enrichment to background. THIS bead tracks #3: pool LSP servers.

**Current behavior**: `enrich_files` (lsp_pass.rs) calls `LspClient::start` and `client.shutdown` per call. Each lazy-enrich invocation spawns gopls/rust-analyzer/etc., does work, kills it. Cold-start cost (gopls index, rust-analyzer build graph) repeats every time.

**Desired behavior**: `DaemonContext` holds `lsp_pool: Arc<LspServerPool>` keyed by language. First call for a language spawns + caches; subsequent calls reuse. Pool drains servers idle for >N minutes to reclaim resources. On shutdown, all servers cleanly stopped.

**Files**: rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/lsp_pass.rs, rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mod.rs, new file rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/lsp_pool.rs

**Acceptance**:
- First gopls call: full spawn cost (~hundreds of ms for index)
- Second gopls call (same language): reuses pool, ~ms latency
- pin test: 50 hovers across 3 languages → exactly 3 LSP server spawns observed
- pool gracefully shuts down on daemon teardown

**Related**: 5f7100-7a is its complement; together they make the enrichment cost amortize correctly.

comments.text:
[2026-05-08] Shipped at commit 800fb5d.

`LspEnrichmentPass` now holds `Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex<HashMap<String, LspClient>>>`. First call per language spawns + caches; subsequent calls reuse. Servers stay alive for daemon lifetime — typical sessions use 1-3 languages, total RSS bounded. OS reclaims on daemon exit.

Refactor: `enrich_files` split into `enrich_files_pooled` (locks pool, find-or-spawn) + `enrich_files_with_client` (runs the per-file timeout-wrapped batch with an existing client; no spawn/shutdown). `cmd_daemon.rs` updated to `LspEnrichmentPass::new()`.

**Pin tests**: `lsp_pass_pool_starts_empty`, `lsp_pass_pool_is_per_instance` (Arc::ptr_eq for per-instance isolation). Live "second-call-skips-spawn" measurement requires real LSP in CI; structural pins ensure the pool is plumbed through correctly.

Tests: 153/153 lib + 33/33 integration. Clippy clean.

Closing.

title: 5f7100-7a: lazy LSP enrichment runs on UDS thread (60f75d follow-up)
description:
Follow-up to ley-line-open-60f75d. Per-file timeout landed at cbca333 (#1 of 3 acceptance criteria). This bead tracks #2: move the enrichment off the UDS connection thread.

**Current behavior**: `op_lsp_hover` calls `with_lazy_enrich_retry` which (post-606e64 fix) drops the live_db lock then calls `try_enrich_file` synchronously on the same thread that's handling the UDS request. With LSP enrichment taking 1-5 seconds (per-file timeout), one slow `lsp_hover` head-of-line-blocks every other UDS op.

**Desired behavior**: `try_enrich_file` queues the work to a background tokio task and returns immediately with `enriched=false`. The hover op returns the current (empty) data; the background task populates `_lsp` for the next request. Optionally: the hover can wait via `Notify` for a bounded time (~100ms) to let the enrichment complete in the common-case fast path.

**Files**: rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs, rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mod.rs (DaemonContext gains a background-enrichment-tx channel or JoinSet), rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs (spawn the consumer task)

**Acceptance**:
- op_lsp_hover returns within UDS round-trip latency (~ms) regardless of LSP enrichment state
- background enrichment populates _lsp asynchronously
- pin test: 50 concurrent hovers on different files, p99 < 100ms (timeout-bounded by the wait, not by the enrichment)

**Related**: 5f7100-7b (LSP server pool) is the natural sibling — both reduce per-call cost.

comments.text:
[2026-05-08] Shipped at commit f6890a6.

**Change**: `try_enrich_file` now queues via `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` and returns within microseconds. Pre-fix held `live_db.lock()` for 1-5s synchronously on the UDS thread.

**Behavior**:
- `op_lsp_hover` returns immediately with current (empty) data + `enriched: true` (retry hint).
- Background task populates `_lsp*` on the blocking pool.
- Next request after enrichment lands sees fresh data.

**Plumbing**: `handle_base_op` and the LSP ops (`op_lsp_hover`, `op_lsp_defs`, `op_lsp_refs`, `op_lsp_position`) + `with_lazy_enrich_retry` now take `&Arc<DaemonContext>`. Other ops stay `&DaemonContext` via auto-deref. `socket.rs::dispatch` and `mcp.rs::handle_tools_call` updated. Test fixture `setup()` returns `Arc<DaemonContext>`.

**Pin test**: `try_enrich_file_returns_synchronously_within_budget` — asserts <100ms return time. Pre-fix would have blocked for 1-5s.

**60f75d siblings**: timeout (cbca333) ✓; this bead 7a ✓; pool 7b still open. 60f75d's umbrella stays open until 7b lands.

Tests: 151/151 lib + 33/33 integration pass. Clippy clean.

Closing.
