Close (runnable): find-smells gate green in LLO CI over the Rust workspace; baseline committed; lint:blake3 retained.

title: toml 0.8 → 1.1 in leyline-ts (pyproject feature, major bump)
description:
From dep audit 2026-06-17. `toml` is at 0.8 in `leyline-ts`'s optional pyproject feature; latest is 1.1. Major bump — Deserialize API likely shifted.

## Acceptance gates

- `cargo test -p leyline-ts --features pyproject --manifest-path rs/Cargo.toml` passes (whatever feature gates the toml dep).
- pyproject.toml parsing fixtures round-trip.
- `task ci` green.

## Scope

- `rs/ll-open/ts/Cargo.toml` — version bump (verify the feature gating)
- `rs/ll-open/ts/src/*.rs` — Deserialize call-site changes if toml 1.0 changed serde integration

## Risk

Low-medium. toml is well-maintained; major bumps usually surface clear breakage. Feature is optional so impact is contained.

title: Release version coupling: daemon binary, schema-client, capnp wire — define the matrix
description:
## Surfaced by

PR #388 audit on mache (2026-05-18). LLO ships three independently-tagged versioned artifacts that consumers must keep in sync:

1. **Daemon binary** (`leyline`) — released via `agentic-research/ley-line-open` GitHub Releases. Latest: `v0.4.3` (as of 2026-05-18 evening).
2. **Schema-client Go module** (`clients/go/leyline-schema`) — released via tags `clients/go/leyline-schema/vX.Y.Z`. Latest: `v0.4.2`.
3. **Capnp schema** — embedded in the schema-client module; bumps when the wire format changes.

The audit confirmed:
> "In parallel work (commit b1ba5ec, 3 hours ago, on a different branch), mache's `go.mod` was updated to `leyline-schema v0.4.2`. This is not yet on the base branch, so the PR is not *immediately* stale. However, v0.4.2 exists on the LLO releases page and includes additional sheaf GC features (per release notes)."

So in the same week:
- Daemon binary is at v0.4.3
- Schema-client Go module is at v0.4.2
- Some mache branches pin v0.4.1, others v0.4.2

This isn't necessarily broken — semver allows compatible versions to talk — but the discipline is **undocumented**. There's no single source of truth saying "daemon v0.4.3 is compatible with schema-client v0.4.x for x >= ?".

## What to define

A `compatibility.md` in `rs/ll-open/` (or top-level) that, for each released version of each artifact, specifies:

| Daemon binary | Compatible schema-client | Wire-format major | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| v0.4.0 | v0.3.x, v0.4.0 | 1 | sheaf ops introduced |
| v0.4.1 | v0.4.0+ | 1 | δ⁰ inputs |
| v0.4.2 | v0.4.0+ | 1 | (had the sheaf.invalidate event-push bug per `ley-line-open-5caa59`) |
| v0.4.3 | v0.4.0+ | 1 | fixed event push; pre-publishes typed payloads (after `ley-line-open-503971` lands) |

The wire-format major bumps when the JSON event/op envelope shape changes incompatibly (e.g., the `data: {...}` nesting we hit in `ley-line-open-5caa59`). Inside the same major, additions are non-breaking; removals or renames bump.

## Implementation choices

1. **Static doc**: hand-maintained `compatibility.md`. Cheap, drifts under pressure.
2. **CI-enforced**: each LLO release tags a `compatibility.json` artifact alongside the binary. Consumers can `curl` it at install/build time. Slightly more infra.
3. **Daemon-reported**: the handshake op (`ley-line-open-{handshake-bead-id}`) returns this matrix at runtime. Combines with (2) — the daemon serves the same matrix it ships with.

Recommendation: start with (1), graduate to (3) once the handshake bead lands.

## Mache-side coupling

`mache-9051f0` (closed) pins the daemon auto-download URL to a hardcoded `leylineBinaryVersion` const. That const mirrors `go.mod`'s schema-client pin. Today the discipline is "they should match, more or less"; with a compatibility matrix this becomes "the daemon-binary version must satisfy the schema-client's `compat_min` requirement," which is a hard contract.

## Test plan

`compatibility.md` itself doesn't need tests, but:

- A CI job that parses `compatibility.json` (if option 2/3) and verifies the matrix is self-consistent (no version listed as compatible with a non-existent counterpart).
- The handshake op tests (`ley-line-open-{handshake-bead-id}`) automatically validate the runtime version against the documented matrix.

## References

- `ley-line-open-503971` — typed payloads; defines the JSON wire shape that the matrix's "wire-format major" refers to
- `ley-line-open-a4e860` — future capnp migration; this matrix gets a new column for "capnp protocol version" once that lands
- `mache-9051f0` — the pin-to-version-or-rot symptom this bead addresses
- `mache-8kif` — mache-side startup version check, consumes the matrix
- This bead's sibling — handshake op — surfaces the matrix at runtime

## Why P3 (not P2)

The drift hasn't bitten anyone yet (compatibility happens to hold across recent versions). The handshake bead (sibling) and typed-payloads bead (`ley-line-open-503971`) address the immediate symptoms; this is the longer-term discipline that prevents the next class of drift.

title: witchcraft: document NDCG_BASELINE = 0.40 provenance (corpus, labels, what justifies bumping)
description:
## Context

`rs/ll-open/text-search/tests/acceptance_gates.rs` sets `NDCG_BASELINE = 0.40` for the `ndcg_at_10_meets_baseline` test. Witchcraft's own README reports 0.31–0.33 on NFCorpus (their shipped corpus). The 0.40 here is presumably against the local hand-labeled `tests/fixtures/eval_corpus.json` where the bar should be easier — but without a staged measurement, it's unclear whether 0.40 is meaningful or trivially-satisfied.

## What to do

1. Add a doc comment on the const naming:
   - The corpus it measures against (size, label process)
   - The retrieval task shape (binary relevance, k=10)
   - What range a *baseline* retriever (e.g. BM25 alone) lands at — so 0.40 reads as "X above floor" not just a magic number
   - Conditions under which bumping is justified (corpus growth, retrieval method change)

2. Stage one real run with the T5 assets bundle:
   - Confirm the gate is non-trivial (engine clears 0.40 with meaningful margin)
   - If it clears at 0.85, the bar is too low — tighten
   - If it scrapes 0.42, the bar is meaningful — leave it

## Surfaced

PR #31 review, 2026-05-18.

title: Optional polish: typed Request enum for socket.rs dispatch (A-3.3 follow-up)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11. P3 chore. Splits out from the original `b69606` (A-3) scope after the load-bearing work landed.

## Status

A-3 shipped the typed RESPONSE side (`wire.rs` + 17 of 20 ops swapped from `json!({...})` to `serde_json::to_string(&TypedResponse {...})`). Schema is genuinely load-bearing for responses; future handler drift is caught at compile time or by the cross-runtime gate.

The REQUEST side of `socket.rs` still uses `serde_json::Value` + `req.get("op")` extraction. That's NOT a drift risk (handlers use typed `required_str_field()` extractors that return `Result<&str>`), but it's a missed type-safety win.

## What this bead does

Add a `BaseRequest` enum to `wire.rs`:

```rust
#[derive(Deserialize)]
#[serde(tag = "op", rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum BaseRequest {
    Status,
    Flush,
    Snapshot,
    Load { db: String /* base64 */ },
    Reparse { source: Option<String>, lang: Option<String>, files: Option<Vec<String>> },
    Enrich { pass: String, files: Option<Vec<String>> },
    Query { sql: String, limit: Option<usize> },
    ListChildren { id: Option<String> },
    ListRoots,
    ReadContent { id: String },
    FindCallers { token: String },
    FindDefs { token: String },
    FindCallees { id: String },
    GetNode { id: String },
    GetRefsMap,
    GetDefsMap,
    GetSchema,
    GetDbPath,
    // LSP / vec ops still pass through as Value (not in schema yet)
}
```

`socket.rs` dispatch becomes:

```rust
match serde_json::from_str::<BaseRequest>(&line) {
    Ok(typed) => handle_typed_base_op(ctx, typed),  // returns typed response
    Err(_) => {
        // Fall back to untyped Value for events/extensions/LSP/vec.
        let raw: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_str(&line)?;
        dispatch_untyped(ctx, raw).await
    }
}
```

## Why P3 / not necessary now

- The structural drift-prevention contract already holds via the response side
- Adding the Request enum is ~50-100 lines of mechanical work that mostly removes hand-extracted fields
- LSP/vec/query stay untyped under both schemes
- Doesn't affect mache or any external consumer

## Acceptance

- `BaseRequest` enum in `wire.rs` with one variant per typed op
- `socket.rs` tries typed parse first, falls back to untyped Value
- `handle_base_op` either rewrites to take `BaseRequest` directly OR stays as-is with a thin typed wrapper
- All existing tests pass
- New tests: each variant deserializes from its canonical fixture request shape

## Files

- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/wire.rs`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/socket.rs`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/ops.rs` (if `handle_base_op` signature changes)
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/integration.rs`

comments.text: Closing — this bead was created mid-A-3 as me hedging on whether the typed Request enum was "necessary" or "polish." User correctly pushed back: it was explicitly in A-3's scope. Folding it back into the A-3 PR rather than splitting it out.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: Wire sccache as RUSTC_WRAPPER in Taskfile (caches release/incremental builds)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11. P3 chore, mechanical.

## Problem

`task release` / `task install` recompile the workspace from scratch ~50s each invocation on M-series. Most of that is rustc work on dependencies that are byte-identical between runs — sccache handles exactly this case.

Observed today: user installed `sccache 0.15.0` via brew but it's not yet wired in. Without `RUSTC_WRAPPER=sccache` set, the cache is dormant.

## Work

In `Taskfile.yml`:

```yaml
env:
  PKG_CONFIG_PATH: '{{.FUSE_PKG_CONFIG_PATH}}'
  RUSTC_WRAPPER:
    sh: command -v sccache 2>/dev/null || true
```

The `sh:` block keeps it conditional — if sccache isn't installed, the env stays empty and cargo proceeds normally. No build-pipeline regression for contributors who don't have sccache.

Add a one-line note to `README.md` "Build" section: `brew install sccache` (macOS) recommended for caching across rebuilds.

## Acceptance

- `task release` on a warm sccache cache builds in <15s (vs current ~50s)
- `task ci` similarly benefits
- `sccache --show-stats` shows hits
- CI (GHA) gets a follow-up to install + configure sccache with the GitHub Actions cache backend, separate work

## Why P3

Cosmetic perf win, doesn't block anything. But low-effort and high-frequency-benefit for local dev.

title: Add get_db_path daemon op (capnp readthrough fast-path for serve_find_smells)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11. T2 of decade `daemon-typed-wire`, thread `missing-ops`. LOW priority — degrades safely without it.

## Context

mache's `serve_lsp.go` and `serve_find_smells.go` type-assert a `dbPathProvider` to enable an OPTIONAL fast-path: read directly from LLO's `.db` file (or its capnp segment-file siblings) for bulk queries that otherwise round-trip through the daemon's `query` op.

`SQLiteGraph` exposes this. `udsGraph` doesn't, so these handlers fall back to the slower SQL query path. Functionally fine — no user-visible regression. Just unused-but-available perf.

## Work

`get_db_path` op that returns the daemon's `ctrl_path.parent()` (where the .db lives) and the path to the `${db}.bindings.capnp` segment files. Read-only.

### Schema

```capnp
struct GetDbPathRequest {}

struct GetDbPathResponse {
  ok           @0 :Bool;
  dbPath       @1 :Text;
  bindingsPath @2 :Text;  # absolute path to ${db}.bindings.capnp
  astPath      @3 :Text;
  sourcePath   @4 :Text;
}
```

### Handler

Trivial — read paths from `ctx.ctrl_path` + sibling capnp segment-file paths.

### Acceptance

- `get_db_path` in `base_op_names`
- Returns paths that the test daemon can read from
- A-1 drift gate fixture entry
- Integration test
- Regen Go bindings; task ci green

## Why LOW priority

Functionally optional — `serve_lsp.go` / `serve_find_smells.go` work fine without it (SQL query fallback). Filing for completeness but no rush to implement vs the higher-priority gaps (`find_callees`, refs/defs map).

title: Future ADR: daemon RPC wire framing — when and why to evolve past JSON
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Standalone research bead — no thread, no commit pressure.

## Context

ADR-0014 (LLO, accepted 2026-05-08) line 105:
> *"Live RPC / `interface` schemas. ADR-0014 covers data schemas (`struct`-only). Daemon RPC, distributed segment fetch, and `Persistent`/SturdyRef capabilities live in a separate file ID set under a future ADR. Scope boundary is explicit."*

That future ADR is this bead's eventual output.

The daemon UDS wire is line-JSON today, deliberately, for shell debuggability and symmetry with the MCP HTTP JSON-RPC transport. The typed contract is `daemon.capnp` (locked by Bead `a373ba` once it lands). The wire ENCODING question is genuinely deferred — not because the answer is unclear, but because no consumer has forced the question yet.

## Forcing functions (any one triggers reopening this bead)

1. **Cloister starts using `udsForward` against LLO directly.** Per cloister ADR-0005 (2026-04-29, amended through 2026-05-10), cloister's `udsForward` backend wire expects capnp ToolCall/ToolResult. If cloister wants LLO as a `udsForward` backend, LLO needs a second protocol on UDS (or a multiplexed handshake). This is the most likely trigger.
2. **Performance ceiling on JSON encoding hits production.** Profile data showing JSON serialize/parse is a meaningful fraction of daemon CPU/latency.
3. **A non-mache, non-cloister consumer wants the type safety hard.** E.g. a TypeScript/Swift client where capnp's generated types are easier to consume than JSON+hand-validation.
4. **A specific debugging episode shows JSON parsing was the bug.** E.g. mishandled Unicode, schema-vs-wire mismatch the typed lockdown didn't catch.

## What the ADR should cover (when written)

- The three transport surfaces in play: LLO UDS, LLO MCP HTTP, cloister-internal capnp ToolCall
- Whether to converge them (and how), or formalize them as distinct peers
- Wire-format choice (capnp framing? canonical encoding? envelope shape?)
- Migration story for existing consumers (mache, cloister, ad-hoc tools)
- Hand-debuggability replacement (CLI probe tool? capnp-to-JSON gateway?) if line-JSON debuggability is dropped
- Versioning model (file IDs vs op-id table vs handshake negotiation)

## Why this is filed now (rather than waiting)

Keeping the question visible so it doesn't drift. Future-us / future-agent will find this bead when searching for "daemon RPC" or "wire" or "capnp framing." The forcing functions are real possibilities, not hypotheticals.

## Not in scope of this bead

- Implementation (that's the ADR's follow-up bead, filed after the ADR is written)
- Migrating the wire today (`a373ba` locks the typed contract without changing encoding)
- Adding the cloister-shaped second protocol on UDS (that's Path 2 from the plan — a separate concern that would get its own bead/thread when needed)

title: Research: vec sidecar trajectory — first-class projection or opt-in feature?
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Standalone research bead.

## Question

The `vec` cargo feature gates a sidecar vector index, embedder, and an enrichment pass (`crate::daemon::embed::EmbeddingPass`). Today it's:
- Off by default in `leyline-cli` (the leyline-cli crate's `[features]` declares `default = ["lsp"]`, no `vec`)
- Plumbed extensively in `cmd_daemon.rs` (per the diagnostics output: lines 31, 178, 184, 192, 210, 228, 230, 232, 238 — at least 9 cfg-gate sites)
- Not exposed in the production image (the v0.2.1 distroless build is `--features lsp` default, no vec)

That suggests vec is *experimental* — wired but not relied on. Need a clear answer for the next design phase:

- Stays opt-in feature gate forever (current shape)?
- Becomes first-class — embedding is a projection alongside SQL tables, always-on (or default-on)?
- Gets retired — code lives in a branch / experiments folder, removed from mainline?

## Inputs to gather

1. Which crates / call sites depend on `vec` being available? Is it self-contained or does it leak into supposedly-feature-free code?
2. What's the embedder model? (`ZeroEmbedder` default in `cmd_daemon.rs:177-180` suggests no real model is wired today — placeholder.) What was the original intent?
3. Does the substrate (capnp schemas) reserve room for embedding bytes? (Look at `binding.capnp`, `common.capnp` — do any structs have an embedding field, even feature-gated?)
4. What would "first-class embedding" mean for Σ root advance? Are embeddings part of the canonical bytes that get BLAKE3'd, or sidecar?
5. Performance: is the embedding pass cheap enough to be always-on? Or does it gate on having a GPU / model loaded?

## Possible outcomes

- **Keep opt-in**: document trajectory clearly in `cmd_daemon.rs` module doc + `vec` feature description in Cargo.toml. No production change.
- **Promote to default**: write ADR for "embedding as projection," wire ZeroEmbedder as the production default, leave real embedder behind a runtime config (so production deploys ship empty embeddings until a model is loaded).
- **Retire**: delete the feature gate, move code to a `crumb-experiments/` branch or out-of-tree crate, leave behind a one-line comment pointing at the moved code.
- **Split**: separate "embedding storage" (substrate-level) from "embedding computation" (engine-level). Storage stays in substrate; computation stays opt-in.

## Acceptance

- A bead comment with the audit + recommendation
- If a clear direction emerges, a follow-up bead (or thread) filed for the work

## Why no thread / no commit pressure

This is a strategy question, not a defect. Filing it captures the question so it doesn't drift; doesn't demand resolution this cycle.

title: Research: leyline-hdc — production-path or research-shelf?
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Standalone research bead — no thread, no commit pressure.

## Question

`leyline-hdc` (in `rs/ll-open/hdc/`) is described in README as "Hyperdimensional computing — `HvCell` sheaf-stalks-over-hypervectors (Heyting algebra; bit-level Hamming agreement)." That's a strong, distinctive claim. But it's listed alongside ordinary projection-engine crates with no obvious caller in the daemon hot path.

Need to know: **is HDC pulling weight in production code paths today, or is it research-shelf code that's a workspace member because it's convenient?**

## Audit work

1. `rg "leyline_hdc::" --type rust` — find all call sites
2. Categorize:
   - Daemon hot path (called per-request)
   - Build-time / startup (called once at init)
   - Test-only
   - Demo / example
   - Dead code (no callers — workspace member only because Cargo workspace)
3. Read each call site briefly — what does it do with the HDC types?
4. Read `leyline-hdc/src/` overview — how big is the surface? Is there a "production API" vs "exploratory API" distinction?
5. Compare the README claim ("sheaf-stalks-over-hypervectors with Heyting algebra") to what's actually wired — is the math claim load-bearing or aspirational?

## Possible outcomes

- **Pulling weight**: leave alone, document caller patterns better in `leyline-hdc/README.md`
- **Sometimes used / opt-in**: feature-gate it (`--features hdc`), document as opt-in, set off-by-default
- **Research-shelf**: move to a separate `experiments/` crate or out of the workspace entirely; README "Tier 2" table should reflect status accurately
- **Mixed**: split into a thin "always-on" core + an "experimental" extended module

## Acceptance

- A bead comment summarizing the audit findings
- A recommendation (one of the four above) with one-paragraph rationale
- If recommendation is "feature-gate" or "split", a follow-up bead filed for the actual work

## Why no thread

Standalone — answer informs whether more work is needed, but the audit itself is self-contained. If it produces follow-up beads, those can form a thread later.

title: Decompose cmd_daemon.rs (1300+ LOC) into lifecycle-phase modules
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T1.3 of decade `daemon-shape-debt`, thread `daemon-config-refactor`. Depends on `badb31` (the module-registry refactor — easier to split a file once features are registry-driven).

## Problem

`rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs` is 1300+ lines. Its module docstring already enumerates the lifecycle in 10 phases:

> 1. Arena setup (create/open arena + controller)
> 2. If `--source`: parse source dir → load .db into arena
> 3. Event router created
> 4. `ext.on_init(emitter)` — extension initializes state
> 5. UDS socket spawned (base ops + extension ops)
> 6. If `--mount`: mount via NFS/FUSE (omit for headless mode)
> 7. `ext.on_post_mount(ctrl_path, router)` — extension spawns background tasks
> 8. If mache on PATH: spawn `mache serve --control <ctrl>` as child
> 9. Wait for shutdown (Ctrl+C or timeout)
> 10. Cleanup (kill mache child, remove socket file)

The docstring already does the decomposition mentally. The code doesn't.

## Work

Split into a `daemon/lifecycle/` module with one file per phase:

```
daemon/lifecycle/
  mod.rs              -- pub fn run(config: DaemonConfig) -> Result<()>
                          orchestrates the phases
  arena_setup.rs      -- phase 1
  source_parse.rs     -- phase 2
  router.rs           -- phase 3
  extension_init.rs   -- phase 4
  socket_spawn.rs     -- phase 5
  mount.rs            -- phase 6 (cfg-feature mount)
  post_mount.rs       -- phase 7
  mache_child.rs      -- phase 8
  shutdown.rs         -- phase 9 + 10
```

Each phase is a free function or small struct with `fn run(state: &mut LifecycleState) -> Result<()>`. `cmd_daemon.rs` becomes the thin orchestrator (~100 LOC).

This is the BIGGEST of the three thread beads. Land after T1.1 + T1.2 reduce the surface area of what each phase needs from the surrounding state.

## Acceptance

- `cmd_daemon.rs` is < 200 LOC
- Each lifecycle phase has its own file with a focused docstring
- `daemon/lifecycle/mod.rs::run` reads top-to-bottom as the lifecycle docstring describes
- `task ci` green; `task image:smoke` green
- All feature combos (default / +vec / +mount / +lsp / --all-features) build clean

## Why this is T1.3 (not T1.1)

Decomposing 1300 LOC ahead of the struct refactor (T1.1) and registry refactor (T1.2) means re-doing it twice. Stack the cheap shape changes first, then split.

title: Refactor: extract per-feature daemon initialization into a registry/trait pattern
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. T1.2 of decade `daemon-shape-debt`, thread `daemon-config-refactor`. Depends on `ba8294` (DaemonConfig struct — easier to plumb a registry once the args are a struct).

## Problem

`cmd_daemon.rs::run_daemon` body is full of `#[cfg(feature = "vec")]` and `#[cfg(feature = "mount")]` blocks, each doing its own initialization (resolve embedder → register vec → build queue; or backend → mount setup → spawn fs handlers). Adding a new feature means inserting another `#[cfg]` block at the right point in the lifecycle.

Lines from rust-analyzer's diagnostics on the file:
- `13:1` `mount` cfg block
- `31:1` `vec` cfg block
- `178:5`, `184:5`, `192:5`, `210:13`, `228:9`, `230:9`, `232:9`, `238:5` — more `vec` blocks scattered through
- `263:5` `mount` block
- `1326:9`, `1331:9`, `1344:13`, `1346:13`, `1348:13` — more in the lower half

That's a lot of feature-conditional code interleaved with core lifecycle. Two readability problems compound: which of these are core lifecycle vs feature-gated additions, and which features depend on which other lifecycle phases.

## Work

Pattern: each feature contributes a `DaemonModule` (or similar trait) that hooks into named lifecycle phases. Core lifecycle becomes `for module in registered_modules { module.on_phase(...) }`.

Concrete shape (subject to revision when the work starts):

```rust
trait DaemonModule {
    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    fn on_init(&self, ctx: &mut InitContext);  // called before context built
    fn on_post_mount(&self, ctx: &PostMountContext);  // after mount stage
    // etc.
}
```

`#[cfg(feature = "vec")]` then registers a `VecModule`. `#[cfg(feature = "mount")]` registers a `MountModule`. Core `run_daemon` body is feature-gate-free; module registration block is the only `#[cfg]` zone.

This is a bigger refactor than T1.1 (config struct). Take it after the config struct lands, when the context plumbing is cleaner.

## Acceptance

- `run_daemon` body has zero `#[cfg(feature = ...)]` blocks (registration block at the top is the exception)
- New feature is added by writing a `DaemonModule` impl + adding it to the registry — no edits to `run_daemon` itself
- `task ci` green with all feature combinations: default, `--no-default-features`, `--features vec`, `--features mount`, `--features lsp`, `--all-features`
- Existing tests still pass

## Why this is T1.2

The config struct (T1.1) was a shape change. This is a structure change. They compound — config-as-struct makes module-context plumbing pleasant; doing this before T1.1 would mean threading a giant context through a giant function.

title: Document krust pinning policy or fallback recipe
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Risk-mitigation follow-up to ley-line-open-2b255c.

`task image` depends on [`krust`](https://github.com/imjasonh/krust) v0.0.1 (alpha). During the v0.2.1 image migration we hit two real bugs in krust 0.0.1 in a single session:
1. **Path doubling** — when `project_dir` is a relative path, krust passes `--target-dir <relative>` to cargo *after* `cd`'ing, so cargo resolves it against the new cwd → nested `rs/ll-open/cli/rs/ll-open/cli/target/krust/...` (workaround: pass an absolute path).
2. **Crate-name ≠ bin-name** — krust assumes `cargo metadata`'s package name is the binary name, ignoring `[[bin]] name = ...` (workaround: `-- --bin <bin-name>`).

Both have working workarounds in `Taskfile.yml`'s `image` task. But:
- We're coupled to a barely-released tool with active alpha churn
- A regression in krust could silently break our `task image` flow
- New contributors hitting `krust install --force` could get a different version than what we tested against

## Two paths (pick one)

### Option A: Pin to known-good
- Add a comment + `cargo install krust --git https://github.com/imjasonh/krust --rev <sha>` to README install instructions
- Or vendor krust as a workspace member (heavier)
- Pro: deterministic, survives upstream regressions
- Con: misses upstream fixes for the two bugs above; manual bumps

### Option B: Document fallback
- Add a `task image:no-krust` Taskfile target that does:
  - `cd rs && cargo zigbuild --release -p leyline-cli --target aarch64-unknown-linux-musl --target-dir target/krust`
  - `docker build -f image.Dockerfile -t ley-line-open:0.2.1 .`
- README mentions both: "active path uses krust; if krust regresses, `task image:no-krust` is the documented escape hatch"
- Pro: zero coupling to upstream stability; we own the recipe
- Con: two paths to maintain; each release cycle re-verifies both

Recommend **Option B** — krust is a thin wrapper over `cargo zigbuild` + image assembly; replicating it ourselves is ~10 lines and removes a dependency from our hot path. Option A makes us a permanent downstream adopter for tooling we don't need long-term.

## Acceptance
- README `## Building the image` section names the policy
- `task image:no-krust` exists and produces an identical-shape image (same tag, same CMD, smoke passes against it)
- A post-mortem comment on this bead lists which bugs in krust 0.0.1 we worked around (so future-us knows what to test against if/when they're fixed upstream)

title: Delete apko.yaml + melange.yaml — abandoned image-build path
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Cleanup follow-up to ley-line-open-2b255c.

Both `apko.yaml` and `melange.yaml` were retained "for reference" in PR #3 after the krust pivot, with a README disclaimer + a `.gitignore` note pointing readers at the post-mortem (`ley-line-open-2b255c`). That was the right call at the time — abandoning the work felt premature mid-PR.

Now that the krust path is settled and bead `2b255c` is closed, retaining these files is net-negative:
- They look authoritative to anyone grepping for "apko" or "melange" without reading the disclaimer
- They drift silently — nothing tests them, so any future correction (e.g. apko schema changes) goes unnoticed
- The current README disclaimer + closed bead `2b255c` post-mortem are a better audit trail than the rotting code itself

## Work
1. `git rm apko.yaml melange.yaml`
2. Update `.gitignore` — remove the `!apko.yaml` / `!melange.yaml` allow-rules (keep `!image.Dockerfile`); update the comment to point at bead `2b255c` only
3. Update root `README.md` — replace the "apko.yaml / melange.yaml are retained for reference but abandoned" sentence with a one-line "see bead `ley-line-open-2b255c` for the apko/melange post-mortem"
4. `task ci` green
5. PR with `[ley-line-open-XXXXXX] chore: delete abandoned apko/melange image configs` commit message

## Acceptance
- Both files gone from `git ls-files`
- README still mentions the rationale (one line) but doesn't keep stale config alive
- `task image:smoke` still passes (the krust path doesn't depend on either file)

title: Complete OCI image build verification (run task image + runtime smoke)
description:
Filed 2026-05-10. Spinoff from ley-line-open-17739e: the apko/melange/Taskfile scaffolding is in place but `task image` was killed mid-run when the agent session ended. No `.tar` artifact produced; no runtime verification performed.

## What needs to happen

1. `cd /Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/ley-line-open` and confirm the in-tree apko.yaml + melange.yaml + Taskfile.yml `image` target are present (they were uncommitted last we checked — may need to be committed first).
2. Run `task image`. Expect ~5-10 minutes for the cold Rust + capnpc compile inside the melange build container.
3. `docker load < ley-line-open.tar`.
4. `docker run --rm -p 8384:8384 ley-line-open:0.1.0` in the background.
5. `curl http://localhost:8384/mcp` with a minimal MCP initialize payload — verify a valid JSON-RPC response.
6. Kill the container, verify clean exit.
7. Update README's "Building the image" section with any caveats discovered (e.g. memory required for the build, arch quirks).

## Acceptance

- `ley-line-open:0.1.0` image is reproducibly built from a clean checkout.
- HTTP MCP on :8384 confirmed working from inside the container.
- Image size noted in README for ops planning.

## Cross-repo context

- ly-line-open-17739e: the parent bead that authored the scaffolding.
- Once verified, this image becomes the candidate for inclusion in cloister's cluster.capnp as a sibling bundle (separate follow-up bead in cloister).
- For mache↔llo IPC (mache bead — "Define + implement mache↔llo IPC wire"), llo will need a UDS server counterpart. This bead is JUST the HTTP-on-:8384 image; the UDS server work is downstream.

## Out of scope

- Adding llo to cluster.capnp (cloister-side follow-up).
- Adding a UDS server mode for mache↔llo IPC (separate llo bead).

comments.text:
**Pivoting from apko/melange to krust.**

Re-attempted the apko/melange build per the existing Taskfile and hit two blockers that make this path unworkable for local dev on Apple Silicon:

1. **Workspace tmpdir leak.** `melange build` defaults `--source-dir` to `dirname(melange.yaml)` = repo root. It then recursively copies the entire repo (including `rs/target/`, `.git`, prior `./packages/`, and any prior `melange-workspace-*` that landed in CWD) into a new `/var/folders/.../T/melange-workspace-XXXX` tmpdir before any pipeline step runs. With `--rm` not passed, those workspaces persist across runs. We accumulated ~80 GB across 10 leaked dirs from interrupted sessions (largest: 30 GB). Each run also stalls during the copy on Docker Desktop's virtiofs (millions of small files = pathological).

2. **apko x86_64 step will fail.** `apko.yaml` declares `archs: [x86_64, aarch64]`, but the Taskfile only invokes `melange build --arch aarch64`. apko has no graceful-skip for missing arches; it errors out with `unable to select packages: ley-line-open (no such package)` for x86_64. Per chainguard-dev/apko#415.

Both are fixable in-place (add `--rm`, `--cache-dir`, `--workspace-dir`, explicit `--source-dir <small dir>` to melange; add `--arch aarch64` override to apko CLI). But for a Rust binary → distroless image, **krust is the better fit:**

- No docker daemon involved in the build → no virtiofs bottleneck, no workspace leaks
- Cargo cache survives across runs (`target/krust/`), so subsequent builds are seconds
- Native cross-compile from M-Mac for both amd64+arm64 via `cargo-zigbuild` (no qemu)
- Static musl binary + distroless `cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest` base = ~few MB image
- Multi-arch manifest list "for free"

Tooling already on system (`zig`, `cargo-zigbuild`, `krust 0.0.1` all installed via brew/cargo).

What apko was giving us that needs alternate handling under krust:
- `leyline` user/uid/gid/`runAs`: chainguard/static ships nonroot uid 65532; or use `cgr.dev/chainguard/static:nonroot`
- `/data` writable mount, `HOME=/data`, `RUST_LOG=info` env, `cmd: daemon --mcp-port 8384`: runtime concerns, set in the deployment surface (cloister) or a thin entrypoint wrapper
- OCI annotations (title, description, license, source): krust supports via `[package.metadata.krust]` in Cargo.toml

**Updating bead scope: build + verify LLO OCI image via krust** (was: via apko/melange). Will keep the existing `apko.yaml`/`melange.yaml`/`Taskfile` `image` task on disk for now (not deleting yet) but treat them as broken/deferred until/unless we revisit for CI publishing.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
**Closing — image build verified end-to-end via krust + docker.**

`task image:smoke` runs clean:
```
== tools/list ==
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{"tools":[{"description":"Daemon lifecycle status: ...
smoke passed
```

## What landed

- **`Taskfile.yml` `image` task rewritten** — `krust build rs/ll-open/cli --no-push` produces a static musl aarch64 binary; `docker build -f image.Dockerfile` COPYs it onto `cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest`. Total image: ~20 MB. Build time: <2s on warm cache.
- **`image:smoke` task added** — full e2e: build → start daemon → wait for HTTP listen → POST `tools/list` → cleanup. Used as the verification gate.
- **`image.Dockerfile` added** — single COPY, sets `HOME=/tmp` and `RUST_LOG=info`, ENTRYPOINT `/usr/bin/leyline`, CMD `daemon --mcp-port 8384`.
- **Daemon code fix:** `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mcp.rs:272` hardcoded `127.0.0.1` bind, which made the MCP listener unreachable from the host even with `docker -p host:8384` (loopback-only). Added `--mcp-bind <IpAddr>` flag (default `127.0.0.1` for back-compat); container deployments pass `--mcp-bind 0.0.0.0`. Threaded through `cmd_daemon::run_daemon` → `daemon::mcp::spawn`. Tests/integration call site updated.
- **README image section rewritten** for the krust path; `.gitignore` allows `image.Dockerfile`.
- **`apko.yaml` / `melange.yaml` retained** but no longer referenced by `task image`. Documented in README + .gitignore comment why they were abandoned on Apple Silicon.

## Verification

```
$ task image:smoke
task: [image] krust build ... → leyline static musl aarch64
task: [image] docker build → ley-line-open:0.2.0 (~20 MB)
task: [image:smoke] docker run -d ... daemon --mcp-port 8384 --mcp-bind 0.0.0.0
task: [image:smoke] curl tools/list → {jsonrpc, result.tools[]} OK
task: [image:smoke] docker stop llo-smoke
smoke passed
```

Container log confirms bind: `MCP HTTP server listening on http://0.0.0.0:8384/mcp`.

## Files touched (for the record)

- `Taskfile.yml` — `image` and `image:smoke` tasks
- `image.Dockerfile` — new
- `rs/ll-open/cli/src/main.rs` — `--mcp-bind` clap arg
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/cmd_daemon.rs` — `mcp_bind` parameter through both entry points
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/src/daemon/mcp.rs` — `spawn` takes `bind: Option<IpAddr>`
- `rs/ll-open/cli-lib/tests/integration.rs:778` — call site updated
- `README.md` — image section rewritten
- `.gitignore` — allow `image.Dockerfile`, comment updated

`cargo check -p leyline-cli-lib -p leyline-cli` passes. No new diagnostics from the patch.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: Ship apko + melange in-repo for ley-line-open OCI image
description:
Filed 2026-05-10 from cloister's deployment-readiness audit.

## What needs to happen

cloister's deployment topology (`~/remotes/art/cloister/cluster.capnp`) doesn't currently declare a ley-line-open bundle as a sibling container — today llo is reached via `LLO_MCP_URL` (external HTTP service, not a bundle in the cluster). But the cluster substrate (ADR-0009 + cluster-be0607) could absorb llo as a sibling bundle the same way it absorbs mache + rosary — IF llo has an in-repo apko/melange that produces a runnable OCI image.

ley-line-open currently has NO apko/melange. This bead is the work to add one.

## Architectural context (from the cloister side)

- llo is a **userspace FS optional projection** + memory arena. No FUSE kernel deps required. The image should be a plain subprocess container.
- The cloister-side wire to llo today is HTTP at `LLO_MCP_URL`. Eventually that should migrate to capnp-over-UDS to match mache + rosary (per ADR-0005 amendment — "cloister ↔ sibling bundles is plain capnp IPC inside the trust boundary"). This bead is the IMAGE-build piece; the wire migration is separate work.

## Scope

1. **Add `apko.yaml` + `melange.yaml` at the repo root**. Reference patterns:
   - `~/remotes/art/notme/packages/{apko-notme.yaml,melange-*.yaml}` (worker-bundle style)
   - `~/remotes/art/rosary/{apko.yaml,melange.yaml}` (Rust subprocess style — closer match for llo)
   - `~/remotes/art/cloister/{apko.yaml,melange.yaml}` (workerd Worker bundle)

2. **Add `Taskfile.yml` `task image` target**: `melange build` → `apko build` → produces tagged OCI artifact. Pin a version (e.g. `ley-line-open:0.1.0`).

3. **Verify the image runs**: `docker run ley-line-open:0.1.0 daemon --mcp-port 8384` (or whatever the current invocation is) should boot llo's MCP server on the published port.

4. **Document the build invocation** in llo's README.

## Eventual sibling-bundle integration (NOT in this bead)

Once the image ships, a follow-up bead in cloister will add llo as a `cluster.capnp` bundle entry alongside mache + rosary. The wire would switch from HTTP (today) to capnp-over-UDS (the cluster-internal contract).

## Acceptance

- `task image` builds on clean checkout.
- `ley-line-open:0.1.0` (or chosen tag) is loadable via `docker load`.
- The image runs llo's existing MCP server entrypoint correctly.
- README has a "Building the image" section.

## Cross-repo context

- **cloister** at `~/remotes/art/cloister/` — the future consumer (cluster topology + ADR-0005 wire contract).
- **mache** (`~/remotes/art/mache`) — sibling Go/Rust subprocess pattern; reference its apko once mache-172a50 lands.

## Out of scope

- WASM/wasi packaging (could be a follow-up given llo doesn't need FUSE).
- Migrating the wire to capnp-over-UDS (cloister-side follow-up bead).
- Adding llo to cluster.capnp (cloister-side follow-up bead, blocked on this).

title: Decide: drop signingTime from CMS SignedAttributes (cloister's wasm32-portability fix)
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-10 from cloister-bd8c41 audit. cloister's `cms.rs` REMOVES `signingTime` from CMS signed attributes; ll-open 0.2.0's still includes it.

## Context

RFC 5652 §5.3 lists `signingTime` as a useful-but-unauthenticated attribute. It's spec-legal to omit. cloister's rationale (per `cms.rs` doc comment):

> The wasm32 emit needs a per-host time source. `js_sys::Date` in V8, `wasi:clocks` in WASI, `SystemTime::now()` native — and a fixed-time placeholder shipping across hosts would silently collapse the temporal-binding property of any verifier that trusts signingTime.

So cloister's `sign_data` doesn't write a signingTime at all; temporal context comes from `cert.not_before` / `cert.not_after` (signed by the master at mint time) and the attestation row's server-timestamped `created_at`.

## The decision for ll-open

A. **Mirror cloister and drop signingTime upstream.** Any verifier that relied on it was relying on an unauthenticated attribute anyway; the spec doesn't promise it.

B. **Keep signingTime in ll-open** and let cloister diverge. ll-open's native builds have a real time source so the attribute is meaningful for them. cloister keeps its local fork for the wasm32-only behavior.

C. **Make signingTime conditional on a `#[cfg]` flag.** wasm32 builds omit it; native builds emit it. Compiles to the right behavior per-target without forking.

Recommend C — cleanest, no upstream/downstream divergence, no behavior surprises for native consumers.

## Acceptance

- Decision recorded in an llo doc / ADR / commit message
- If A or C: cloister's `cms.rs` doc comment + NOTICE updated to reflect no longer being a delta
- If B: cloister's NOTICE keeps the delta entry; bd8c41 closes as "won't merge upstream"

## Refs
- cloister cms.rs (current source of the delta + rationale)
- RFC 5652 §5.3 (signingTime is optional + unauthenticated)
- ll-open's existing leyline-sign cms.rs (the upstream baseline)
- cloister-bd8c41 — audit identifying the delta

comments.text:
## Update from cloister-side audit (2026-06-24) — recommend Cargo-feature gating

Per-file audit of cloister's vendored `cms.rs` re-confirms: signingTime omission is still wasm32-required for cloister, still inline-documented, still spec-legal per RFC 5652 §5.3.

**Recommendation: land the change behind a default-off Cargo feature** rather than unconditionally dropping signingTime. That way existing LLO consumers (signet, ley-line, others) see no behavior change; cloister enables it explicitly in its wasm32 build. Feature name suggestion: `no-signing-time` (short, says exactly what it does).

Patch shape (gated):

```rust
#[cfg(not(feature = "no-signing-time"))]
let signing_time_attr = build_attribute(&oid::ID_SIGNING_TIME, &encode_utc_time_now());

#[cfg(not(feature = "no-signing-time"))]
let mut attrs = vec![content_type_attr, message_digest_attr, signing_time_attr];
#[cfg(feature = "no-signing-time")]
let mut attrs = vec![content_type_attr, message_digest_attr];
```

`Cargo.toml`:
```toml
[features]
default = []
no-signing-time = []
```

Cloister already has a regression test pinning the omission (`signed_attributes_omits_signing_time`) that would carry over once cloister-side consumes upstream — happy to PR it along with the gating change.

Full coordinated-PR plan + cloister-side post-merge work at [`docs/problems/leyline-sign-wasm32-convergence.md`](../problems/leyline-sign-wasm32-convergence.md) (staged in this repo today).

Cloister ADR ratifying the convergence principle: [`cloister/docs/adr/0035-cloister-llo-boundary.md`](../../../../cloister/docs/adr/0035-cloister-llo-boundary.md) — "bridge crates in cloister; leyline-* names in LLO."

P3 still feels right (P2 for the FFI exports because those unblock cloister build path; P3 here because signingTime is a behavioral change that wants the feature gate). With the Cargo-feature approach, this becomes a low-risk additive change.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 5f7100-14: mache-driven hot-spot review — weekly bead-auto-file from code-intelligence
description:
Parent: 5f7100. The "Mache / LLO powered" idea: dogfood the code-intelligence we just shipped to drive its own hardening backlog.

Goal: a recurring task (cron, GH Action, or rsry pipeline) that runs against the LLO codebase and auto-files chore beads when it finds:

1. **Functions over 100 LOC** (god-function red flag) — use mache `find_definition` on every function symbol, sum the line range, list top-N.
2. **Files in the same community with no shared callers** (unused churn) — `mache get_communities` for clusters; cross-reference with `find_callers` count.
3. **Newly drifted contracts** — diff `find_callers(symbol)` count week-over-week; a sudden spike in callers of an internal helper means the helper has accidentally become public API.
4. **Files with coverage drop > X% week-over-week** — combine coverage report with mache file list.
5. **Untested public functions** — `mache search "pub fn"` ∩ coverage report's "Missed Functions" set.
6. **TODO/FIXME debt aging** — grep + git blame; auto-file a chore bead when a TODO is older than N days.

Output: each finding becomes a draft bead via `rsry_bead_create` with the right file scope. Human reviews, deduplicates, dispatches.

Why this matters: the rule "shipped ≠ hardened" is only enforceable if hardening is automated. Hand-driven hardening reviews (like the one that produced 5f7100-1 through -12) are valuable but not sustainable as a per-commit thing. mache+LLO are the right tools to make it cheap.

Acceptance:
- Script at `scripts/mache-hardening-scan.sh` (or rust binary) that produces a JSON report
- Taskfile target `task harden:scan` runs it locally; outputs current findings, no auto-file
- Optional `task harden:scan -- --commit` actually files draft beads via rsry_bead_create
- CI runs it on every push to main, posts findings as a PR comment; nothing auto-files in CI

This bead is half tool-build, half meta-design. Ship the tool first, then iterate on what to scan for.

title: perf: multi-pass pipeline — definitions, then calls, then enrichment
description:
Currently parse does everything per-file: walk AST → emit nodes + refs + defs + imports all in one pass. This works but prevents cross-file optimizations.

codebase-memory-mcp uses a multi-pass pipeline:
1. pass_definitions — collect all function/type/class definitions
2. pass_calls / pass_usages — resolve call targets against known definitions
3. pass_enrichment — add type info, semantic edges
4. pass_similarity — detect similar code patterns

Each pass is independent and can be parallelized. The definition pass must complete before calls can be resolved (needs the defs index).

Lower priority — the current single-pass works. This is an optimization for when cross-file resolution needs to be more accurate.

title: Benchmark GigaToken at LLO tokenizer ingestion seam
description: Evaluate GigaToken against LLO's current fastembed and Witchcraft/HuggingFace tokenization paths. The published headline is tokenizer-only, native whole-file, heavily parallel throughput, while LLO's meaningful metric is end-to-end embedding or late-interaction ingestion. Benchmark on representative mache/rosary/Claude-chat corpora; separate native direct-file and compatibility modes; require exact token-ID parity for supported model vocabularies; measure wall time, peak RSS, and downstream embedding throughput. Also determine whether a stable Rust library/crate integration exists and document unsupported WordPiece/SentencePiece limitations before proposing dependency changes.

comments.text: Scope clarification: this remains the later tokenizer/fastembed spike. GigaToken is not proposed as a dependency for CDC. The immediately relevant technique-transfer work is tracked separately in ley-line-open-416cd9: parity-preserving affine GearHash SIMD/register lanes, one-time backend dispatch, and a falsifiable end-to-end CDC performance gate.
comments.author: rsry-cli

acceptance_criteria: A reproducible benchmark records exact-parity results plus tokenizer-only and end-to-end ingestion throughput/RSS against current fastembed/Witchcraft paths on representative corpora, ending in an explicit adopt/defer/reject decision.

title: Spike parity-preserving SIMD and affine GearHash CDC scanner
description: Apply GigaToken-style techniques to LLO CDC without adding GigaToken as a dependency or changing chunk semantics. Build an internal scanner backend registry selected once per process (scalar oracle; aarch64 NEON; x86 backend only if it beats existing gearhash). Exploit GearHash's affine recurrence h[i+k] = 2^k*h[i] + sum(2^(k-j)*G[b[i+j]]) mod 2^64 to break the serial dependency into independent SIMD/register lanes while still testing every candidate boundary and returning the earliest exact match. Preserve MIN_CHUNK, MAX_CHUNK, BOUNDARY_MASK, offsets, hashes, and manifest bytes exactly. Keep boundary-policy/FastCDC normalization experiments separate because those intentionally change manifest identity. TDD order: failing backend differential and forced-dispatch tests first, then implementation, then end-to-end performance gate. Baseline observed on Apple M3 Max merged main: scan 1870-1875 MiB/s and full chunk+BLAKE3 1560-1572 MiB/s over five 256 MiB runs.
acceptance_criteria: PASS only if: (1) forced scalar and optimized backends match the gearhash scalar oracle for arbitrary initial hashes, masks, lengths, boundary-edge/adversarial inputs, and at least 10,000 deterministic randomized cases; (2) end-to-end chunk manifests and reconstructed bytes are byte-identical on the CDC oracle corpus; (3) at least 20 interleaved A/B release trials on M3 Max across random, source/text, and repetitive corpora show >=1.15x median full chunk() throughput, bootstrap 95% CI lower bound >1.05x, and no corpus regresses >5%; (4) task ci remains green. Otherwise reject/revert the optimized backend and retain only benchmark evidence.

title: Nothing enforces "every nodes.record writer invalidates the chunk manifest" — 4 sites were missed; make it structural
description:
Follow-up from the adversarial review of `feat/cdc-materialize-on-read` (2026-07-23). The four bugs it found are FIXED with falsifying regression tests; this bead is about the structural gap that let all four through at once.

## What happened

`chunked.rs` documents the invariant: `nodes.record` is authoritative, the chunk manifest is a derived index, and ANY writer of `record` must invalidate it or the chunked read path serves stale bytes. The invariant was enforced by hand at three call sites (`write_content`, `flush_node`, `batch_splice`'s AST branch) and silently missed at four others:

- `truncate` — silent no-op for chunk-backed nodes (verified: returned 5 bytes after truncate)
- `remove_node` — CROSS-GENERATION CONTENT LEAK. Node ids are PATHS and paths get reused, so an orphaned manifest attaches to whatever node is created at that path next. Verified: write "secret-old-bytes" -> remove_node -> create_node(same path) -> read returns "secret-old-bytes" on a brand-new, never-written file.
- `batch_splice` non-AST arm — both the update and delete sub-arms write `nodes` directly; the post-reproject invalidation loop walks `_ast`-derived ids so it structurally cannot see them
- `rename_node` — manifest keyed by old id with no FK, orphans onto the vacated path

All four now fixed + tested (`truncate_invalidates_the_chunk_manifest`, `recreating_a_removed_path_does_not_leak_the_old_content`, `removing_a_directory_invalidates_descendant_manifests`, `batch_splice_plain_node_arm_invalidates`, `rename_does_not_orphan_a_manifest_on_the_vacated_path`) — each verified to FAIL when its fix is removed.

## Why it is still fragile

Correctness depends on a human remembering to add a call at every future write site. The next writer added to `graph.rs` reintroduces the same class of bug, and the failure mode is silent wrong data (or content disclosure), not an error.

## Options to make it structural (pick one, they are alternatives)

1. **Single choke point.** Route every `nodes` mutation through one `write_record(conn, id, Option<&str>)` / `delete_nodes(conn, id)` helper in `graph.rs` that invalidates as part of the same call. Then "forgot to invalidate" becomes "bypassed the helper", which is greppable and lintable.
2. **Push it into SQL.** `content_manifest.node_id` currently has no FK. Add `FOREIGN KEY(node_id) REFERENCES nodes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE` and enable `PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON`. Kills the remove/rename leaks at the storage layer (does NOT cover the update-in-place cases — truncate/record-update still need explicit invalidation). Note: the arena is `sqlite3_deserialize`d and portability of the pragma across the arena lifecycle needs checking.
3. **Freshness token.** Store the `size`+`mtime` the manifest was built from and have `has_chunked_content` compare against the node row, so a stale manifest fails CLOSED (falls back to `record`) instead of serving bytes. Costs one small indexed read per read; covers every writer including ones outside this crate, which options 1 and 2 do not.

Option 3 is the only one that also defends against a writer in another crate (leyline-ts already writes `record` directly), which is why the current design needs `flush_node`/`batch_splice` invalidation at all.

## Coverage gap that hid it

`task mutants` scopes `cargo mutants` to `ll-open/cdc/src/lib.rs` + `ll-open/fs/src/chunked.rs`. `graph.rs` is deliberately out of scope (~1900 lines of pre-existing untested surface would swamp the gate — see the Taskfile comment). So mutants verified `invalidate_chunked_content`'s BODY while nothing verified that `graph.rs` CALLS it at each write site. Consider a narrowly-scoped mutation run over just the write-path functions in `graph.rs` (`cargo mutants --re` can filter by function name) rather than the whole file.

comments.text: Reconciled against merged main e35697e. Option 3 is implemented structurally: content_manifest_meta records source_len/source_mtime transactionally; has_chunked_content joins against live nodes and fails closed; an_unknown_writer_cannot_cause_stale_bytes_to_be_served and equal-length rewrite tests prove external/unaware writers fall back to nodes.record. The code explicitly documents that every graph invalidation call can be removed without correctness failure; mutation gates passed in PR #265 (fs: 65 caught, 4 unviable, 0 missed; CDC: 57 caught, 7 unviable, 9 timeouts, 0 missed). Explicit invalidation remains only for storage hygiene and has its own deletion/cascade pins. Acceptance is satisfied; closing as already shipped rather than duplicating a solved abstraction.
comments.author: rsry-cli

acceptance_criteria: One of options 1-3 implemented, plus a test that fails when a NEW `nodes.record` writer is added without invalidation (e.g. option 3's freshness check makes the four existing regression tests pass even with the explicit invalidate calls removed). Verify with `task test:fs-cdc` and `task mutants:fs`.

title: Add reachability GC and storage accounting for CDC chunks
description: Production CDC activation makes content_chunks long-lived. Current invalidation deletes manifest rows but deliberately retains every historical chunk forever; module docs say a reachability sweep is required before a long-lived writable mount. Add explicit transactional GC over chunks unreachable from content_manifest plus dry-run/storage accounting. Preserve chunks shared by any live manifest and keep GC off the write hot path. The production command should be safe to rerun and report reclaimed rows/bytes.
acceptance_criteria: TDD proves GC removes only unreachable chunks, preserves a shared chunk until its final manifest disappears, leaves every live manifest reconstructible byte-for-byte, is idempotent, and reports deterministic candidate/reclaimed rows and bytes; an interrupted/failed transaction leaves the store usable; dry-run mutates nothing; task test:fs-cdc and task ci pass.

title: Pin CDC ownership contract and downstream consumer smoke test
description: Define the stable consumer surface before mache/rosary adopt CDC. nodes remains the cross-runtime authoritative schema; content_chunks/content_manifest/content_manifest_meta are LLO-owned derived indexes and must not force a leyline-schema bump unless that ownership decision changes. Document activation, fallback, freshness, incremental-write, and GC behavior. Add a downstream-shaped smoke fixture that builds a normal nodes-only arena, activates it through the supported surface, range-reads it, mutates it, and reopens it. Reconcile the stale v0.10.2 changelog sentence that still says incremental writes are unwired.
acceptance_criteria: README and changelog state one unambiguous ownership/versioning contract; a consumer-shaped integration test starts from a nodes-only arena and uses only supported public/CLI surfaces to activate, range-read, incrementally update, serialize/reopen, and GC it; no consumer writes LLO-owned content_* tables directly; an explicit decision records whether leyline-schema changes (expected: no while tables remain derived/private); task ci passes.

title: Release consumer-ready CDC after activation, GC, and parity gates
description: Cut the next patch release only after the production CDC entrypoint, GC, consumer contract/smoke test, and parity-preserving SIMD spike have resolved. The SIMD spike may resolve by rejection if its falsifiable performance gate fails; release correctness must not depend on accepting an optimization. Confirm package versions, nested leyline-schema tagging policy, release assets, and downstream pin instructions for mache/rosary.
acceptance_criteria: All blocking CDC beads are done; task ci, task test:fs-cdc, mutation gates, all-features release build, and consumer smoke pass from the release commit; changelog contains no contradictory unwired claims; schema/tag decision is explicit; GitHub release and required nested tags/assets exist; mache/rosary can pin the released version.

title: Epic: complete CDC as a production-consumable subsystem
description: Goal umbrella for finishing CDC so mache, rosary, and other consumers can use a released stable surface. Already shipped: ley-line-open-87bf00 (primitive/materialize-on-read), ley-line-open-bd8d33 (incremental write wiring), and ley-line-open-0330c7 (freshness makes missed invalidation fail closed; reconciled/closed). Remaining execution order: (1) ley-line-open-f16e53 production activation/backfill, (2) ley-line-open-035363 reachability GC/accounting, (3) ley-line-open-040775 ownership contract + consumer smoke, (4) ley-line-open-416cd9 parity-preserving SIMD/affine spike, whose implementation is rejected if its gate fails, and (5) ley-line-open-048393 patch release/downstream pins. Related but non-blocking: ley-line-open-33e6fe later tokenizer spike and ADR-0029 workspace research. Discoveries about duplicate effort, missing shared abstractions, or generalizable SQL/JSON/code smells must become linked repo-local beads rather than hidden scope expansion.

comments.text: Audit discovery: both create_chunked_content_schema and store_content_chunked have only test callers on merged main, so CDC is validated but not production-activatable. Filed f16e53 as the first blocking implementation. Generalized the class of gap into mache-0715f4 (advisory test-only production API smell) rather than embedding a one-off grep in LLO.
comments.author: rsry-cli

acceptance_criteria: Every listed blocking bead is terminal with its falsifiable gate satisfied (or SIMD explicitly rejected by its predefined gate); a production command activates/backfills a nodes-only arena; long-lived writes have explicit GC; consumer ownership/versioning is documented and integration-tested; a patch release is published and usable by mache/rosary.

title: Activate and backfill CDC manifests through a production entrypoint
description: The CDC schema and store_content_chunked path are validated but currently have only test callers. No production path creates/backfills content manifests, so existing arenas and ordinary writes remain on nodes.record unless a library consumer manually invokes internals. Design and implement an explicit, resumable production activation/backfill surface (CLI and reusable Rust API), with gradual compatibility for arenas lacking CDC tables. TDD first: a real arena fixture must demonstrate pre-activation record reads, activation, chunk-backed range reads, then incremental writes. Do not require mache or rosary to know LLO-owned content_* DDL.
acceptance_criteria: A production command/API activates CDC on a real mache-shaped arena; activation is idempotent and resumable; pre-existing nodes are backfilled without changing authoritative bytes; a 4 KiB read of a large activated node is proven to select only overlapping chunks; subsequent small writes take the incremental path and remain byte-identical to full rechunk; foreign/unactivated arenas retain record fallback; task test:fs-cdc and task ci pass.
